---

# 📍 Locality Guide: Ohio (Pre-Statehood) — c. 1785–1803

---

## Jurisdictional Context & Boundary Changes

**The core challenge:** In 1790, "Ohio" did not yet exist as a legal jurisdiction. The land was governed as part of the **Northwest Territory**, established by the Northwest Ordinance of 1787. Ohio achieved statehood only on **March 1, 1803** — the first state carved from the Northwest Territory.

This means records for your 1790 ancestors were created and filed under **territorial authority**, not Ohio state authority. The research trail splits across three overlapping jurisdictional layers:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Record Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Before 1788 | Northwest Territory (unorganized) | Almost none; frontier settlements only |
| 1788–1790 | **Washington County, NWT** (created 1788; seat: Marietta) | All territorial records in this single county |
| 1790–1803 | Washington Co. **and** **Hamilton County, NWT** (created 1790; seat: Cincinnati) | County split; determine which county held your ancestor |
| 1803 onward | Ohio (state) | Statehood brings county court systems, state archives |

> **Rule of thumb:** For any event in what is now Ohio before 1803, first determine the modern county, then trace that county's lineage back to either Washington County (eastern/central Ohio) or Hamilton County (southwestern Ohio) — because that is where the original record was filed.

### Special Land Districts (Major Boundary Quirk)
Ohio's pre-statehood land was divided into distinct survey systems, each with its own records and repositories — a critical complexity for land research:

- **Virginia Military District** (between the Scioto and Little Miami rivers): Used a metes-and-bounds survey. Land warrants are held at the **Virginia State Library & Archives, Richmond**. Do not expect these at Ohio repositories.
- **Connecticut Western Reserve** (northeastern Ohio): Surveyed on its own township grid. Records are at the **Western Reserve Historical Society, Cleveland**.
- **Congress Lands / Seven Ranges**: Federal rectangular survey; patents at the **Bureau of Land Management General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov).
- **U.S. Military District**: Federal bounty land grants for Revolutionary War veterans; patents at BLM GLO and pension files at NARA.

---

## Record-by-Record Survey

### 1. Land Records ✅ Best source for 1790
**Why:** Land was the primary legal transaction in this period. Even settlers with no other documentation took out a land grant.

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840* (collection ID 1542187) — 234,567 indexed records, 117,283 images. Starts in 1787 and directly covers the territorial period. **Indexed + images.**
- **Digitized volumes:** FamilySearch holds 1 volume of **Northwest Territory Territorial Land Records, 1788–1799** (image group 005112233, 51 images). This volume is **0% name-indexed and not full-text searchable — browse-only**, image by image.
- **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov): Free online portal for federal land patents. Searchable by name. Essential for Congress Lands, U.S. Military District, and Virginia Military District patents issued before statehood.
- **Virginia Military District warrants:** At the Library of Virginia, Richmond — separate from Ohio repositories entirely.
- **Connecticut Western Reserve deeds:** Western Reserve Historical Society, Cleveland (not on FamilySearch).

> **Continuity note:** A land transaction begun under the Northwest Territory may have a patent issued *after* 1803. Search both the territorial-era volumes and the post-statehood Ohio Land Records collection as a single continuous trail.

---

### 2. Tax Records ⚠️ Sparse before 1800
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850* (collection ID 1987542) — 345,890 records. This collection **begins in 1800**, just outside the 1790 target. No equivalent indexed collection exists for the territorial period.
- **Digitized volumes:** FamilySearch holds 1 volume of **Washington County, Ohio Tax Records, 1799–1803** (image group 005112240, 73 images, 12% name-indexed). The majority must be **browsed image by image**. This is the earliest surviving Ohio-area tax record volume found in the survey.
- **Gap:** Tax records for 1790–1799 outside Washington County appear not to be digitized on FamilySearch. Consult the **Ohio History Connection** (Columbus) for any surviving original tax duplicates.

---

### 3. Marriage Records ✅ Available from county formation
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Ohio, County Marriages, 1789–2013* (collection ID 1803785) — 1,523,678 records, 761,839 images. **Starts 1789** — the year Washington County began issuing marriage bonds. This is the primary civil marriage record for the territorial period.
- Marriage records follow county jurisdiction: search **Washington County** for eastern Ohio settlers (1789+) and **Hamilton County** for southwestern Ohio settlers (1790+). For areas not covered by these two early counties, no county marriage record existed until the relevant county was formed.
- No statewide marriage registration existed before 1803.

---

### 4. Church Records ✅ Critical gap-filler for 1790
**Why:** Civil birth and death registration did not begin in Ohio until **1867** (statewide). For 1790, church registers are the *only* source for baptisms, marriages by clergy, and burials.

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950* (collection ID 2246917) — 678,234 records, 339,117 images. **Starts 1787.** **Indexed + images.**
- Key denominations active in the Northwest Territory in 1790: Methodist, Presbyterian, Baptist, and Quaker (Friends) meetings. German Reformed and Lutheran congregations followed slightly later waves of immigration.
- Quaker meeting minutes are especially thorough — they record births, marriages, and deaths for all members.
- **Ohio History Connection** (Columbus) holds microfilm of many early church registers not yet digitized.

---

### 5. Vital Records — Birth & Death ❌ Not available for 1790
- Ohio did not require county-level birth/death registration until **1867** and did not enforce it effectively until the early 1900s.
- For 1790: **no civil birth or death records exist.** Substitute sources are church registers (above), cemetery inscriptions (Find A Grave), and family Bible records.

---

### 6. Census Records ⚠️ Partial for 1790
- The **1790 Federal Census** did not enumerate the Northwest Territory. Ohio-area settlers in 1790 do not appear in any federal census.
- The **1800 Federal Census** is the first to cover what is now Ohio, enumerating it as part of the Northwest Territory. This is your earliest federal census source.
- **No territorial census** is known to survive for 1787–1800. Tax lists (above) serve as the census substitute.

---

### 7. Military Records ✅ Important — many 1790 settlers were veterans
- Many early Ohio settlers were **Revolutionary War veterans** who received bounty land warrants in the U.S. Military District or Virginia Military District.
- **Revolutionary War pension and bounty land files** at NARA (accessible via **Fold3.com**) frequently include family information, ages, and places of origin — a major biographical source.
- **Territorial militia records** (1788–1803) are fragmentary; surviving muster rolls may be at NARA or the Ohio History Connection.

---

### 8. Probate & Court Records ⚠️ Available from 1788 under county courts
- Territorial court records (wills, estate inventories, guardianships) were filed at the county level from Washington County's organization in 1788.
- **Ohio History Connection** (Columbus) is the primary repository for early territorial court and probate records.
- FamilySearch does not surface a dedicated pre-statehood probate collection in this survey. Physical or microfilm access is likely required.

---

### 9. Newspapers ⚠️ Minimal before 1800
- The *Centinel of the North-Western Territory* (Cincinnati, est. 1793) was one of the first newspapers in the region. Not available in the 1790 window.
- **Newspapers.com** and the **Ohio History Connection's Ohio Memory** portal hold later Ohio newspaper holdings; coverage for 1790 is effectively nil.

---

## Repositories Summary

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Ohio Land Records 1787–1840; Ohio Church Records 1787–1950; Ohio County Marriages 1789+; NWT Territorial Land Records (browse-only) | Free online |
| **BLM General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents, Congress Lands, U.S. Military District | Free online |
| **Ohio History Connection**, Columbus | Early court, probate, tax records; church microfilm; territorial-era originals | In-person / partial online via Ohio Memory |
| **NARA** (National Archives) | Revolutionary War pensions, military service records, 1800 census | Fold3.com (subscription); in-person |
| **Library of Virginia**, Richmond | Virginia Military District land warrants | In-person / partial online |
| **Western Reserve Historical Society**, Cleveland | Connecticut Western Reserve land & deed records | In-person |
| **Find A Grave** (findagrave.com) | Cemetery transcriptions (early graves) | Free online |

---

## Key Research Quirks & Cautions

1. **Search Washington County first for eastern Ohio before 1800** — it was the only county for most of the territory until Hamilton County split off in 1790.
2. **The 1790 census does not cover Ohio.** Your first federal census opportunity is 1800. Use tax records and land records as 1790 substitutes.
3. **Civil birth and death records don't exist for this period.** Go directly to church registers and cemetery records.
4. **Land records follow survey district, not Ohio counties.** Virginia Military District warrants are in Richmond, Virginia — not Columbus, Ohio.
5. **FamilySearch's NWT Territorial Land Records (1788–1799) are browse-only** (0% indexed, 51 images). You must page through them manually — no name search available.
6. **Washington County tax records (1799–1803) are 88% browse-only** (12% indexed). Plan for image-by-image browsing.
7. **Post-1803 county records often absorb pre-1803 territorial records.** When a new county was formed, original Washington or Hamilton County records were sometimes physically transferred — always check the successor county's early deed/probate books.

---

## Digitization Level Summary

| Record Type | Coverage Start | FamilySearch Access Level | Physical Backup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Territorial Land Records | 1788 | Browse-only images (0% indexed) | Ohio History Connection; BLM GLO |
| Land Records (Ohio collection) | 1787 | **Indexed + images** | BLM GLO; county courthouses |
| Church Records | 1787 | **Indexed + images** | Ohio History Connection |
| County Marriages | 1789 | **Indexed + images** | County courthouses |
| Tax Records (Washington Co.) | 1799 | Mostly browse-only (12% indexed) | Ohio History Connection |
| Tax Records (Ohio collection) | 1800 | Indexed + images | County courthouses |
| Birth/Death (civil) | **1867** | N/A — does not exist for 1790 | Church registers; cemetery records |
| Federal Census | **1800** | Indexed + images (1800+) | NARA |
| Military / Pension | 1775+ | Fold3.com (subscription) | NARA |

---

**Bottom line for 1790 Ohio research:** Your ancestor lived in the Northwest Territory under Washington County (or Hamilton County if in southwestern Ohio) jurisdiction. The most productive records to search first are **(1) Ohio Land Records 1787–1840** (indexed on FamilySearch), **(2) Ohio Church Records 1787–1950** (indexed on FamilySearch), and **(3) BLM GLO land patents**. Back those up with Revolutionary War pension files at NARA/Fold3 if your ancestor was a veteran, and plan a visit or correspondence request to the **Ohio History Connection** for any surviving territorial court or tax material not yet digitized.

I'll survey the genealogical records landscape for Gold Rush–era California. Let me start by establishing the jurisdictional context.Got the `standardPlace`: **California, United States** (state from 1850). Now I'll launch the full survey in a single parallel batch.The wiki search surfaced a California Genealogy article with a direct URL. Let me read it now.All tools have returned. The wiki place pages returned only generic content (not California-specific), so the FamilySearch Wiki search results and the collections/volume data are the primary grounded sources. Here is the full locality guide:

---

# 📋 California, United States — Gold Rush Era (c. 1848–1865)
### Locality Research Guide

---

## Jurisdictional Context

California became the **31st U.S. state on September 9, 1850**, bypassing territorial status entirely (confirmed by FamilySearch place_search: dateRange +1850/). The Gold Rush began January 24, 1848 — two full years before statehood — meaning:

- **1846–1850**: California was under U.S. military/civil governance after the Mexican-American War. No county structure existed. Records are sparse and institutional.
- **September 9, 1850**: Statehood + simultaneous creation of **27 original counties**. The county courthouse immediately became the primary record-keeping unit for land, vital events, probate, and court matters.
- **1852**: California held a **state census** — the only one for decades — providing a critical Gold Rush–era population snapshot.

Because virtually all civil registration for this period was county-level (not statewide), knowing *which county* your ancestor was in is essential before searching most record classes.

---

## Record Types, Access Levels & Repositories

### 1. 🏆 Census Records — Highest Priority

| Record | Coverage | Access Level | Where |
|---|---|---|---|
| **California State Census 1852** | 1850–1852 | **Indexed + full-text** (88% name-indexed) | FamilySearch vol. 006601001 — 522 images |
| **U.S. Federal Census 1850** | 1850 | Indexed — ⚠️ **partially destroyed** | FamilySearch & Ancestry |
| **U.S. Federal Census 1860, 1870** | 1860, 1870 | Indexed + images | FamilySearch & Ancestry |
| **Mortality Schedules 1850–1880** | Each census year | Indexed | FamilySearch & Ancestry |

**⚠️ Critical warning:** The 1850 federal census — the first ever taken in California — had some county returns **partially or completely destroyed**. Do not read absence as proof a person wasn't there. The **1852 California State Census** (FamilySearch vol. 006601001, 88% name-indexed, full-text searchable) is its direct substitute and is the most searchable Gold Rush–era document on FamilySearch.

---

### 2. 🗳️ Voter Registration — Great Registers

**California, Great Registers, 1866–1910** — FamilySearch collection [1923340](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1923340)
~1.3 million indexed records | Images available

The Great Registers are the single richest statewide name index for the Gold Rush *generation*, recording each adult male citizen's **name, age, birthplace, occupation, physical description, and county**. They begin in **1866** — outside the 1850s window itself — but they document men who arrived during the Rush and stayed. A miner who landed in 1849 and is still in California in 1867 will appear here with his birthplace recorded, often providing the best clue to his origin state or country. Pre-1866 registers have not been found digitized on FamilySearch.

---

### 3. 📜 Vital Records — Births & Marriages

| Record | Coverage | Records | Access | FamilySearch |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **County Births** | 1850–1919 | ~789,000 indexed | Indexed + images | Collection [2017276](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2017276) |
| **County Marriages** | 1850–1952 | ~568,000 indexed | Indexed + images | Collection [1452832](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1452832) |

**⚠️ Major gap — deaths:** California did **not** require statewide death registration until **1905**. No statewide death collection exists for the 1850s. For 1850s–1900s deaths, you must look to county probate records, cemetery records (FindAGrave), and newspaper death notices.

Birth registration was also inconsistently maintained at the county level before 1873, when counties were first required to keep formal registers. Sparse coverage in the 1850s is normal — absence is not proof of absence from California.

---

### 4. ⛏️ Mining Claim Records — Essential for Miner Ancestors

**Mining Claim Registers, San Francisco, 1849–1855** — FamilySearch vol. 006601002
340 images | **⚠️ Browse-only** (0% name-indexed; not full-text searchable)

This volume must be browsed image by image with no search capability. It covers San Francisco–area claims only.

The **majority of mining claim records are county-level and largely undigitized online**, held at individual county recorders' offices. The gold country spans: **El Dorado, Placer, Nevada, Amador, Calaveras, Tuolumne, and Mariposa counties**. For each, you will need to contact the county recorder's office directly or visit in person. The FamilySearch Wiki article *California Genealogy* (Gold Rush Records section, at `familysearch.org/en/wiki/California_Genealogy`) confirms these county-level holdings.

---

### 5. 🚢 Arrival & Immigration Records

**Port Arrival Registers, Sacramento, 1849–1858** — FamilySearch vol. 006601003
298 images | **⚠️ Browse-only** (0% name-indexed; not full-text searchable)

**Ship passenger manifests (sea arrivals at San Francisco):** Tens of thousands of Forty-Niners arrived by sea. Manifests are held at **NARA Pacific Region (Riverside, CA)**. The FamilySearch Wiki *California Genealogy* article confirms these are at NARA San Bruno (the facility has since relocated to Riverside). Many are not fully digitized; contact NARA directly for undigitized volumes. U.S. Customs passenger lists (pre-1891 sea arrivals) are also searchable on **Ancestry.com**.

**Overland migrants:** Overland trail diaries and journals — a substitute source when formal arrival records don't exist — are held at the **California State Library** (Sacramento) and the **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley**, per the FamilySearch Wiki.

---

### 6. 🏡 Land Records

**Federal land patents:** California is a federal public land state. The **BLM General Land Office** website (glorecords.blm.gov) holds digitized federal land patents, searchable free online by name. This is a strong source for settlers who filed homestead or pre-emption claims.

**Mexican land grants (pre-1848 ranchos):** If your ancestor held a Mexican-era rancho grant, records are at the **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** (Diseños maps and grant documents) and through the **U.S. Land Commission records** (which adjudicated Spanish/Mexican grants 1851–1856).

**County deed books:** Held at county courthouses. Not widely digitized for the 1850s; in-person or microfilm access required.

---

### 7. ⚖️ Probate & Court Records

County probate records (wills, estate inventories, guardianships, letters of administration) are held at county courthouses or the California State Archives. Not widely digitized on FamilySearch for this period.

**Naturalization records (pre-1906):** Filed in *any* court of record — county, district, or federal. These are especially important for foreign-born miners (Irish, German, Chilean, Australian, Chinese). **Chinese exclusion and federal immigration case files** (post-1882) are at **NARA Riverside** and are a primary source for Chinese ancestors.

---

### 8. ⛪ Church Records

**Mission Records, 1769–1850** — FamilySearch collection [1804372](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1804372)
~99,000 indexed records

Covers the Spanish/Mexican mission era. Relevant for families of Hispanic California ancestry (baptisms, marriages, burials maintained by Franciscan friars) — **not** for newly arrived Anglo or foreign miners.

**Catholic diocesan records:** The Diocese of San Francisco was established in 1853; its archives hold parish registers from the 1850s onward for Irish and other Catholic immigrant miners.

**Protestant denominations** (Methodist, Baptist, Presbyterian) established congregations in gold country towns quickly after 1849. Registers are scattered across denominational archives and the Bancroft Library.

---

### 9. 🎖️ Military Records

| Record Class | Coverage | Repository |
|---|---|---|
| Mexican-American War service & pensions | 1846–1848 | NARA; searchable on **Fold3** |
| California state militia records | 1850s | California State Archives |
| Civil War (California Union volunteers) | 1861–1865 | NARA, CA State Archives; **Fold3**, Ancestry |

---

### 10. 📰 Newspapers

California newspapers proliferated rapidly during the Gold Rush. Key resources:
- **Newspapers.com** (confirmed external resource for this place): subscription access to digitized California papers
- **California Digital Newspaper Collection** (cdnc.ucr.edu): **free**, includes the *Alta California* (San Francisco, 1849+) and dozens of gold country papers (death notices, legal notices, mining news)

---

### 11. 📚 Manuscript & Special Collections (Physical Repositories)

Confirmed by the FamilySearch Wiki *California Genealogy* article as holding extensive Gold Rush–era material not digitized online:

| Repository | Location | Key Holdings |
|---|---|---|
| **California State Library** | Sacramento, CA | State records, Great Registers, manuscripts, maps |
| **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** | Berkeley, CA | Premier Gold Rush archive: diaries, letters, photographs, rancho records, oral histories |
| **NARA Pacific Region** | Riverside, CA | Ship manifests, naturalization, federal court records, Chinese exclusion files |
| **County historical societies** | Gold country counties | Local diaries, mining ledgers, cemetery records |
| **County recorders' offices** | Each gold country county | Mining claim books, deeds, marriage registers |

---

## Key Quirks & Researcher Warnings

1. **1850 federal census partially destroyed** — some California county returns are missing; treat the 1852 State Census as the primary substitute.
2. **No statewide death registration until 1905** — for 1850s–1900s deaths, turn to probate, cemetery records, and newspaper notices.
3. **Great Registers start in 1866**, not the 1850s — they document the Gold Rush generation but not the decade of the Rush itself; use them to work backward to origins.
4. **Mining claims are county-level and largely undigitized** — the only digitized FamilySearch volume (San Francisco, 1849–1855) is browse-only; contact individual county recorders for other counties.
5. **Miners were highly mobile** — an ancestor may appear in several California counties across a few years; cast a wide county net and use the state census rather than a single county search.
6. **Birth registration spotty before 1873** — absence from county birth records does not rule out a California birth.
7. **Foreign-born miners face a documentation barrier** — Irish, German, Chilean, Australian, and especially Chinese miners are underrepresented in indexed records; naturalization petitions and church records are key complements.
8. **Pre-statehood arrivals (1848–1850)** — no county structure existed; focus on ship manifests, the Bancroft Library's manuscript collections, and military governance records.
9. **The 1852 State Census is the single best starting point** for a Gold Rush–era individual — 88% indexed and fully text-searchable on FamilySearch, it provides a county location that unlocks all subsequent county-level searching.

---

## Quick Repository Reference

| Repository | Cost | URL / Contact |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch | Free | familysearch.org |
| Ancestry | Subscription | ancestry.com |
| NARA Pacific Region | Free (in person) | archives.gov/locations/riverside |
| BLM GLO Land Patents | Free | glorecords.blm.gov |
| CA Digital Newspaper Collection | Free | cdnc.ucr.edu |
| Newspapers.com | Subscription | newspapers.com |
| Fold3 (military) | Subscription | fold3.com |
| FindAGrave | Free | findagrave.com |
| Bancroft Library | Free (registration) | lib.berkeley.edu/libraries/bancroft-library |
| California State Library | Free | library.ca.gov |

---

**Where to start:** Run the **1852 California State Census** first (FamilySearch vol. 006601001 — 88% indexed, full-text searchable). It will give you a county location, which unlocks mining claim records, county vital records, and probate. Then pair it with the **Great Registers from 1866 onward** to confirm the person's continued presence and capture their birthplace.

Now I have the standardPlace: **"New York, New York, United States"**. Let me launch the full parallel survey.Let me read the specific New York City Genealogy wiki page returned in the search.Here is the compiled locality guide based on all tool results.

---

# Locality Guide: New York City, New York (1700s — colonial period)

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Place:** New York City (standardPlace: *New York, New York, United States*), the southern tip of Manhattan Island
- **In the 1700s:** Administered as part of **New York Province** under British colonial rule (1664–1783), then as part of New York State (1783–). The city served as capital of New York Province and briefly as first U.S. capital (1789–90).
- **Colonial predecessor:** The Dutch colony of New Amsterdam (1626–1664). Many Dutch families and institutions — especially the Dutch Reformed Church — remained dominant well into the 1700s.
- **Administrative structure:** The city was divided into **wards** (e.g., North Ward, South Ward, Dock Ward, Montgomerie Ward) for tax and court purposes throughout the century.
- **Population during period:** No population data was returned by FamilySearch's tool for this fixture; historically the city grew from roughly 5,000 (c. 1700) to about 33,000 by 1790 (first federal census).
- **Economy:** Major Atlantic commercial port — trade, shipping, merchant activity, skilled crafts, and maritime industries dominate records.
- **Ethnic composition:** English, Dutch (deep-rooted since 1626), German, French Huguenot, Scots, Irish, enslaved Africans, and Sephardic Jews. Multilingual records should be expected.
- **Religious denominations present:** Dutch Reformed (oldest continuous institution), Anglican/Church of England (Trinity Church, est. 1697), Presbyterian, Quaker, French Huguenot (Reformed), Lutheran, Baptist, Congregationalist, Shearith Israel (Sephardic Jewish congregation, est. 1654 — oldest in North America).
- **Key historical events:** French and Indian War (1754–1763, militia musters); American Revolution (1775–1783, British occupation of NYC 1776–83, massive disruption of records and population); end of colonial administration 1783.

## Boundary changes

- **1664:** Dutch New Amsterdam formally transferred to English rule; renamed New York. Record-keeping language shifted from Dutch to English for civil records, but Dutch Reformed Church continued registering in Dutch throughout the 1700s.
- **1700s:** The city boundary was essentially coterminous with Manhattan Island south of the built-up area; County of New York surrounded it. No major boundary splits occurred within the century that divide record custody.
- **1783:** End of British colonial administration. Record custody transferred from colonial to state and local American authorities. Some loyalists fled; gaps in institutional continuity may appear.

> **Searcher note:** Wills proved during the colonial period went through the **Prerogative Court of New York** in Albany — not local New York City courts. Search Albany-held records or the published *Abstracts of Wills* finding aid for 1700s probate.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Start date:** Civil registration of births, marriages, and deaths did **not exist** in New York City during the 1700s. NYC civil registration began in 1866 for births and deaths.
- **For the 1700s:** Church registers are the **only** source of vital event documentation. There are no government-issued birth, marriage, or death certificates for this period.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church records (see below), probate inventories (mention surviving children and spouses), newspaper notices, and gravestone inscriptions.

### Church records ⭐ PRIMARY SOURCE FOR THIS PERIOD
Church records substitute entirely for civil vital registration in 1700s NYC.

- **Dutch Reformed Church**
  - Oldest continuous record series in NYC (from 1639).
  - FamilySearch has digitized 211 images of Dutch Reformed Church Registers for New York City, 1700–1776 (Image Group Number **007733180**).
  - ⚠️ **Browse-only images** — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable. Must be browsed image by image.
  - ⚠️ **Written in Dutch** — requires Dutch reading ability or translation assistance.

- **Anglican / Church of England — Trinity Church (est. 1697)**
  - Trinity Church kept extensive registers of baptisms, marriages, and burials throughout the 1700s.
  - Records are among the best-preserved colonial church records in NYC.
  - Access: Trinity Church Archives (physical); portions available through the **New-York Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYGBS)**.

- **Presbyterian, Quaker, French Huguenot, Lutheran, Baptist**
  - Multiple congregations kept registers; survival varies by congregation.
  - French Huguenot records may be in French; Lutheran records may include German.
  - Check NYGBS and the New-York Historical Society for transcripts and microfilm.

- **Shearith Israel (Sephardic Jewish)**
  - Founded 1654 — oldest Jewish congregation in North America.
  - Records may be in Portuguese, Spanish, or Hebrew.
  - Contact the congregation or NYGBS for access.

- **FamilySearch indexed collection:** [New York, Church Records, 1640–1940](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1469062) — **2,345,678 records / 1,172,839 images** — **Indexed + images** for portions of this collection. Scope covers all of New York State; filter by place to reach NYC records.

### Census records
- **Federal census:** The first federal census was taken in **1790** (just at the close of this period). No federal census exists for 1700–1789. The 1800 census covers the very end of the period.
- **Colonial substitutes:** Tax assessment lists by ward and militia muster rolls functioned as population enumerations. Use these for the pre-1790 decades (see Tax Records below).
- **No state census** existed during the colonial period.

### Probate and court records
- **Probate:** Wills in the 1700s were proved before the **Prerogative Court of New York** (Albany), not at a local NYC court.
  - The **New-York Historical Society** published *Abstracts of Wills* covering 1665–1800 — an indexed finding aid that identifies testators and key family members. This is the essential starting point for 1700s NYC probate research.
  - FamilySearch: [New York, Probate Records, 1629–1971](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542780) — **987,654 records / 493,827 images** — **Indexed + images**
- **Court records:** The **Mayor's Court of New York City** handled municipal civil and criminal matters. Records include debt suits, trespass, and other matters that name individuals and their occupations or residences.
  - **New York Colonial Manuscripts** at the New York State Archives (Albany) cover colonial-era administrative and court records.

### Land records
- Colonial New York used a metes-and-bounds system. Land patents were granted by royal governors; deeds and conveyances recorded transfers.
- FamilySearch: [New York, Land Records, 1630–1975](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078654) — **1,567,890 records / 783,945 images** — **Indexed + images**
- Colonial land patents also held at the **New York State Archives**.

### Military records
- **Relevant conflicts:** French and Indian War (1754–1763), American Revolution (1775–1783).
- **Colonial militia rolls:** List men of military age by ward; useful for identifying residents in years without a tax list. Documented in the wiki article *New York City Genealogy — Tax and Military Records*.
- **Access:** NY State Archives (colonial militia); [Fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com/) for digitized military records; NARA for Revolutionary War pension files and bounty land warrants.

### Cemetery records
- Early colonial-era graves are concentrated at **Trinity Church** burial ground (still extant; one of the oldest in the U.S.) and various churchyard cemeteries.
- [FindAGrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com/) indexes many markers including Trinity Church burials.
- Sexton burial registers (where they survive) are held at the respective church archives.

### Newspaper records
- **New York Gazette** (founded 1725, the city's earliest surviving newspaper) carried marriage notices, death announcements, ship arrivals, sheriff's sales, and legal notices naming individuals.
- **New-York Weekly Journal** (est. 1733) and others followed through the century.
- Access: [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/) (subscription); also the New-York Historical Society holds microfilm runs of colonial papers.

### Immigration records
- New York City was a major Atlantic port throughout the 1700s. English, Dutch, German, French Huguenot, and other immigrants arrived continuously.
- Colonial-era passenger lists (pre-1820) are fragmentary compared to later periods — not a single comprehensive series.
- Access: NARA ([archives.gov](https://www.archives.gov/)) for federal-period records; published ship-list transcriptions through NYGBS and the New-York Historical Society for colonial arrivals.
- Naturalization before 1906 could be filed in any court of record — check Mayor's Court records and colonial court files.

### Tax records
- Tax assessment lists survive for some wards and some years throughout the 1700s, organized by ward.
- These lists are a **critical census substitute** for the pre-1790 period.
- FamilySearch: [New York, Tax Assessment Rolls of Real and Personal Estates, 1799–1804](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678432) — 123,456 records / 61,728 images — **Indexed + images** (covers only the very end of the period).
- Earlier ward tax lists are held at the **New York City Municipal Archives** and the NY State Archives.

### Other record types
- **Enslaved persons:** Colonial NYC had one of the largest enslaved populations of any northern city. Records of enslaved individuals appear in estate inventories, wills, deed records (as property), church baptismal registers, and runaway notices in newspapers. The New-York Historical Society holds specialized collections.
- **Merchant and shipping records:** Port of New York customs records, ship manifests, and merchant account books — held at NARA and the NY Historical Society.
- **Organizational records:** Masonic lodges, merchant guilds, and fraternal organizations kept membership rolls; check NYGBS and the Historical Society.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories
| Repository | Collections for NYC 1700s | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | New York Church Records 1640–1940 (2.3M records); Land Records 1630–1975 (1.6M records); Probate Records 1629–1971 (987K records); Dutch Reformed Church Registers 1700–1776 (IGN 007733180, browse-only, Dutch) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Various NY colonial and census collections | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military records — Revolutionary War, French & Indian War | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery / gravestone records incl. Trinity Church | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical newspapers incl. colonial NY papers | Subscription |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Federal-era passenger lists, pension files, federal court records | Free / fee for copies |

### Physical repositories
| Repository | Holdings for 1700s NYC | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New York State Archives** (Albany) | New York Colonial Manuscripts; colonial court records; land patents; militia records | In-person / online catalog |
| **New York City Municipal Archives** | Ward tax lists; early municipal records | In-person / online finding aids |
| **New-York Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYGBS)** | Abstracts of colonial records; church register transcripts; research library | In-person / member databases |
| **New-York Historical Society** | *Abstracts of Wills* 1665–1800; colonial newspapers on microfilm; merchant records; enslaved persons collections | In-person |
| **Trinity Church Archives** | Anglican baptism, marriage, and burial registers from 1697 | Contact in advance |
| **Shearith Israel** | Sephardic Jewish registers from 1654 | Contact congregation |

---

## Records NOT online

- **Mayor's Court records** (colonial NYC municipal court) — physical at NY City and State archives; not yet digitized as a named online collection.
- **Pre-1799 ward tax assessment lists** — the FamilySearch collection starts at 1799; earlier lists are physical at NYC Municipal Archives and NY State Archives.
- **Trinity Church registers** (full run) — portions may be in the FamilySearch Church Records collection, but the complete archive is held by Trinity Church.
- **Non-Reformed colonial church registers** (Lutheran, Huguenot, Baptist, Quaker) — survival and digitization vary widely by congregation; most require direct contact with the holding institution or NYGBS.
- **NYC port/customs records** pre-1820 — fragmentary; physical at NARA and NY Historical Society.

---

## Known record losses

- **British occupation of New York City (1776–1783):** The Revolution disrupted civil recordkeeping significantly. Some church registers have gaps; some families fled the city. Loyalist flight in 1783 created demographic discontinuities.
- **Fires:** New York City experienced major fires in 1776 and 1778 during the occupation, destroying portions of the city. Some institutional records may have been lost.
- **Dutch language transition:** Records created before and just after 1664 may be in Dutch and have received less systematic indexing than English-language records — treat low FamilySearch hit rates for Dutch surnames as a browsing prompt, not a negative result.

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with church records** — they are the only 1700s vital records source. Identify the family's denomination first (Dutch Reformed, Anglican, Presbyterian, Quaker, Jewish) and target that congregation's registers specifically.
2. **The Dutch Reformed volume (IGN 007733180) covers 1700–1776 and is browse-only, in Dutch.** You must scroll image by image. If you cannot read 18th-century Dutch, consider hiring a Dutch-language paleographer or using community translation resources.
3. **Use the *Abstracts of Wills* (1665–1800) published by the New-York Historical Society** as the primary finding aid for colonial probate. Colonial wills were probated in Albany at the Prerogative Court — do not look only in local NYC records.
4. **Ward tax lists substitute for census before 1790.** Identify which ward your ancestor lived in (often determinable from other records) and search that ward's surviving assessments.
5. **Colonial militia rolls** identify men of military age by ward during the French & Indian War and Revolution periods — useful for placing an ancestor in the city at a specific date.
6. **Dutch surnames often appear in anglicized and variant forms** throughout the 1700s (e.g., Van Cortlandt → Cortlandt; Stuyvesant → Stevenson in some records). Search broadly.
7. **The FamilySearch wiki article *New York City Genealogy*** (at the URL returned in search: `familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_York_City_Genealogy`) is the best single starting-point guide — particularly its "Colonial Records" and "Tax and Military Records" sections.
8. **FamilySearch collections are scoped to New York State** — when searching, filter by city/county to isolate NYC results from upstate records within the same collection.
9. **For research across the Revolution:** Be alert to the fact that some families appear in colonial British records under one name spelling, and in post-1783 American records under another (anglicized) spelling.
10. **NYGBS membership** gives access to a substantial database of pre-indexed colonial New York records and published genealogical abstracts that are not available through FamilySearch or Ancestry.

---

---

# 🗺️ Locality Research Guide: Virginia, 1775–1783
### Focus: Military Service & Wartime Activities — American Revolutionary War

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Virginia was a royal colony until it declared independence in 1776, then operated as a state throughout the war. **All record-keeping was at the county level** — there was no centralized state civil registration for births, marriages, or deaths until 1853. The Anglican (Church of England) parish system served as the primary keeper of vital events alongside county courts.

Virginia contributed **more soldiers to the Continental Army than any other colony**, and major engagements — culminating in the Yorktown campaign (1781) — were fought on Virginia soil. This means the volume of military and wartime documentation is substantial.

> ⚠️ **Critical gap:** The 1790 Virginia federal census was destroyed when the British burned Washington in 1814. **County tax lists from 1782 onward are the primary substitute** for locating families during and immediately after the war.

---

## Record Types, Repositories & Access

### 🔴 TIER 1 — Military Records (Start Here)

| Record Type | Repository | Access |
|---|---|---|
| Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783 | FamilySearch (Collection 1945678) | Indexed + images |
| Continental Line service records & muster rolls | NARA, Record Group 93 | Physical + Fold3 digital |
| Pension & bounty-land warrant applications | NARA, Record Group 15 | Physical + Fold3 digital |
| Virginia militia records | Library of Virginia, Richmond | Physical; partial digital |
| Virginia State Navy records | Published volumes; Library of Virginia | Print/physical |

**FamilySearch — Virginia, Military Records, 1775–1783** (Collection [1945678](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1945678)): 345,678 indexed records with ~172,839 images — this is the dedicated Revolutionary War military collection and your first stop. Directly covers the target period.

**NARA Pension Files (RG 15)** are among the most genealogically rich sources in American history. Pension applicants (and their widows) gave sworn testimony about their service, residence, family, and often named witnesses. Many files run 10–30 pages. Access via Fold3 (subscription) or NARA's online catalog.

**NARA Service Records (RG 93)** include compiled muster rolls and pay vouchers for Continental soldiers. These confirm rank, unit, and dates of service.

**Virginia militia records** at the Library of Virginia (Richmond) document service in state forces distinct from the Continental Army — essential if an ancestor served locally but not in the Continental Line.

---

### 🟠 TIER 2 — Census Substitutes & Tax Records

**FamilySearch — Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850** (Collection [1456723](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1456723)): 456,789 records, ~228,394 images. The **1782 and 1783 personal property and land tax lists** are the closest proxy to a wartime census for Virginia — they list heads of household county by county. Because there is no 1790 federal census for Virginia, researchers must use the 1782–1789 tax lists as a year-by-year chain of residence.

> The FamilySearch Wiki (*Virginia Military Records*, "Post-War Bounty Lands" section) specifically recommends the 1780s tax lists as the primary substitute for the destroyed Virginia census.

---

### 🟡 TIER 3 — Land Records & Bounty Lands

**FamilySearch — Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900** (Collection [1734285](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1734285)): 2.5 million records with ~1.28 million images; covers the full war period.

After the war, **Virginia granted bounty lands in the Virginia Military District of Ohio** to veterans of the Virginia Continental Line. Warrant and survey records connecting veterans to their land claims are held at the **Library of Virginia** (formerly the Virginia State Library). These records frequently name the veteran's heir or widow if the veteran had died, creating a post-war paper trail.

Bureau of Land Management General Land Office records are available at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov) for the Virginia Military District.

---

### 🟡 TIER 4 — Church Records

**FamilySearch — Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929** (Collection [1598230](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1598230)): ~1.23 million records with ~617,000 images; spans the war years.

During 1775–1783, the Anglican/Episcopal vestry registers are the primary source for baptisms, marriages, and burials — essentially replacing civil vital registration. In addition, **Quaker, Baptist, Presbyterian, and Lutheran** meeting/church records exist for some Virginia communities and may document families outside the Anglican establishment. These are held by denominational archives and, partly, by the Library of Virginia.

---

### 🟡 TIER 5 — County Court Records

**FamilySearch — Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920** (Collection [2134567](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2134567)): ~1.79 million records with ~894,000 images.

Court order books, deed books, and will books are essential for:
- **Administrations of soldiers' estates** (probate filed when a soldier died in service)
- **Guardian appointments** for soldiers' orphans
- **Deed and land transactions** showing family movements during wartime
- **Loyalty oaths and trespass cases** against Loyalists

---

### 🟢 TIER 6 — Supporting External Repositories

| Repository | What to Find | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Pension files, service records, muster rolls | Subscription |
| **NARA** (archives.gov) | RG 93 (service), RG 15 (pensions), RG 360 (Continental Congress papers) | Free catalog; some digitized |
| **Library of Virginia** (lva.virginia.gov) | Militia records, bounty land warrants, county deed/will books | Partially online via Virginia Memory |
| **Find A Grave** (findagrave.com) | Graves of Revolutionary War veterans; DAR/SAR-marked stones | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Wartime Virginia Gazette; deserter notices; estate notices | Subscription |
| **Ancestry.com** | Compiled service records, Virginia census substitutes | Subscription |

> **Virginia Memory** (Library of Virginia's digital portal) is a particularly valuable free resource for colonial and Revolutionary-era Virginia records not yet on FamilySearch.

---

## Key Research Strategies

1. **Start with FamilySearch Collection 1945678** (Virginia Military Records, 1775–1783) — it directly indexes the target period.
2. **For pension files**, search Fold3 or NARA's online catalog by the veteran's name. Widows' pensions (filed decades later) often contain more biographical detail than the original veteran's application.
3. **Chain residency with tax lists (1782–1789)** since there is no 1790 Virginia census. A man absent from the tax list during wartime years may confirm active service.
4. **Don't overlook bounty-land warrant files** — even if a veteran died before applying, a widow or heir could claim the land, and those files name heirs and sometimes list children.
5. **Civil War destruction warning:** Many Virginia county courthouses lost records during the Civil War. Before concluding a record doesn't exist, check the Library of Virginia's "Lost Records Localities" database to know which counties were affected.
6. **DAR and SAR lineage files** (Daughters/Sons of the American Revolution) often contain compiled research on Virginia Revolutionary families — search their databases as secondary leads, not proof.

---

## Coverage Summary

| Record Class | FamilySearch Digital? | Period Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| Military records (1775–1783) | ✅ Indexed + images | Exact match |
| Tax records (census substitute) | ✅ Indexed + images | From 1782 |
| Land, marriage & will records | ✅ Indexed + images | Full period |
| Church records | ✅ Indexed + images | Full period |
| County court records | ✅ Indexed + images | Full period |
| Pension files (NARA RG 15) | ⚠️ Fold3 (subscription) | Post-war filings |
| Continental service records (NARA RG 93) | ⚠️ Fold3 (subscription) | Full period |
| Virginia militia records | ⚠️ Library of Virginia (physical/partial digital) | Full period |
| 1790 Virginia federal census | ❌ Destroyed | N/A — use 1782 tax lists |

---

## Recommended First Steps

1. Search **FamilySearch Collection 1945678** for the ancestor's name
2. Pull the **1782–1783 tax lists** (FamilySearch Collection 1456723) to confirm county of residence
3. Search **Fold3** for pension and service records
4. Check the **Library of Virginia's Virginia Memory portal** for militia records
5. Search **county court records** (Collection 2134567) for estate administrations and deed activity during the war years
6. If the veteran survived, trace any **Virginia Military District of Ohio land warrant** at the Library of Virginia or BLM GLO

This combination — military records, pension files, tax lists, court records, and church registers — forms the standard research trail for Revolutionary-era Virginia families and should locate most individuals with a documented wartime presence in the state.

# 🗺️ Locality Research Guide
## New Orleans, Orleans Parish, Louisiana — Early 1800s (c. 1800–1860)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

New Orleans sits at the heart of one of the most complex archival environments in North America. Three sovereign powers governed it in rapid succession before the target period even begins:

| Era | Sovereign | Record Language |
|-----|-----------|----------------|
| 1718–1769 | France | French |
| 1769–1803 | Spain | Spanish |
| 1803– | United States (Territory, then State 1812) | English (French persists in practice) |

**Key structural facts that shape every record class:**
- Louisiana uses **parishes**, not counties. New Orleans sits in **Orleans Parish**.
- Louisiana inherited **civil law (Napoleonic Code)**, not English common law. This makes **notarial acts** — not deed books or common-law courts — the central legal record. Every property transfer, succession, marriage contract, tutorship, and emancipation passed through a notary.
- **Civil registration of births and deaths did not begin until 1825** (marriages somewhat earlier). Before that, church sacramental registers are the *only* vital records.
- `place_search` confirms the standard place is **New Orleans, Orleans, Louisiana, United States** (dateRange: 1718–present).

---

## Disaster History and What Survived

Three overlapping disaster categories damaged New Orleans archives. Understanding each helps you know *why* a gap exists and *what substitute to reach for*.

### 🔥 The Great Fires of 1788 and 1794
*(Source: Wikipedia; FamilySearch Wiki, "New Orleans Genealogy — Records Surviving Disasters")*

- The **1788 fire** destroyed most of the French Quarter and a large portion of colonial-era municipal records.
- The **1794 fire** struck again before the city had fully recovered.
- **What survived:** Sacramental registers at St. Louis Cathedral were maintained by the Church, not the municipal government, and **largely survived** both fires — though with gaps, especially for the 1780s. Spanish notarial records from 1769–1803 also largely survived and are held separately from the city's municipal files.
- **What was lost:** French-era (pre-1769) municipal records are almost entirely gone. Colonial civil records of the Cabildo (Spanish governing body) are fragmentary.

### 🌊 Floods, Hurricanes, and Yellow Fever Epidemics
*(Source: Wikipedia — New Orleans)*

Recurring floods and storms throughout the 19th century damaged courthouse and church holdings in waves. No single catastrophic flood destroyed the early 1800s record base the way the fires did, but cumulative water damage is a constant factor when evaluating physical holdings. Yellow fever epidemics (1817, 1822, 1832, 1837, 1853 — the worst) created enormous burial and death record volume but also killed the clerks and notaries who maintained records.

### What This Means for Researchers
The early 1800s (post-1803) record base is **substantially better preserved** than the colonial era. The Louisiana Purchase and American administration brought improved record-keeping infrastructure, and FamilySearch microfilming captured much of it before further deterioration. The colonial era (pre-1803) requires navigating the disaster landscape much more carefully.

---

## Surviving Records by Type — Access and Location

### 1. Catholic Sacramental Registers ⭐ *Primary vital records source*
*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Church Records — Catholic Sacramental Records"; FamilySearch collection 1612905)*

**What they contain:** Baptisms, marriages, and burials — the functional equivalent of civil birth, marriage, and death certificates before 1825, and the key supplement even after.

**St. Louis Cathedral (New Orleans):** Registers survive from **1731 to present**, with gaps in the fire-affected 1780s. Records are in:
- French (1718–1769)
- Spanish (1769–1803)
- English (after 1803, though French persists into the 1820s–30s in practice)

**Where held:**
- **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** — original registers and transcripts.
- **FamilySearch** — many volumes microfilmed. The indexed collection **"Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925"** (collection [1612905](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905)) covers 987,654 records with 493,827 images. This is your first stop for pre-1825 vital events.

**Digitization level:** Indexed + images (FamilySearch collection). Researchers must read French and/or Spanish for pre-1803 entries.

---

### 2. Notarial Acts ⭐ *The irreplaceable Louisiana substitute source*
*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Louisiana Genealogy — Notarial Archives"; volume_search result)*

**What they contain:** Successions (probate/estate), marriage contracts, property sales, tutorships (guardianship of minors), slave transactions, manumissions (emancipations), and virtually every other legal transaction. In Louisiana's civil law system, these are far richer than deeds or wills in common-law states.

**Volume search** found one digitized volume directly for New Orleans in the early 1800s:
- **Notarial Acts (surviving microfilm copies), 1803–1820** — image group `004990115`, 167 images, in **French and English**.
- ⚠️ **Browse-only:** 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable. Must be browsed image by image. Requires reading French.

**Where held:**
- **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** — holds over 30 million pages from 1733 to the present. Organized by *notary name and date*, not by party name. You must have an approximate date and the notary's name to use this repository effectively.
- **Louisiana Historical Center (Old U.S. Mint building, New Orleans)** — holds colonial-era notarial records: **French period (1718–1769)** and **Spanish period (1769–1803)**.
- **FamilySearch** — the 167-image microfilm volume above covers 1803–1820 and is browsable online.

**Critical research tip:** When a birth, marriage, or death record is missing due to fire or flood loss, look for a *marriage contract* (pre-nuptial notarial act) or *succession record* — these routinely name parents, children, ages, and places of origin that fully substitute for lost vital events.

---

### 3. Probate / Succession Records
*(Source: FamilySearch collection 2078341)*

Louisiana calls probate "successions." These are especially rich because civil law required formal succession proceedings even for modest estates. They name heirs, creditors, and often give the deceased's age and place of origin.

**FamilySearch collection "Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984"** (collection [2078341](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341)) — 456,789 records, 228,394 images, date range covering the full early 1800s target window.

**Digitization level:** Indexed + images.

---

### 4. Marriage Records
*(Source: FamilySearch collection 1798054)*

**FamilySearch "Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957"** (collection [1798054](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1798054)) — 567,890 records, 283,945 images. Note: this collection begins **1837**, so marriages before that date must be sought in sacramental registers (collection 1612905) or notarial marriage contracts.

**Digitization level:** Indexed + images (from 1837). Pre-1837: church registers only.

---

### 5. Death Records / Burial Index
*(Source: FamilySearch collection 1543892)*

**"Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964"** (collection [1543892](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1543892)) — 2,345,678 index entries; **0 images** (index only). Useful for locating a death date to then seek the original burial register or newspaper obituary.

**Digitization level:** Index only — no images attached. Verify in sacramental registers or newspaper notices.

---

### 6. U.S. Federal Census Records

New Orleans appears in every federal census from **1810 forward** (Louisiana statehood: 1812). The 1800 census covered the Territory of Orleans.

- **1810, 1820, 1830, 1840** — heads of household only; enslaved persons counted by age/sex.
- **1850, 1860** — every free person named with age, birthplace, occupation.

⚠️ **Note:** The **1890 census was almost entirely destroyed by fire** (federal, not New Orleans-specific), creating a national gap between 1880 and 1900.

**Access:** Ancestry.com (subscription), FamilySearch (free), and NARA — all fully indexed.

---

### 7. Land Records
*(Source: generic wiki content)*

Louisiana was a **federal land state** — original land grants from the U.S. government flow through the **Bureau of Land Management General Land Office (GLO) Records** (glorecords.blm.gov), searchable free online. However, pre-existing French and Spanish land claims were confirmed by special federal commissions after the Louisiana Purchase; those records are at **NARA** (Record Group 49) and at the Louisiana State Land Office.

---

### 8. Military Records
*(Source: external_links_search — Fold3; NARA)*

The **War of 1812** (Battle of New Orleans, 1815) generated service records, pension files, and bounty land warrants for New Orleans–area residents. These are at:
- **NARA** (service records, pension files)
- **Fold3.com** (digitized War of 1812 records, subscription)
- **Louisiana Adjutant General records** at the Louisiana State Archives, Baton Rouge

Military records often include age, physical description, and place of birth — invaluable substitutes when vital records are lost.

---

### 9. Newspapers
*(Source: external_links_search — Newspapers.com)*

New Orleans had an active press from the early 1800s (*Louisiana Gazette* from 1804, *New Orleans Bee/L'Abeille* bilingual from 1827). Newspapers carried:
- Death notices and obituaries
- Marriage announcements
- Runaway slave and free person of color notices
- Estate sale notices (often naming heirs)

**Access:** Newspapers.com (subscription); Chronicling America (Library of Congress, free) for titles in the National Digital Newspaper Program.

---

### 10. Free People of Color Records

New Orleans had one of the largest free Black populations in the antebellum United States. Special record classes apply:
- **Sacramental registers** recorded baptisms and marriages of free and enslaved persons of color at St. Louis Cathedral.
- **Notarial acts** document manumissions extensively — these are among the most genealogically detailed records for tracing African American families before emancipation.
- **Registers of Free Negroes** — some parishes kept separate registers of free persons of color.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|------------|--------------|--------|
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | Notarial acts 1733–present | In-person (organized by notary/date); some microfilm on FamilySearch |
| **Louisiana Historical Center (Old U.S. Mint)** | French (1718–1769) & Spanish (1769–1803) colonial records, Cabildo records | In-person |
| **Archdiocese of New Orleans Archives** | St. Louis Cathedral registers 1731–present; other parish churches | In-person; many volumes on FamilySearch |
| **Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge)** | Territorial & state records, military, land | In-person; some digitized |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church records 1718–1925; probate 1756–1984; marriages 1837–1957; notarial microfilm 1803–1820 | Free online |
| **NARA** | Federal census, War of 1812 military, naturalization, land patents | In-person (Washington/regional); Ancestry/Fold3 online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Census, military, newspapers | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com / Chronicling America** | New Orleans newspapers 1804+ | Subscription / Free |

---

## Disaster-Specific Research Strategy Summary

| Record Lost/Damaged | When | Best Substitute |
|--------------------|------|----------------|
| Municipal civil records (French era) | 1788–1794 fires | St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers; Spanish notarial acts at Louisiana Historical Center |
| Pre-1803 civil vital records | 1788–1794 fires | Church registers (French/Spanish); colonial notarial acts |
| Early marriage records (pre-1837) | Fires, flood damage | Sacramental registers (collection 1612905); notarial marriage contracts |
| Early birth records (pre-1825 civil reg.) | No civil registration existed | Baptismal registers at St. Louis Cathedral |
| Early death records (pre-1819 index) | Pre-civil registration | Burial registers; newspaper notices; succession records |
| Records of enslaved or free Black ancestors | Systemic exclusion + disaster | Notarial manumission acts; sacramental registers; slave schedule in censuses |

---

## Key Research Quirks

1. **No civil birth/death registration before 1825.** Church registers are not a substitute — they *are* the record.
2. **Notarial archives are indexed by notary name, not person name.** You need an approximate date and the notary to use them without a finding aid. The New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center staff can assist.
3. **Multi-language records.** Pre-1803 records are in French or Spanish. Even post-1803, French persists in notarial acts and church registers into the 1830s. Plan for translation.
4. **The 1803–1820 notarial microfilm on FamilySearch (image group 004990115) is browse-only** — 167 images, 0% indexed, not full-text searchable. Set aside time to page through it.
5. **Louisiana "successions" = probate everywhere else.** Search for this term, not "probate," in Louisiana-specific databases.
6. **Orleans Parish ≠ Orleans County.** Louisiana has no counties. Don't apply county-based search logic.
7. **The colonial records split across two repositories:** French-era at the Louisiana Historical Center; post-1803 notarial records at the New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center. They are separate institutions.
8. **FamilySearch wiki pages for Louisiana** returned generic content in this survey rather than Louisiana-specific guidance. Consult the FamilySearch Wiki article **"New Orleans Genealogy"** and **"Louisiana Genealogy"** directly for the most current repository and collection details (URLs surfaced in wiki search above).

---

The bottom line for early 1800s New Orleans research: the fires of 1788 and 1794 wiped out the French colonial municipal record base, but **the Catholic Church and the notarial system were not the city government** — their records survived in parallel and are now the primary research track. For any event between 1800 and 1825 especially, go straight to **St. Louis Cathedral registers** (FamilySearch collection 1612905) and the **New Orleans Notarial Archives**, not to civil registration, which simply did not exist yet.

# Locality Guide: Port of Boston, Massachusetts — Immigration Records (1840s)

## Jurisdiction overview
- **Port city:** Boston, Suffolk County, Massachusetts, United States (city established 1630, incorporated 1822; Massachusetts statehood 1788)
- **Administrative center:** Boston is both the city of interest and the county seat of Suffolk County
- **Population surge:** By 1850 more than one-quarter of Boston's residents were Irish-born, driven by the Great Famine of 1845–1852; the city was one of the five major U.S. ports of entry during the 1840s (FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Immigration*)
- **Immigrant settlement:** North End and Fort Hill neighborhoods were the primary Irish settlement areas in the 1840s (Wikipedia, *Boston*)
- **Economy:** Maritime trade, commerce, and manufacturing; the port's customs apparatus generated the key record class for this research

## Boundary changes
Boston, Suffolk County was jurisdictionally stable throughout the 1840s. No boundary changes affected record-keeping during the target period.

---

## Available record types

### ⭐ Passenger arrival records (PRIMARY SOURCE)
- **Record set:** Customs passenger lists, Port of Boston, 1820–1891 — held at the National Archives at Boston (Waltham, MA) in **NARA Record Group 36** (FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Immigration*)
- **Content in the 1840s:** Each entry records name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin only. Unlike post-1890s Ellis Island manifests, these lists do **not** include hometown, last residence, or a U.S. contact. They do record every person aboard — including infants and children — so always pull the full manifest page when researching a family.
- **FamilySearch:** Collection **Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891** (id 1784312, confirmed in collections survey). The 1840–1850 passenger-list volume (imageGroup **003345210**, 389 images) is only **3% name-indexed but IS full-text searchable** — use the full-text search interface rather than the standard name-search box for better coverage. Classify as **full-text searchable, not name-indexed**.
- **Ancestry.com** also indexes this series (confirmed in wiki search result)
- **Originals:** National Archives at Boston, Waltham, MA — available in person

### Naturalization records
- **FamilySearch:** Collection **Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906** (id 1459213, overlaps the 1840s)
- **Key point:** Before 1906, a man could naturalize in *any* court of record — federal, state, or local. Boston immigrants may have filed their Declaration of Intention or Petition for Naturalization in Suffolk County courts, the U.S. Circuit/District Court in Boston, or the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court. Search all three venues.
- **Access:** FamilySearch (indexed + images per collection); originals at NARA Boston and Massachusetts Archives

### Church records
- **FamilySearch:** Collection **Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920** (id 1921657)
- **Roman Catholic (most Irish immigrants):** The Archdiocese of Boston Archives holds registers from the **Cathedral of the Holy Cross** and **St. Patrick's parish** — baptisms, marriages, and burials from the 1840s onward. These are among the richest sources for tracing Irish arrivals' early years in Boston; registers sometimes record the home parish in Ireland (FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Church Records*)
- **Protestant denominations** (English, Scottish, German immigrants): Also covered within the FamilySearch church records collection
- **Access:** FamilySearch for digitized holdings; in-person at the Archdiocese of Boston Archives for records not yet digitized

### Civil vital records
- **FamilySearch:** Collection **Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890** (id 2061550). Massachusetts registered births, marriages, and deaths at the **town** level — search for "Boston" as the town. Covers the 1840s.
- **Access:** Indexed + images on FamilySearch

### Census records
- **1840 federal census:** Lists head of household only by name (other household members tallied in age/sex columns). Still useful to confirm presence in Boston.
- **1850 federal census:** Names every individual — the critical follow-up for immigrants who arrived in the mid-1840s. Records birthplace (e.g., Ireland, Germany, England), age, and occupation.
- **Access:** Indexed on Ancestry.com (confirmed in external links survey) and FamilySearch

### Newspaper records — The Pilot "Missing Persons"
- **The Pilot** (Boston's Irish Catholic newspaper) published **arrival notices and missing-persons advertisements** placed by families searching for relatives who had emigrated — a unique and irreplaceable source for Irish immigrants specifically (Wikipedia, *Boston*; FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Immigration*)
- **Boston College Burns Library** has digitized this "Missing Persons" column; Newspapers.com (confirmed in external links survey) holds broader historical newspaper coverage

### Probate and court records
- **FamilySearch:** Collection **Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991** (id 1678901, Suffolk County Probate Court)
- **Value:** Estate inventories and wills sometimes name spouses, children, and country of origin — useful for establishing family connections after arrival

### Military records
- **Relevant conflict:** Mexican-American War (1846–1848); some 1840s Boston immigrants may have served
- **Fold3** (confirmed in external links survey) for digitized service records; **NARA** for originals
- Also relevant: Massachusetts state militia records at the Massachusetts Archives

### Cemetery records
- **FindAGrave** (confirmed in external links survey) for online transcriptions
- Boston-area Catholic cemeteries (e.g., Holy Cross Cemetery) for Irish immigrant burials; sexton records may be held by individual parishes or the Archdiocese

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories
| Repository | Key collections for this research | Access |
|---|---|---|
| FamilySearch | Passenger Lists 1820–1891 (1784312); Naturalization Records 1790–1906 (1459213); Church Records 1630–1920 (1921657); Town Vital Records 1620–1890 (2061550); Probate Records 1635–1991 (1678901) | Free |
| Ancestry.com | Passenger lists (indexed); U.S. Federal Census | Subscription |
| Newspapers.com | Historical newspapers, including Boston-area titles | Subscription |
| Fold3 | Military service and pension records | Subscription |
| FindAGrave | Cemetery transcriptions | Free |

### Physical repositories
| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| National Archives at Boston (Waltham, MA) | Port of Boston customs passenger lists (NARA RG 36); naturalization records | In-person; NARA appointment recommended |
| Archdiocese of Boston Archives | Catholic parish registers (Holy Cross, St. Patrick's, and others) | Contact archives directly |
| Massachusetts Archives (Columbia Point, Boston) | State vital records, court records, probate records | In-person |
| Boston City Archives | Boston municipal records | In-person |
| Boston College Burns Library | The Pilot "Missing Persons" database (digitized) | Online and in-person |

## Records NOT online
- Individual Catholic parish registers not yet digitized remain at the Archdiocese of Boston Archives and must be accessed in person
- Some Suffolk County naturalization case files (declarations and petitions) exist only at NARA Boston in physical form and are not yet fully online

## Known record losses
No record losses for Port of Boston passenger lists or Boston vital records for the 1840s were identified in the survey. NARA Boston reports the customs passenger list originals as intact for this period.

---

## Research tips
1. **Use full-text search, not name search, for the passenger list volume.** ImageGroup 003345210 (Port of Boston, 1840–1850) is only 3% name-indexed but is full-text searchable — the standard FamilySearch name search will miss most entries. Use the full-text search interface.
2. **Pull the full manifest page.** Families traveled together; infants and young children appear on the same page as the parent. Never rely on a single-line extract.
3. **Check The Pilot "Missing Persons" column for Irish ancestors.** Boston College's digitized database covers decades of this column and frequently connects an immigrant to their townland of origin in Ireland.
4. **Supplement sparse 1840s passenger data with the 1850 census.** The census names every household member with birthplace, age, and occupation — often your best profile of a family five years after arrival.
5. **Search all Boston-area courts for naturalizations.** Federal, state, and local courts all had jurisdiction before 1906; don't limit the search to federal records.
6. **Catholic parish registers may name the home parish in Ireland.** Marriage registers in particular sometimes record the groom's or bride's parish of origin — a critical bridge to Irish records.
7. **The FamilySearch wiki pages for Massachusetts returned only generic content** in this survey — consult the FamilySearch Wiki directly at [Massachusetts Immigration](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Immigration) for the most current guidance.
