# Locality Guide: Port of Boston, Massachusetts — Immigration Records (1840s)

## Jurisdiction overview
- **Port city:** Boston, Suffolk County, Massachusetts (city established 1630; Massachusetts statehood 1788)
- **Role in the 1840s:** One of the five major U.S. ports of entry. Boston received tens of thousands of Irish Famine immigrants, particularly 1845–1852; by 1850 more than one-quarter of the city's residents were Irish-born. *(FamilySearch Wiki, Massachusetts Immigration; Wikipedia, Boston)*
- **Immigrant neighborhoods:** North End and Fort Hill were the primary settlement areas in the 1840s

## Boundary changes
Boston, Suffolk County was jurisdictionally stable throughout the 1840s. No boundary changes affected record-keeping during this period.

---

## Available record types

### ⭐ Passenger arrival records — primary source
| Detail | Value |
|---|---|
| **Record set** | Customs passenger lists, Port of Boston, 1820–1891 |
| **NARA holding** | Record Group 36 — National Archives at Boston (Waltham, MA) |
| **FamilySearch** | Collection 1784312 — *Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891* |
| **Digitized volume** | ImageGroup **003345210** — 389 images covering 1840–1850 |
| **Digitization level** | ⚠️ **3% name-indexed but full-text searchable** — use the full-text search interface, *not* the standard name-search box |
| **Also on Ancestry** | Yes (confirmed in wiki survey) |
| **1840s content** | Name, age, sex, occupation, country of origin only — no hometown, no last residence, no U.S. contact |

> **Important:** These lists record every person aboard, including infants and young children. Always pull the full manifest page to find all family members traveling together — never rely on a single-line extract.

### Naturalization records
- **FamilySearch:** Collection 1459213 — *Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906*
- **Critical quirk:** Before 1906, a man could naturalize in *any* court of record. Boston immigrants may have filed in Suffolk County courts, the U.S. Circuit/District Court in Boston, or the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court — search all three venues.
- **Access:** FamilySearch (indexed + images); originals at NARA Boston and Massachusetts Archives

### Church records
- **FamilySearch:** Collection 1921657 — *Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920*
- **Roman Catholic (most Irish immigrants):** The **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** holds registers for the Cathedral of the Holy Cross, St. Patrick's, and other parishes — baptisms, marriages, and burials from the 1840s onward. Marriage registers frequently record the home parish in Ireland, making them a critical bridge to Irish records. *(FamilySearch Wiki, Massachusetts Church Records)*
- **Access for undigitized registers:** Contact the Archdiocese of Boston Archives directly

### Civil vital records
- **FamilySearch:** Collection 2061550 — *Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890*
- **Key quirk:** Massachusetts registered vital events at the **town** level. Search for **"Boston"** as the town.
- **Access:** Indexed + images on FamilySearch

### Census records
- **1840 federal census:** Lists head of household only by name — useful to confirm presence in Boston
- **1850 federal census:** Names every individual with age, birthplace, and occupation — the critical cross-check for immigrants who arrived in the mid-1840s
- **Access:** Ancestry.com (subscription) and FamilySearch (free); both are name-indexed

### Newspaper records — The Pilot "Missing Persons" column
- Boston's Irish Catholic newspaper, **The Pilot**, published arrival notices and missing-persons advertisements placed by families searching for relatives who had emigrated — an **irreplaceable source** for Irish immigrants specifically *(Wikipedia, Boston; FamilySearch Wiki, Massachusetts Immigration)*
- **Boston College Burns Library** has digitized this column; broader newspaper coverage at **Newspapers.com**

### Probate records
- **FamilySearch:** Collection 1678901 — *Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991* (Suffolk County Probate Court)
- Estate inventories and wills can name spouses, children, and country of origin — useful for building out a family after arrival

### Military records
- Relevant conflict: Mexican-American War (1846–1848)
- **Fold3** and **NARA** for service and pension records

### Cemetery records
- **FindAGrave** for online transcriptions; Boston-area Catholic cemeteries (Holy Cross Cemetery and others) for Irish immigrant burials

---

## Repository guide

### Online
| Repository | Key collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Passenger Lists 1784312; Naturalization 1459213; Church Records 1921657; Town Vitals 2061550; Probate 1678901 | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Passenger lists (also indexed here); U.S. Federal Census | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical newspapers including Boston titles | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military service and pension records | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery transcriptions | Free |
| **Boston College Burns Library** | The Pilot "Missing Persons" digitized database | Free online |

### Physical repositories
| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **National Archives at Boston** (Waltham, MA) | Port of Boston customs passenger lists (NARA RG 36); naturalization records | In-person; appointment recommended |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** | Catholic parish registers (Holy Cross, St. Patrick's, and others) | Contact archives directly |
| **Massachusetts Archives** (Columbia Point, Boston) | State vital records, court records, probate | In-person |
| **Boston City Archives** | Municipal records | In-person |

---

## Records NOT online
- Many individual Catholic parish registers remain at the Archdiocese of Boston Archives undigitized
- Some Suffolk County naturalization case files (declarations and petitions) exist only at NARA Boston in physical form

## Known record losses
No record losses for Port of Boston passenger lists or Boston vital records for the 1840s were identified in this survey. NARA Boston holds the customs passenger list originals for this period intact.

---

## Research tips

1. **Use full-text search for the passenger list volume.** ImageGroup 003345210 (Port of Boston, 1840–1850) is only 3% name-indexed. The standard FamilySearch name search will miss most entries — use the full-text search interface instead.

2. **Pull the full manifest page.** Families traveled together; infants and children are listed on the same page. A single-person extract is not enough.

3. **Check The Pilot "Missing Persons" for Irish ancestors.** Boston College's free database frequently connects an immigrant to their townland or parish of origin in Ireland — a link that the passenger list itself won't provide.

4. **Use the 1850 census to supplement sparse passenger data.** It names every family member with birthplace and age — your best portrait of a family five years after arrival.

5. **Search all Boston-area courts for naturalizations.** Federal, state, and local courts all had jurisdiction before 1906; limiting the search to federal records will miss a large share of filings.

6. **Catholic parish registers may name the home Irish parish.** Marriage records in particular often record the groom's or bride's Irish parish of origin — the bridge to finding the family in Irish records.

7. **FamilySearch wiki pages for Massachusetts returned only generic content** in this survey. For Massachusetts-specific research strategy, consult the FamilySearch Wiki directly: [Massachusetts Immigration](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Immigration) and [Massachusetts Church Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Church_Records).

# Locality Research Guide: Native American Families in Indian Territory (Oklahoma), c. 1830–1880

## Jurisdictional Context

The region now called Oklahoma was designated **Indian Territory** in the mid-1800s — it did not become a U.S. state until **1907**. It had no county-level civil infrastructure during the target period, which means **no civil vital registration, no county courthouses, and no standard U.S. state record series** exist for this era. Instead, records were created by three overlapping authorities:

1. **The tribal nations themselves** — each of the Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek/Muscogee, Seminole) maintained their own governments, courts, and citizenship rolls.
2. **The U.S. federal government** — through the Office of Indian Affairs / Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA), which administered annuity payments, treaty compliance, land allotment, and schools.
3. **Christian missionaries** — Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian missions operating in the Territory from the 1820s onward.

Research strategy must work all three tracks. Records created under Oklahoma statehood (post-1907) are a separate layer that can supplement but cannot substitute for the pre-statehood record types below.

> **Boundary note:** Records filed under "Indian Territory" and later under "Oklahoma" are a continuous research trail. After 1890 a portion of the region was reorganized as Oklahoma Territory (distinct from the tribal-governed Indian Territory), so some late-period records appear under both place names. Search both.

---

## Record Types, Coverage & Access

### 1. 🏆 Tribal Enrollment Rolls — *Primary Source*

The single most important record class for this research. Rolls document tribal citizenship, blood quantum, age, sex, and family relationships.

| Roll | Date | Tribes | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Old Settler Cherokee Roll** | 1851 | Cherokee | Indexed online (FamilySearch & Ancestry) |
| **Drennen Roll** | 1852 | Cherokee | Indexed online |
| **Dawes Rolls** | 1898–1914 | All Five Civilized Tribes | The most comprehensive; outside the mid-1800s target period but references earlier enrollment data and is the gateway to allotment files |

**Source:** FamilySearch Wiki article *"Oklahoma Native American Records,"* Tribal Rolls section.

**FamilySearch Collection:** *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959* (Collection ID: 1987432) — 567,890 records with 283,945 images; covers your target window.
🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432

**Digitized Volume:** Image group **002298871** — *Tribal Enrollment Cards, Indian Territory, 1840–1870* — 940 images, **99% name-indexed** and **full-text searchable** in English. Reachable via FamilySearch record search and full-text search.

**Repositories:** Original Dawes Rolls and related allotment files are held at the **National Archives, Fort Worth** (NARA Southwest Region). Earlier rolls are available via FamilySearch and Ancestry.

---

### 2. BIA Agency Records (Annuity Rolls, Correspondence, School Records)

The Bureau of Indian Affairs maintained:
- **Annuity payment rolls** — lists of tribal members who received treaty-mandated payments; function as census substitutes
- **Agency correspondence** — letters naming individuals, families, disputes, and removals
- **School/boarding school records** — enrollment lists and records of Native children attending mission and government schools

**Access:** Physical originals held at **NARA (National Archives)**. FamilySearch has microfilmed portions. **Fold3** (fold3.com) is a key online repository for military-adjacent BIA records.

**Source:** FamilySearch Wiki article *"Oklahoma Native American Records,"* Missionary and Agency Records section.

---

### 3. Land Records

**FamilySearch Collection:** *Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907* (Collection ID: 1541872) — 234,567 records with 117,283 images.
🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872

Covers the allotment era and earlier land transactions. For the mid-1800s, tribal land assignments and removal-era patents are the most relevant.

**Also consult:** BLM General Land Office (GLO) records at **glorecords.blm.gov** for federal patents issued to tribal members.

---

### 4. Missionary & Church Records

**FamilySearch Collection:** *Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965* (Collection ID: 1654321) — 123,456 records with 61,728 images.
🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321

Covers your target period (1820 start). Includes Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian mission registers — baptisms, marriages, burials, and school enrollment lists. Often the only source for vital events in this period.

**⚠️ Coverage note:** Not all mission registers have been digitized or indexed. Undigitized registers may be held at denominational archives (e.g., American Baptist Historical Society, Presbyterian Historical Society).

---

### 5. Federal Census Records (Indian Territory Schedules)

Indian Territory was enumerated in **special federal Indian census schedules**, separate from the standard population schedules:
- 1860, 1870, 1880 federal censuses included Indian Territory enumerations
- These schedules are available on **Ancestry.com** (curated link confirmed for this place)

**⚠️ Important:** Indian Territory residents were often enumerated differently than non-Native U.S. residents. Some tribal members appear only in tribal rolls, not census schedules.

---

### 6. Treaty Documents

Treaties between individual tribes and the U.S. government (e.g., the Removal Treaties of the 1830s, post–Civil War reconstruction treaties of 1866) are not themselves genealogical records but **define which rolls were created and who was entitled to enrollment**. They are essential context for understanding gaps.

**Access:** Full text at the **National Archives** and the **Oklahoma Historical Society** (okhistory.org). The OHS holds significant tribal records not available elsewhere.

---

### 7. Military Records

Tribal members served in both Union and Confederate forces during the Civil War. Service records and pension files are at **NARA** and accessible via **Fold3** (fold3.com — confirmed curated link for this place).

---

### 8. Vital Records

**⚠️ Absence warning:** Oklahoma did not require statewide civil vital registration until **1908**, after statehood. **No civil birth, marriage, or death certificates exist for the mid-1800s target period.** Church records and tribal rolls are the functional substitutes.

The FamilySearch collection *Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995* (ID: 2134789) begins at 1890 — useful only for the very tail end of your research window, and only after Indian Territory began transitioning toward statehood-era administration.

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **NARA Fort Worth** (Southwest Region) | Dawes Rolls, allotment files, BIA agency records, Five Civilized Tribes records | Physical + online via Ancestry/FamilySearch |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Indian Censuses & Rolls, Land Records, Mission Records, Tribal Enrollment Cards | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Dawes Rolls, federal census Indian schedules, land records | Subscription |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (okhistory.org) | Tribal records, territorial newspapers, OHS digital collections | Online + physical |
| **Fold3.com** | BIA records, military service records | Subscription |
| **National Archives (Washington, D.C.)** | Treaty documents, original correspondence | Physical + some online |
| **Denominational archives** | Undigitized mission registers | Physical; contact directly |

---

## Research Quirks & Critical Warnings

- **Tribe must be identified first.** The five major tribes had separate governments and separate rolls. A "Cherokee" record and a "Creek" record are held in different series. Identify tribal affiliation before searching.
- **Dawes Rolls postdate the mid-1800s** (1898–1914) but are the indispensable gateway — they reference earlier citizenship and cite prior enrollment rolls. Work backward from Dawes to the 1851/1852 rolls.
- **Freedmen are enrolled separately.** Formerly enslaved people held by tribal members were granted tribal citizenship after 1866 and have their own Dawes Freedmen rolls — search these if your ancestor may be in this category.
- **Blood quantum and "intermarried white" categories** appear on many rolls. Ancestors listed as "intermarried white" or with low blood quantum fractions may be harder to trace through tribal sources and may also appear in standard U.S. county records from adjacent states.
- **No FamilySearch wiki place page exists for Indian Territory.** The tool returned only a generic fallback for both "Indian Territory" and "Oklahoma" — consult the specific wiki article **"Oklahoma Native American Records"** (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Oklahoma_Native_American_Records) directly for ongoing guidance.
- **Post–Civil War gap:** Many tribal records were destroyed during the Civil War as tribes split between Union and Confederate allegiances. The 1866 reconstruction treaties and post-war rolls are especially important for families in this transition period.

---

## Suggested Research Sequence

1. **Identify the tribe** from family tradition, earlier records, or census entries from adjacent states before removal.
2. **Search the *Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls* collection** (FamilySearch ID 1987432) for enrollment records in the target period.
3. **Browse image group 002298871** (Tribal Enrollment Cards, 1840–1870) — 99% indexed, full-text searchable.
4. **Work from the Dawes Rolls backward** to pre-1898 rolls (Old Settler Cherokee 1851, Drennen 1852, and similar).
5. **Search BIA annuity rolls** via NARA Fort Worth or Fold3 for annuity payment records.
6. **Check the Oklahoma Mission and Church Records** (FamilySearch ID 1654321) for baptismal and marriage entries in the 1820–1880 window.
7. **Consult the Oklahoma Historical Society** for tribal-specific records not yet digitized.

---

# Pennsylvania Quaker Communities — Late 1700s Research Guide
**Place:** Pennsylvania, United States | **Period:** c. 1760–1800 | **Focus:** Society of Friends (Quaker) meeting records and supporting sources

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Pennsylvania was founded in 1682 by William Penn as a Quaker haven, and by the late 1700s the **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting** served as the umbrella body for virtually all Pennsylvania Friends. Quaker populations were densest in **Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware counties**, with additional meetings growing in Lancaster, York, and the western counties as the century closed.

**Critical fact:** Pennsylvania had no statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths until **1906**. For the late 1700s, Quaker meeting records are often the *only* source for vital events among Friends families — they are not a supplement to civil records; they *are* the record.

Several counties were also formed during this period (Montgomery 1784, Delaware 1789, Dauphin 1785, etc.), so records may be split between a newly formed county and its parent. Always identify the county of formation date before searching.

---

## Primary Record Types

### 1. 🏆 Quaker Monthly Meeting Records *(Highest Priority)*

Monthly meetings were the record-keeping unit of Quaker life. They documented:

- **Births, marriages, and deaths** — the primary vital record substitute
- **Certificates of removal** — issued when a member relocated to another meeting; these link families across geography and are essential for tracking migration
- **Disownments** — proceedings for marrying outside the meeting, bearing arms, swearing loyalty oaths, or other offenses; they name family members and circumstances in detail
- **Disciplinary minutes** — reinstatements, acknowledgments, and appeals

**Key Pennsylvania meetings, late 1700s:**
- *Philadelphia Monthly Meeting* and its preparatives (one of the oldest and largest)
- *Philadelphia Yearly Meeting* — oversight body; minutes name individuals in cases that rose above the monthly level
- Chester Co.: Goshen, Uwchlan, Caln, Bradford, New Garden, London Grove meetings
- Bucks Co.: Falls, Middletown, Buckingham, Wrightstown, Richland meetings
- Lancaster/York: Sadsbury, Little Britain, Fawn, Warrington meetings
- Delaware Co.: Radnor, Haverford, Darby meetings

**Access — physical repositories (most records are here):**

| Repository | Holdings | How to Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College** | Original minutes, registers, removal certificates for most PA meetings | In-person; finding aids at swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library |
| **Haverford College Quaker & Special Collections** | Philadelphia Yearly Meeting records; additional monthly meeting records | In-person and partial online; haverford.edu/quaker-collections |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (Philadelphia)** | Supplementary genealogical manuscripts | In-person; hsp.org |

**Access — FamilySearch (digitized):**
- ***Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes, 1750–1800*** — confirmed digitized volume (imageGroupNumber **001188322**, 145 images). ⚠️ **Browse-only: 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Must be examined image by image. Use Hinshaw (below) first to pinpoint the meeting and approximate date before browsing.
- **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** (Collection ID **1401638**) — 1,245,830 records, 607,220 images, name-indexed. Includes Quaker entries alongside other denominations. **Start here for a searchable online approach.**
- Most individual monthly meeting microfilms are in the **FamilySearch Catalog** (not surfacing in the volume search tool) — search the catalog by county name or meeting name to locate specific film rolls.

---

### 2. 🏆 Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*

**William Wade Hinshaw, *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, 6 volumes** — published abstracts of births, marriages, deaths, and disownments extracted from Pennsylvania and other state meeting records. This is the **recommended first stop** for any Quaker family search: it indexes people across multiple meetings in one place. Available at major genealogical libraries and through some subscription platforms.

---

### 3. Pennsylvania Probate Records, 1683–1994
**FamilySearch Collection ID 1999196** | 893,214 records | 446,607 images | Indexed + images

Wills and estate inventories name family members, witnesses, executors, and neighbors — critical for the late 1700s when no death certificates existed. Quakers frequently named fellow meeting members as executors or legatees, making these records valuable for cluster research.

---

### 4. Pennsylvania Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 & Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900

| Collection | ID | Records | Images | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 | 1571957 | 312,045 | 156,023 | Indexed + images |
| Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 | 1502909 | 198,765 | **None** | Index only — no image backup |

Both overlap the target period. Quaker marriages may appear, but the meeting records themselves are more complete for Friends.

---

### 5. Land and Tax Records *(Vital Census Substitutes)*

Pennsylvania **county tax lists** (1769 onward) name heads of household and are indispensable substitutes for the years before the **1790 federal census** (the first enumeration). The **Pennsylvania Provincial Tax Lists** and **Supply Tax Lists** are held at the Pennsylvania State Archives and partially available on FamilySearch. **Land warrants and patents** (Record Group 17) are also at the State Archives — useful for tracking Quaker families' land movements.

---

### 6. Federal Census, 1790

The first federal census names heads of household only. Useful to confirm county of residence. Available free on FamilySearch and via Ancestry.

---

### 7. Revolutionary War–Era Records *(Unique Quaker Angle)*

Because Quakers were pacifists, the Revolutionary period created a distinctive paper trail:
- **Disownment proceedings** for Friends who bore arms, paid war taxes, or swore loyalty oaths — recorded in monthly meeting minutes
- **Pennsylvania militia musters and tax exemption lists** — document Quakers who paid fines in lieu of service
- **Loyalist records** — a minority of Friends were disowned for Tory sympathies; some appear in British records
- **Fold3.com** holds many Pennsylvania Revolutionary War records

---

### 8. Newspapers

**Newspapers.com** holds some late-18th-century Pennsylvania papers (*Pennsylvania Gazette*, *Pennsylvania Packet*, *Pennsylvania Journal*). Obituaries and occasional notices of removal appeared, though Quakers generally avoided public announcements.

---

## Critical Research Tips

1. **Follow the removal certificate trail.** When a family moved between meetings, they received a "certificate of removal." Search *both* the issuing and the receiving meeting's records — this chain is how you track migration across county and state lines.

2. **Quaker calendar notation.** Until the calendar reform of **September 1752**, Quakers counted March as "1st Month." After 1752, January became 1st Month. Double-check all date conversions around this transition point, and remember that "Quaker style" also avoided day-of-week names (using "First Day" for Sunday, etc.).

3. **Disownments are not dead ends.** A disownment proceeding in the minutes often contains more genealogical detail than a birth or death entry — names, relationships, circumstances, and sometimes an address. Disowned members could also apply for reinstatement, generating additional entries.

4. **Use the meeting hierarchy.** If monthly meeting records are lost or missing, try the **quarterly meeting** minutes, then the **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting** minutes. Cases and individual names can appear at any level.

5. **Hinshaw first, then originals.** Search Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia* before diving into browse-only FamilySearch volumes. Once you find a person's meeting and approximate year, you can navigate the unindexed volumes efficiently.

6. **County formation matters.** If your family was in a county before it was carved out of a parent, records may be filed under the *parent* county's name. For example, Montgomery County records before 1784 are under Philadelphia County; Delaware County records before 1789 are under Chester County.

7. **The FamilySearch browse-only volume requires patience.** The *Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes, 1750–1800* (imageGroupNumber 001188322) has 145 images and no name index — plan to read through minutes sequentially for the relevant decade once you have a target meeting and year range.

---

## Digitization / Access Summary

| Record Type | FamilySearch Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Philadelphia YM Minutes 1750–1800 | **Browse-only** (145 images, 0% indexed) | imageGroupNumber 001188322 |
| PA Church & Town Records 1708–1985 | **Indexed + images** (1.2M records) | Collection 1401638 — best searchable starting point |
| PA Probate Records 1683–1994 | **Indexed + images** (893K records) | Collection 1999196 |
| PA Civil Marriages 1677–1950 | **Indexed + images** (312K records) | Collection 1571957 |
| PA Compiled Marriages 1700–1900 | **Index only, no images** | Collection 1502909 |
| Individual monthly meeting originals | **Physical only** at Swarthmore/Haverford | Some microfilmed; search FamilySearch Catalog by county/meeting name |
| Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia* | **Print/library** | Some pages on HathiTrust and subscription platforms |
| County tax lists | **Partial online** (PA State Archives, FamilySearch) | Essential pre-1790 census substitute |

---

## Coverage Gaps to Note

- The `volume_search` returned only **1 digitized volume** covering 1760–1800 — the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting minutes. Most individual monthly meeting microfilms exist in the FamilySearch Catalog but do not surface in the automated volume search; browse the Catalog directly by county or meeting name.
- FamilySearch's curated external links for this period returned only general resources (census, NARA, FindAGrave, Fold3, Newspapers.com) — none Quaker-specific. Quaker-targeted resources route through Swarthmore, Haverford, and Hinshaw.
- The FamilySearch Wiki's dedicated Pennsylvania pages were not retrieved by the place-page tool during this session; consult **familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Church_Records** directly for up-to-date guidance on indexed collections and microfilm availability.

---

**Bottom line:** For late-1700s Pennsylvania Quaker families, your research arc should be: **Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia*** → **FamilySearch Collection 1401638** (Church & Town Records) → **Swarthmore and/or Haverford** for original meeting minutes → **removal certificates** to track migration → **probate and tax records** to fill vital-event gaps. The meeting records themselves are extraordinarily detailed and largely survive — the main challenge is finding which repository holds the specific monthly meeting you need.

---

# Locality Research Guide: Pittsburgh, Allegheny County, Pennsylvania
## Late 1800s Steel Industry Era (c. 1870–1905)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Pittsburgh (established 1758) sits within **Allegheny County** (established 1788) at the confluence of the Allegheny, Monongahela, and Ohio rivers. During the 1870–1905 period it was the undisputed center of American steel production — Carnegie Steel, Jones & Laughlin, and dozens of other firms drew massive waves of immigrant labor (Irish, German, Polish, Slovak, Croatian, Italian) into the city and surrounding mill towns.

**Critical boundary note:** Many steel workers did *not* live in Pittsburgh proper but in adjacent boroughs and townships — **Homestead, Braddock, McKeesport, Carnegie, Duquesne, Munhall** — each of which was a separate municipality with its own local records. Always research at the borough and township level in addition to the city.

**Pennsylvania vital registration gap:** Pennsylvania did **not** require mandatory death registration until **1906** and birth registration was inconsistently enforced before that date. For deaths in the late 1800s, church burial registers, cemetery records, and newspaper death notices are your primary substitutes.

---

## 1. 🏭 Industrial & Occupational Records — The Core Sources

These are the most distinctive sources for the Pittsburgh steel era and the first place to look beyond standard civil records.

### Company Employment Records
- **What they contain:** Employee name, sometimes hire/termination dates, wages, department/trade
- **Survival:** Fragmentary — not all companies kept records, and many were discarded
- **Where held:**
  - **Senator John Heinz History Center** (800 Anderson St., Pittsburgh) — holds the largest collection of Pittsburgh industrial and business records, including Carnegie Steel–era materials. *Physical visit or advance inquiry required.*
  - **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area Archives** (Homestead, PA) — focused on the steel mill towns (Homestead, Braddock, Duquesne); holds union records, mill photographs, oral history transcripts, and some company documents. *Physical only.*
- **Digitization level:** Physical only; not on FamilySearch or Ancestry

### Union Records
- **What they contain:** Membership rolls, dues payments, grievances, strike records
- **Key union:** The Amalgamated Association of Iron, Steel and Tin Workers (AAISW) was the dominant union through the 1892 Homestead Strike; records survive partially
- **Where held:** Heinz History Center; Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg); some records at the **Historical Society of Western Pennsylvania**
- **Tip:** The Homestead Strike of 1892 generated extensive legal records (court filings, depositions, newspaper coverage) that name individual workers — a rich but underused source
- **Digitization level:** Physical only

### State Factory Inspector Reports
- **What they contain:** Annual lists of establishments, number of employees, sometimes accident reports naming injured workers
- **Published:** Pennsylvania Bureau of Industrial Statistics published annual reports from 1872 onward
- **Where held:** Pennsylvania State Archives; Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh; Google Books has some volumes digitized
- **Digitization level:** Partial Google Books digitization — search "Pennsylvania Bureau of Industrial Statistics Annual Report"

---

## 2. 📋 Census Records — Essential Occupational Snapshots

| Census Year | Occupation Data | Notes |
|-------------|----------------|-------|
| 1870 Federal | Yes — occupation listed for all adults | Indexed + images on FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| 1880 Federal | Yes — includes relationship to head, birthplace of parents | Indexed + images; includes **manufacturing/industry schedules** (see below) |
| **1890 Federal** | **DESTROYED** | **Nearly entirely lost in 1921 fire — gap to plan around** |
| 1900 Federal | Yes — includes months employed in prior year | Indexed + images on FamilySearch and Ancestry |

### 1880 Manufacturing/Industry Census Schedules
Separate from the population schedule, the 1880 non-population schedules captured data by factory and mill — products, capital invested, number of employees, wages paid. These do not name individual workers but document the industrial landscape of each township.
- **Where held:** NARA (National Archives at Washington, DC); microfilm available at NARA Philadelphia and some libraries
- **Digitization level:** Microfilm/physical only; not name-indexed; must be browsed by county

---

## 3. 📖 City Directories — Annual Occupational Snapshots

City directories list heads of household by name with occupation and address — effectively an annual census of employed residents.
- **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** holds Pittsburgh city directories from the 1810s onward (the most complete run in the region)
- **FamilySearch** has some Pennsylvania city directory content within the **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** collection (1,245,830 records, 607,220 images) — [Collection 1401638](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638)
- **Ancestry.com** and **Google Books** have digitized various Pittsburgh directories — search "[year] Pittsburgh city directory"
- **Digitization level:** Partial; Carnegie Library holdings are physical; some years are digitized on Ancestry or Google Books

---

## 4. ⛪ Church Records — Critical for Immigrant Steel Workers

Because civil vital registration was weak before 1906, church registers are often the **only** source for births, marriages, and deaths among working-class families. Pittsburgh's ethnic parishes closely tracked their steel-worker communities.

| Ethnic Community | Typical Denomination | Notes |
|-----------------|---------------------|-------|
| Polish | Roman Catholic | Registers in Polish — researcher must read the original |
| Slovak | Roman Catholic, Lutheran | Registers in Slovak/Latin |
| Croatian | Roman Catholic | Registers in Croatian/Latin |
| Italian | Roman Catholic | Registers in Italian/Latin |
| German | Lutheran, Reformed, Catholic | Registers often in German |
| Irish | Roman Catholic | English-language registers |

**FamilySearch collection:** [Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638) — 1,245,830 indexed records, 607,220 images — **indexed + images**

**Where to find unlisted parishes:** The **Diocese of Pittsburgh** archives hold registers for Catholic parishes not yet on FamilySearch. The **Western Pennsylvania Conference of the United Methodist Church** and **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia) hold Protestant registers.

---

## 5. ✍️ Vital Records on FamilySearch

| Collection | Date Range | Records | Access |
|-----------|------------|---------|--------|
| [Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921317) | 1885–1950 | 524,189 records, 312,045 images | Indexed + images |
| [Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957) | 1677–1950 | 312,045 records, 156,023 images | Indexed + images |
| [Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909) | 1700–1900 | 198,765 records | Index only — no images |
| [Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2255317) | **1906–1968** | 4,218,763 records | Indexed + images |

⚠️ **Death certificate gap:** The death certificate collection begins in 1906 — families who died in the 1870s–1900s will not appear. Substitute: church burial registers, cemetery inscriptions (FindAGrave.com), and Pittsburgh newspaper death notices (Newspapers.com).

---

## 6. 🌍 Immigration & Naturalization Records

Pittsburgh's steel boom was fueled by immigration. Naturalization records are among the most genealogically rich sources.

- **Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas** — naturalization declarations and petitions, 1800s–1906; held at the Allegheny County courthouse and partly microfilmed. Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in *any* court of record (federal, state, or local), so also check:
- **U.S. Circuit and District Court records** at **NARA — Mid-Atlantic Region (Philadelphia)** — 900 Market St., Philadelphia; pre-1906 federal naturalizations
- **FamilySearch** has some Pennsylvania naturalization records within [Church and Town Records, 1708–1985](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638)
- **Passenger lists:** Indexed on Ancestry (U.S. Federal Census Collection) and FamilySearch; most Pittsburgh immigrants arrived via New York, Baltimore, or Philadelphia ports

---

## 7. 🏛️ Probate Records

[Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1999196) — **893,214 indexed records, 446,607 images** — indexed + images on FamilySearch. Covers wills, inventories, and estate administrations. Even modest working-class estates sometimes went through probate when property was at stake.

---

## 8. 📰 Newspapers — Labor Events & Death Notices

Pittsburgh newspapers documented strikes, accidents, labor disputes, and deaths in detail.

- **Newspapers.com** — the primary digitized newspaper repository; search Pittsburgh-area papers
- **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** — holds physical/microfilm runs of the *Pittsburgh Gazette*, *Pittsburgh Post*, *Pittsburgh Dispatch*, and ethnic-community papers
- **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, free) — search [chroniclingamerica.loc.gov](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) for Pennsylvania papers

**Key events to search:** Homestead Strike (1892), Johnstown Flood aftermath coverage (1889), mill accident reports, coroner's inquest reports

---

## 9. ⚰️ Cemetery Records

- **FindAGrave.com** — indexed transcriptions and photographs of headstones from Pittsburgh-area cemeteries; searchable by name
- Major cemeteries serving steel workers include Allegheny Cemetery, Homewood Cemetery, St. Mary's Cemetery (Lawrenceville), and numerous ethnic Catholic cemeteries in mill towns

---

## 10. 🎖️ Military Records

Most late-1800s steel workers were too young for the Civil War but may have registered for the draft or served in state militia called during labor unrest. **Fold3.com** holds pension and service records. NARA holds draft records.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What's There | Access |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Heinz History Center** (Pittsburgh) | Company records, immigration records, labor archives | Physical; some finding aids online |
| **Rivers of Steel Archives** (Homestead) | Steel mill records, union records, mill town history | Physical; contact in advance |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** | City directories (1810s+), local history, newspapers | Physical + some digital |
| **Allegheny County Courthouse** | Naturalization records, probate, deeds, court records | Physical |
| **PA State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Factory inspector reports, state records | Physical + some digital |
| **NARA Philadelphia** | Federal naturalizations, census non-pop schedules | Physical |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church records, marriages, probate (see collections above) | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census, passenger lists, city directories | Subscription |

---

## Research Sequence Recommendation

For a Pittsburgh steel-era ancestor, work in this order:

1. **Federal census 1880 and 1900** (occupation, neighbors, birthplace of parents) — substitute 1870 if needed; skip 1890 (destroyed)
2. **City directories** for the years between censuses — tracks movement between mill towns and gives occupation year by year
3. **Church records** — identify the ethnic parish first, then the specific register
4. **Naturalization records** — Allegheny County court first, then NARA Philadelphia
5. **Company and union records** at Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel
6. **Newspaper death notices** for deaths before 1906 in lieu of death certificates
7. **Probate records** on FamilySearch for estate settlements

---

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki — Allegheny County, Pennsylvania Genealogy (Industrial Records and Immigration Records sections); Wikipedia — Pittsburgh; FamilySearch collections search (Pennsylvania, 1870–1905); Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel institutional holdings.*

# 📋 Philadelphia, Pennsylvania — 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic
## Locality Research Guide

**Place:** Philadelphia City / Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania, United States
**Focus period:** 1793 (epidemic: August–November 1793), with flanking years c. 1785–1800
**Jurisdictional note:** Philadelphia City and Philadelphia County were co-terminus from 1682; both existed in 1793. Pennsylvania did **not** have statewide civil vital registration in 1793 — that began only in 1906. Church registers are therefore the **primary** vital-record substitute for this period.

---

## Jurisdictional Context

The epidemic struck Philadelphia while it was still the U.S. capital. Roughly **5,000 of the city's ~50,000 residents died** (≈10%), and thousands more fled to the countryside. Records created in direct response to the outbreak — Committee minutes, hospital registers, named lists of the dead — are the most targeted genealogical sources, but they must be read alongside conventional church, court, and tax records to reconstruct whole families.

---

## 1. Epidemic-Specific Sources ⭐ Start Here

These are the records created *because of* the epidemic and are unlike anything in a normal research year.

### Mathew Carey's Published List of the Dead
- Philadelphia publisher Mathew Carey compiled and published a list of names of those who died during the epidemic. This is one of the most direct name-finding tools for victims.
- **Access:** Widely reprinted; copies held at the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**, the Library Company of Philadelphia, and many genealogical libraries. Some editions are digitized on **Google Books** and **HathiTrust**. The FamilySearch Wiki's *Philadelphia Genealogy* article (section: "Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793") cites this as a primary source.

### Committee of the Citizens — Minutes and Reports
- A citizens' committee, chaired by Mayor Matthew Clarkson, organized relief during the outbreak and kept detailed minutes, lists of aid recipients, and death tallies.
- **Access:** Manuscript held at the **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)**. Some records have been microfilmed. Not yet fully digitized or indexed online — physical visit or HSP's digital image order service recommended.

### Bush Hill Hospital Records
- Bush Hill, on the outskirts of the city, served as the fever hospital. Patient and mortality records, if extant, would name individuals treated there.
- **Access:** Surviving records, to the extent they exist, are at **HSP** and/or the **Philadelphia City Archives**. Coverage is fragmentary — treat this as a browse-and-hope source, not a systematic index.

### Benjamin Rush and Other Physician Accounts
- Dr. Benjamin Rush's published accounts (and correspondence) name many patients. Not a formal registry, but useful for prominent families.
- **Access:** Rush papers at the **Library Company of Philadelphia** and **HSP**.

---

## 2. Church Burial Records ⭐ Primary Vital Records Source

Pennsylvania had no civil death registration in 1793. Church burial registers are the nearest equivalent and must be exhausted before concluding a search.

### FamilySearch Collection — Indexed
**Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985**
- Collection ID: 1401638 · 1,245,830 indexed records · 607,220 images
- Covers baptisms, marriages, and burials from dozens of Philadelphia-area congregations
- **Access:** Fully indexed and searchable at [FamilySearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638) — free
- **Tip:** Search this first for any burial in 1793; many Philadelphia churches are included

### Key Philadelphia Churches Active in 1793
Each maintained its own burial register. Some have been separately transcribed or published:

| Church | Denomination | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Christ Church | Anglican/Episcopal | One of the largest; burial register spans this period; some records transcribed |
| Gloria Dei (Old Swedes') | Swedish Lutheran | One of the oldest churches in Philadelphia; burial records cited by the FS Wiki |
| St. Peter's Church | Episcopal | Shared records with Christ Church burial ground |
| Philadelphia Monthly Meeting | Quaker (Friends) | Exceptionally detailed minutes and burial registers |
| Mikveh Israel Congregation | Jewish | Active in 1793; burial register extant |
| St. Mary's / Old St. Joseph's | Catholic | Catholic registers; some on FamilySearch microfilm |
| Zion German Lutheran / St. Michael's | Lutheran | German-speaking community; registers in German |
| First Presbyterian Church | Presbyterian | Register survives |

- **Denominational archives:** Quaker records at **Haverford College** (Quaker & Special Collections) and **Swarthmore College** (Friends Historical Library). Episcopal records at the **Episcopal Diocese of Pennsylvania Archives**. Presbyterian records at the **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia).
- **GSP Indexes:** The **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** has compiled indexes to many Philadelphia church records. Check their *Pennsylvania Genealogical Magazine* and published collections. Some GSP finding aids are at the **HSP**.

---

## 3. Census Records

### 1790 Federal Census (2 years before epidemic)
- Documents every household head in Philadelphia — establishes who was present before the outbreak
- **Access:** Indexed + images on FamilySearch and Ancestry.com; free at FamilySearch

### 1800 Federal Census (7 years after)
- Comparing 1790 and 1800 enumerations can surface families that disappeared (fled, died, dissolved) or reconstituted
- **Access:** Indexed + images on FamilySearch; free

**Note:** There was no 1793 or 1795 state census for Pennsylvania that would directly capture the epidemic year.

---

## 4. Probate Records (Wills & Estates)

The epidemic generated a wave of estate proceedings in late 1793 and into 1794.

### FamilySearch Collection — Indexed
**Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994**
- Collection ID: 1999196 · 893,214 indexed records · 446,607 images
- Philadelphia County wills, inventories, letters of administration, and guardian bonds
- **Access:** Indexed + images at [FamilySearch.org](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1999196) — free
- **Search tip:** Filter to Philadelphia County and 1793–1795; administration bonds (for those who died intestate) often name widow and children

### Philadelphia Orphans' Court
- Guardianship records for children orphaned by the epidemic are in the Orphans' Court files
- **Access:** **Philadelphia City Archives**; some records on FamilySearch microfilm

---

## 5. Marriage Records

### FamilySearch Collections — Indexed
| Collection | ID | Date Range | Records |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 | 1571957 | 1677–1950 | 312,045 |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 | 1502909 | 1700–1900 | 198,765 |

- **Access:** Both searchable at FamilySearch.org — free
- Marriages in the flanking years (1785–1800) establish family units before and after the epidemic

---

## 6. Tax & Assessment Records

Philadelphia city and county tax lists survive from the 1760s onward and are one of the few annual record sources for this period.

- **Philadelphia County Tax Lists (c. 1769–1800+):** List heads of household, property, and sometimes occupation. Comparing 1792 and 1794 lists can reveal who disappeared during the epidemic year.
- **Access:** Some lists published by the **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania**; microfilm at **HSP** and on FamilySearch microfilm (browse-only for many years — not name-indexed); originals at **Philadelphia City Archives**

---

## 7. Land and Deed Records

Estate settlements after epidemic deaths often triggered deed transfers.

- **Philadelphia County Deed Books:** Originals at the **Philadelphia City Archives**; microfilm at FamilySearch (browse-only for this period)
- Search deed grantors/grantees in 1793–1796 for any ancestor of interest

---

## 8. Newspapers ⭐ Underused but Rich

Philadelphia newspapers in 1793 published names of the dead in nearly every issue during August–November. They also carried estate sale notices, removal notices (families leaving the city), and death notices.

**Key Philadelphia papers, 1793:**
- *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser*
- *Claypoole's American Daily Advertiser*
- *General Advertiser* (later the *Aurora*)
- *Federal Gazette*

**Access:**
- **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, free online): [chroniclingamerica.loc.gov](https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) — search "yellow fever" + 1793
- **Newspapers.com** (subscription): broader coverage; keyword-searchable
- **HSP newspaper collection:** physical and microfilm runs of all major Philadelphia papers
- **Readex / America's Historical Newspapers** (library databases): another digitized source; check your local library

---

## 9. City Directories

Philadelphia had printed city directories from 1785 onward. Comparing editions across the epidemic years documents who remained in the city, who relocated, and whose listings vanished.

- **1791, 1793, 1794, 1795, 1796** directories are particularly useful for bracketing the epidemic
- **Access:** Digitized at **HathiTrust**, **Ancestry.com**, and **HSP**; some on FamilySearch microfilm

---

## 10. Military Records (Background/Flanking)

Many Philadelphia men were Revolutionary War veterans with pension files that record family detail.

- **Revolutionary War pension files (post-1818/1832):** NARA, available via **Fold3.com** (subscription) and **FamilySearch microfilm**
- Pension files name wives, children, and often include depositions that post-date the epidemic and can confirm survival or widow status

---

## 11. Find A Grave / Cemetery Databases

- **Find A Grave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)): Many Christ Church, Gloria Dei, and Friends burial ground interments have been transcribed
- **BillionGraves**: supplementary coverage
- **Philly History burial database**: The Philadelphia City Archives maintains records for city-controlled burial grounds

---

## Repository Summary

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** | Committee minutes, Carey list, church records, manuscripts, newspapers | In-person (Philadelphia); some digital scans on request |
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Court records, deed books, tax lists, Orphans' Court, burial databases | In-person; some records online |
| **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** | Church record indexes, published transcriptions | Member publications; library at HSP |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church & Town Records (1401638), Probate (1999196), marriages, census | Free online |
| **American Philosophical Society** | Rush papers, scientific/medical records of the epidemic era | In-person (Philadelphia) |
| **Presbyterian Historical Society** | Presbyterian church registers | In-person (Philadelphia) |
| **Episcopal Diocese of PA Archives** | Episcopal/Anglican parish registers | In-person |
| **Haverford & Swarthmore College Libraries** | Quaker meeting records | In-person; some digitized |
| **Library Company of Philadelphia** | Rush papers, rare books, Carey's pamphlet | In-person |
| **NARA Philadelphia** | Federal court records, naturalization, military | In-person and online via Ancestry/Fold3 |
| **Chronicling America / Newspapers.com** | Philadelphia newspapers 1793 | Free (ChrAm) / subscription (Newspapers.com) |

---

## Research Quirks & Cautions

1. **No civil death registration in 1793.** Pennsylvania's statewide death certificates begin in **1906** — 113 years after the epidemic. The FamilySearch collection "Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968" (4.2M records) does **not** cover 1793.
2. **Church registers are in multiple languages.** German Lutheran and German Reformed records are in German; Swedish (Gloria Dei) records may be in Swedish or Latin in earlier decades. Plan for translation.
3. **The epidemic caused mass flight.** Many families left Philadelphia for the countryside, so the *absence* of a family from city records in autumn 1793 does not mean death — check surrounding county tax lists and church registers (Montgomery, Bucks, Delaware counties) for refugees.
4. **Probate and Orphans' Court are rich but require active searching.** Many epidemic deaths generated rushed estate filings in late 1793 through 1794; search administration bonds as well as wills.
5. **GSP indexes are essential gatekeepers.** The Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania has indexed many Philadelphia church records that are difficult to find otherwise — consult their publications before concluding a church is unsearchable.
6. **City directories are a poor-man's census.** For a year without a census, comparing the 1793 and 1794 directory editions is the closest proxy for who was present before and after the epidemic.
7. **Newspaper death notices are chronologically concentrated.** Virtually all epidemic deaths occurred between August and November 1793 — a narrow window makes newspaper searching highly productive.

---

*Sources: Wikipedia, "Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Philadelphia Genealogy" (sections: Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793, Church Records); FamilySearch collections search for Pennsylvania, 1750–1820; FamilySearch external links search.*

# Locality Guide: Chicago, Cook County, Illinois — Great Chicago Fire (1871)

## Jurisdiction overview
- **Formed:** Cook County organized 1831; Chicago incorporated as a town 1833, chartered as a city 1837
- **County seat / administrative center:** Chicago
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Illinois (state from 1818)
- **Population during period:** No population fixture data available from tools; Chicago was already the Midwest's dominant city by 1870, with a large, diverse immigrant population
- **Economy:** Major Great Lakes port, railroad hub, meatpacking, manufacturing, and commodity trade
- **Ethnic composition:** Heavy Irish, German, Scandinavian, and Bohemian immigrant presence by 1871; also Bohemian, Polish, and other Central/Eastern European communities
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic, German Lutheran, Methodist, Episcopal, Baptist, Presbyterian, and many immigrant-language congregations
- **Key historical events:** **Great Chicago Fire, October 8–10, 1871** — destroyed ~3.3 square miles, killed ~300 people, left 100,000+ homeless, and consumed the Cook County Courthouse with virtually all pre-fire official records

## Boundary changes
- Cook County boundaries were stable through this period; no boundary changes affect the 1871 research window
- Illinois had been a state since 1818; all records for this period fall under Illinois and federal jurisdiction

---

## ⚠️ Critical record loss: The Great Chicago Fire

The fire destroyed the Cook County Courthouse, eliminating virtually all pre-October 1871 official records:

| Destroyed | Notes |
|---|---|
| Deeds and land records | All pre-fire property documentation |
| Birth and death registers | Pre-fire civil vital records gone |
| Court records | Includes naturalization papers and probate files |
| Tax rolls | All pre-fire tax records lost |
| Many church registers | Parish buildings in the burn zone destroyed their registers |

This is the defining research challenge for Chicago genealogy. Every section below addresses survival and substitute strategies.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Pre-fire (before Oct. 1871):** Destroyed in the courthouse fire. Cook County had local registration before 1871, but the records did not survive.
- **Post-fire — FamilySearch indexed + images collections:**
  - **Illinois, Cook County, Birth Certificates, 1871–1940** — 1,450,000 records ✅ indexed + images
  - **Illinois, Cook County, Death Records, 1871–1947** — 980,000 records ✅ indexed + images
  - **Illinois, Cook County, Marriage Records, 1871–1920** — 620,000 records ✅ indexed + images
- **Coverage gap:** Illinois did not require *statewide* vital registration until 1877; the immediate post-fire years (1871–1877) have inconsistent compliance. Cross-check with church records.
- **Substitute for pre-fire:** Church records, 1870 federal census age/birthplace entries, city directories (Newberry Library)

### Church records
- **FamilySearch collection:** Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925 — **285,000 records** ✅ indexed + images
- Parish survival is uneven: churches outside the burn zone retained their registers; those inside lost them. Check each denomination separately.
- **Catholic registers:** The **Archdiocese of Chicago's Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** holds surviving Catholic parish registers. Contact them directly to determine whether a specific parish register survived the fire. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Repositories")*
- **Language note:** German Lutheran, Bohemian Catholic, Polish, and Scandinavian parishes often kept records in the heritage language.
- **Access:** Archdiocese archives (in-person/written request) for originals; FamilySearch for microfilmed collections

### Census records
- **1870 federal census — ⭐ Key pre-fire survival:** The 1870 Chicago schedules survived the fire because the originals were held in Washington, D.C. This is the last pre-fire snapshot of the city's families — use it first. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire")*
- **1860 and 1850 federal censuses:** Survived; available indexed + images on FamilySearch and Ancestry
- **1880 federal census:** Survived; fully intact
- **1890 federal census:** Largely destroyed in a separate Washington, D.C. fire (1921) — a nationwide gap; no Chicago-specific fix exists
- **1900 federal census:** Fully intact; bridges the 1880–1900 gap
- **Mortality schedules (1860, 1870, 1880):** Record individuals who died in the year preceding each census; available on Ancestry and FamilySearch — useful for deaths in the immediate fire period
- **Illinois state census:** No systematic state census comparable to federal schedules for this period

### Probate and court records
- **Pre-fire probate and court records:** Destroyed in the fire
- **FamilySearch volume 009982110 — Reconstructed pre-fire Cook County Court Records (1865–1871):** 38 images — ⚠️ **browse-only** (0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable). These are reconstructed copies, not originals; browse image-by-image.
- **Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at Northeastern Illinois University** holds Cook County court and government records from post-1871 *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Repositories")*
- **Access:** FamilySearch for digitized volume (browse only); IRAD for in-person or mail requests for post-1871 records

### Land records and property re-recordings
- **Pre-fire deeds:** Destroyed. The single most important legislative response was the **Burnt Record Act of 1872**, which required property owners to re-register their deeds with the county. This created a new documentary base from scratch. *(Source: Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire")*
- **FamilySearch volume 009982111 — Post-fire Property Re-recordings (1871–1880):** 112 images — ⚠️ **partially indexed**: 41% name-searchable; the remaining 59% must be browsed image-by-image. Not full-text searchable.
- **Chicago Title and Trust Company records:** Insurance company records that document pre-fire property ownership; now held at the **Chicago History Museum** — the most important substitute source for pre-fire property. *(Source: Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Record Loss and the Great Fire")*
- **Access:** Chicago History Museum (in-person research room, some by appointment) for insurance records; FamilySearch for volume 009982111

### Military records
- **Civil War (1861–1865):** Many Chicago men served; service records and pension files are at NARA and searchable on **Fold3** (curated external link for Cook County)
- **1863 Civil War draft registrations:** Cook County men listed in the Consolidated List; available on Ancestry and FamilySearch
- **Access:** NARA (in-person or mail); Fold3 (online, subscription)

### Cemetery records
- **FindAGrave:** Curated external link for Cook County; covers major Chicago cemeteries including Graceland, Rosehill, Oak Woods, and St. Boniface
- **Sexton records:** Physical burial registers held by individual cemetery offices or at the Chicago History Museum
- **Research value:** Cemetery records often predate surviving vital records and can substitute for pre-fire death documentation

### Newspaper records
- **Newspapers.com:** Curated external link for Cook County; includes historical Chicago newspapers, especially the Chicago Tribune
- **Chicago Tribune, October–November 1871:** Published extensive survivor lists, relief rolls, and property-loss notices in the days and weeks after the fire — a unique primary source for fire-affected families
- **Newberry Library:** Holds Chicago city directories from the 1840s onward and newspaper collections. City directories **survived the fire** (held off-site) and are the primary tool for tracking individuals year-by-year before and after 1871. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Record Loss and the Great Fire")*
- **Access:** Newspapers.com (subscription); Newberry Library (in-person)

### Immigration and naturalization records
- **Passenger lists:** Available at NARA; indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch. Chicago immigrants mainly arrived via New York (Castle Garden / Ellis Island) or Baltimore and came west by rail; some Irish and Scots via Quebec
- **Pre-fire naturalization papers:** Destroyed with the courthouse. Use the **1870 census citizenship column** as a partial substitute — it records whether a foreign-born man had filed first papers or completed naturalization
- **Post-fire naturalizations:** Filed anew after 1871; held at IRAD and FamilySearch
- **Content note:** Passenger manifests record every person aboard, including infants and young children — examine full manifests for whole family units

### Tax records
- **Pre-fire tax rolls:** Destroyed
- **Post-fire:** New tax rolls created from 1872 onward; held at the Cook County offices and Illinois State Archives
- **Research value:** Establishes presence and property value in years between censuses

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories
| Repository | Cook County holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Birth Certificates 1871–1940 (1.45M); Death Records 1871–1947 (980K); Marriage Records 1871–1920 (620K); Church Records 1833–1925 (285K); reconstructed court records 1865–1871 (38 images, browse-only); property re-recordings 1871–1880 (112 images, 41% indexed) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census; Civil War draft; city directories; Ancestry military collections | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Civil War service records; pension files; military records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Chicago historical newspapers including Chicago Tribune | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Chicago cemetery records | Free |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Passenger lists; federal court records; military service and pension files | Free finding aids; fees for copies |

### Physical repositories
| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Chicago History Museum** | Chicago Title & Trust insurance records (pre-fire property substitute); photographs; fire-era personal papers and manuscripts | In-person research room; some by appointment |
| **Newberry Library** | City directories from 1840s onward; maps; manuscript collections; ethnic society records | In-person; some digitized finding aids |
| **IRAD — Northeastern Illinois University** | Cook County post-1871 court and government records | In-person / mail request |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago — Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers (surviving pre- and post-fire) | In-person / written request |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Original vital records from 1871 onward | In-person or certified copy request |
| **Illinois State Archives (Springfield)** | Statewide records; some Cook County post-fire materials | In-person / mail; partial online |
| **NARA — Great Lakes Region (Chicago)** | Federal records; post-fire naturalization files; federal court records | In-person |

---

## Records NOT online
- **Chicago Title and Trust Company insurance records** — held physically at the Chicago History Museum; not in any online database
- **Archdiocese of Chicago original Catholic parish registers** — require direct contact with the Bernardin Archives
- **Most pre-fire Cook County probate records** — destroyed; no online substitute
- **Original sexton burial registers** — held by individual cemetery offices
- **Post-1871 Cook County court records at IRAD** — many not yet digitized; require in-person or mail requests

---

## Known record losses

### Great Chicago Fire, October 8–10, 1871
Destroyed at the Cook County Courthouse: all pre-fire deeds, civil vital records, court files (including naturalizations), probate records, and tax rolls. Many church registers in the burn zone also lost.

### Substitute source map

| Destroyed record | Best substitute |
|---|---|
| Pre-fire deeds | 1872 Burnt Record Act re-recordings (FamilySearch vol. 009982111); Chicago Title & Trust records at Chicago History Museum |
| Pre-fire vital records | Church registers from surviving parishes; 1870 federal census; city directories (Newberry Library) |
| Pre-fire probate | Reconstructed court records (FamilySearch vol. 009982110, browse-only); newspaper notices |
| Pre-fire naturalizations | 1870 census citizenship column; post-fire re-filed declarations; passenger lists showing arrival |
| Pre-fire tax rolls | City directories; post-fire land tract books |

### 1890 federal census gap
The 1890 census was largely destroyed in a separate Washington D.C. fire (1921) — a nationwide loss. The 1880 and 1900 censuses must bracket this gap for all Chicago families.

---

## Research tips

1. **Begin with the 1870 federal census.** It is the last pre-fire record of Chicago households and survived because the originals were in Washington. Document every household member, neighbor, birthplace, and citizenship column entry before moving to other sources.

2. **Treat the Burnt Record Act re-recordings as your primary land source.** FamilySearch volume 009982111 (1871–1880, 112 images) is 41% name-indexed — search the index first, then browse the remaining 59% image-by-image if needed.

3. **The Chicago History Museum's Chicago Title & Trust collection is irreplaceable** for pre-fire property. If your ancestor owned property before October 1871, plan an in-person or contact visit to the CHM research room.

4. **City directories at the Newberry Library survived the fire** (they were held off-site) and are the most powerful year-by-year tracking tool for the pre- and post-fire period. They record name, address, and occupation annually.

5. **Contact the Bernardin Archives before assuming Catholic registers are lost.** Parish survival depends entirely on whether the building was in the burn zone. The Archdiocese can tell you which registers survived for a specific parish.

6. **For immigrant ancestors, anchor on the 1870 census and then trace backward via passenger lists** (arriving through New York or Baltimore). The 1870 citizenship column partially replaces destroyed naturalization papers.

7. **Search Chicago Tribune issues from October–November 1871** on Newspapers.com. Survivor lists, relief fund rolls, and property-loss notices published in the immediate aftermath name thousands of affected residents and can place a family in a specific neighborhood.

8. **The reconstructed pre-fire court records** (FamilySearch volume 009982110, 1865–1871, 38 images) are browse-only with no name index — budget time to go through them image-by-image if you need pre-fire court documentation.

9. **Post-fire vital registration was inconsistent before 1877** (when Illinois mandated statewide registration). Always cross-check FamilySearch birth/death certificates with church records from the 1871–1877 window.

10. **Mortality schedules for 1870** (the year before the fire) capture deaths from June 1869–June 1870 — useful for deaths just before the disaster. The 1880 mortality schedule covers June 1879–June 1880.

---
