# Locality Guide: Chicago, Cook County, Illinois — Great Chicago Fire (1871)

## Jurisdiction overview
| | |
|---|---|
| **Formed** | Cook County organized 1831; Chicago incorporated as town 1833, city 1837 |
| **County seat** | Chicago |
| **Parent jurisdiction** | Illinois (state from 1818) |
| **Key event** | Great Chicago Fire, October 8–10, 1871 — ~300 killed, 3.3 sq. miles destroyed, 100,000+ homeless; Cook County Courthouse consumed with virtually all pre-fire official records |

---

## ⚠️ The defining research challenge: Record destruction in the Great Fire

The Cook County Courthouse held the city's official record archive. The fire eliminated:

| What was destroyed | Impact |
|---|---|
| Deeds and land records | All pre-fire property documentation gone |
| Birth and death registers | Pre-fire civil vital records gone |
| Court records (naturalizations, probate) | No pre-fire citizenship or estate files |
| Tax rolls | No pre-fire property assessment records |
| Many church registers | Parishes in the burn zone lost registers |

*(Sources: Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire")*

**Every strategy below addresses the fire's aftermath.** The 1870 federal census is the indispensable anchor — it captured Chicago's families just one year before the disaster and survived because the originals were held in Washington, D.C.

---

## Available record types

### Vital records (civil registration)
- **Pre-fire:** Destroyed. Civil registration existed locally before 1871 but did not survive.
- **Post-fire — FamilySearch (indexed + images):**
  - **Illinois, Cook County, Birth Certificates, 1871–1940** — 1,450,000 records ✅
  - **Illinois, Cook County, Death Records, 1871–1947** — 980,000 records ✅
  - **Illinois, Cook County, Marriage Records, 1871–1920** — 620,000 records ✅
- **Coverage gap:** Illinois did not mandate *statewide* vital registration until 1877. The 1871–1877 window has inconsistent compliance — always cross-check with church records.
- **Substitutes for pre-fire births/deaths:** Church registers, 1870 census age/birthplace entries, city directories (Newberry Library)

### Church records
- **FamilySearch:** *Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925* — **285,000 records** ✅ indexed + images
- **Parish survival is uneven.** Churches outside the burn zone kept their registers; those inside the burn zone lost them. This must be checked denomination-by-denomination and parish-by-parish.
- **Catholic originals:** The **Archdiocese of Chicago's Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** holds surviving Catholic parish registers. Contact them to determine whether a specific parish register survived. *(FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Repositories")*
- **Language note:** German Lutheran, Bohemian Catholic, Polish, and Scandinavian immigrant parishes often recorded in the heritage language.

### Census records
| Census year | Status | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **1870** ⭐ | **Survived the fire** (originals in Washington) — last pre-fire household record | FamilySearch / Ancestry, indexed + images |
| 1860 | Survived | FamilySearch / Ancestry, indexed + images |
| 1850 | Survived | FamilySearch / Ancestry, indexed + images |
| 1880 | Survived | FamilySearch / Ancestry, indexed + images |
| **1890** | **Largely destroyed** (separate 1921 Washington D.C. fire) — nationwide gap | No recovery; use 1880 + 1900 to bracket |
| 1900 | Survived | FamilySearch / Ancestry, indexed + images |
| Mortality schedules 1860, 1870, 1880 | Survived | Ancestry / FamilySearch — names individuals who died in the year preceding the census |

**No comparable Illinois state census** was conducted during this period.

### Probate and court records
- **Pre-fire:** Destroyed in the courthouse fire.
- **FamilySearch volume 009982110 — Reconstructed pre-fire Cook County Court Records, 1865–1871:** 38 images — ⚠️ **browse-only** (0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable). Reconstructed copies, not originals. Browse image-by-image; budget time accordingly.
- **Post-1871:** Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD) at Northeastern Illinois University holds Cook County court and government records. Many not yet digitized — in-person or mail request required.

### Land records and property re-recordings
- **Pre-fire deeds:** Destroyed entirely.
- **Burnt Record Act (1872):** Illinois law requiring property owners to re-register deeds with the county — the legislative response to the courthouse destruction. *(Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire")*
- **FamilySearch volume 009982111 — Post-fire Property Re-recordings, 1871–1880:** 112 images — ⚠️ **partially indexed**: 41% name-searchable; the remaining 59% must be browsed image-by-image. Not full-text searchable.
- **Chicago Title and Trust Company records** at the **Chicago History Museum:** Insurance company records documenting pre-fire property ownership — the most important substitute source for pre-fire property. **Not available online; in-person visit or research request required.** *(Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire"; FamilySearch Wiki, "Record Loss and the Great Fire")*

### Military records
- **Civil War (1861–1865):** Service records and pension files at NARA; searchable on **Fold3** (subscription). 1863 draft registrations (Consolidated List) on Ancestry and FamilySearch.

### Cemetery records
- **FindAGrave:** Curated external link for Cook County; covers major Chicago cemeteries (Graceland, Rosehill, Oak Woods, St. Boniface, and others).
- **Sexton burial records:** Physical books held by individual cemetery offices or at the Chicago History Museum. Can substitute for pre-fire death documentation.

### Newspaper records
- **Newspapers.com:** Curated external link for Cook County; includes the Chicago Tribune and other historical Chicago papers.
- **Chicago Tribune, October–November 1871:** Published survivor lists, relief fund rolls, and property-loss notices in the immediate aftermath — a unique primary source for fire-affected families.
- **Newberry Library city directories from the 1840s onward:** These **survived the fire** (held off-site). The single best year-by-year tracking tool for pre- and post-fire Chicago residents; records name, address, and occupation annually. *(FamilySearch Wiki, "Record Loss and the Great Fire")*

### Immigration and naturalization records
- **Passenger lists:** NARA; indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch. Chicago immigrants arrived mainly through New York (Castle Garden / later Ellis Island) or Baltimore, then traveled west by rail. Some Irish and Scots via Quebec.
- **Pre-fire naturalization papers:** Destroyed with the courthouse. Use the **1870 census citizenship column** as a partial substitute — it records whether a foreign-born man had filed first papers or completed naturalization.
- **Post-fire naturalizations:** Filed anew after 1871; held at IRAD and FamilySearch.
- **Passenger manifests** record every person aboard including infants — examine full manifests for whole family units.

### Tax records
- Pre-fire: Destroyed. Post-fire rolls from 1872 onward held at Cook County and the Illinois State Archives.
- Useful for establishing presence and property value between census years.

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories
| Repository | Cook County holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Birth Certificates 1871–1940 (1.45M); Death Records 1871–1947 (980K); Marriage Records 1871–1920 (620K); Church Records 1833–1925 (285K); reconstructed court records 1865–1871 (38 images, browse-only); property re-recordings 1871–1880 (112 images, 41% indexed) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census; Civil War draft; city directories; military collections | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Civil War service and pension records; military files | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Chicago Tribune and other historical Chicago newspapers | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Chicago-area cemetery records | Free |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Passenger lists; military service/pension files; federal court records | Free finding aids; fees for copies |

### Physical repositories
| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Chicago History Museum** | Chicago Title & Trust insurance records (pre-fire property substitute); fire-era photographs, manuscripts, personal papers | In-person research room; some by appointment |
| **Newberry Library** | City directories from 1840s onward; maps; manuscript collections; ethnic society records | In-person; some online finding aids |
| **IRAD — Northeastern Illinois University** | Cook County post-1871 court and government records | In-person / mail request |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago — Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers (surviving pre- and post-fire) | In-person / written request |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Original vital records from 1871 onward | In-person or certified copy request |
| **Illinois State Archives (Springfield)** | Statewide records; some Cook County post-fire materials | In-person / mail; partial online |
| **NARA — Great Lakes Region (Chicago)** | Federal records; post-fire naturalization files; federal court records | In-person |

---

## Records NOT online
- **Chicago Title and Trust Company insurance records** — Chicago History Museum only; not in any online database
- **Surviving Catholic parish registers** — Archdiocese of Chicago Bernardin Archives; require direct contact
- **Most pre-fire Cook County probate records** — destroyed; no online substitute
- **Original sexton burial registers** — individual cemetery offices
- **Post-1871 Cook County court records at IRAD** — largely not digitized; in-person or mail only

---

## Known record losses and substitutes

| Destroyed record | Best substitute |
|---|---|
| Pre-fire deeds | 1872 Burnt Record Act re-recordings (FamilySearch vol. 009982111, 41% indexed); Chicago Title & Trust records at Chicago History Museum |
| Pre-fire vital records | Church registers from surviving parishes; 1870 federal census; Newberry Library city directories |
| Pre-fire probate | Reconstructed court records (FamilySearch vol. 009982110, browse-only); newspaper notices |
| Pre-fire naturalizations | 1870 census citizenship column; post-fire re-filed declarations; passenger lists (arrival evidence) |
| Pre-fire tax rolls | City directories; post-fire land records |
| 1890 federal census (nationwide) | 1880 and 1900 federal censuses bracket the gap; city directories for 1890s; state tax records |

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the 1870 federal census — always.** It is the last pre-fire household record for Chicago families and survived because originals were in Washington. Document every household member, age, birthplace, neighbor, and citizenship column entry before moving on.

2. **The Burnt Record Act re-recordings (FamilySearch vol. 009982111) are your first land record stop.** Search the 41% that is name-indexed first, then browse the remaining 59% image-by-image if needed.

3. **The Chicago History Museum's Chicago Title & Trust collection is irreplaceable** for pre-fire property. If your ancestor owned property before October 1871, plan an in-person visit or research inquiry to the CHM.

4. **Newberry Library city directories survived the fire** (held off-site). They provide year-by-year address, occupation, and name confirmation — often the only annual tracking tool across the entire pre- and post-fire period.

5. **Contact the Bernardin Archives before assuming a Catholic register is lost.** Survival depends entirely on whether the parish building was in the burn zone. The Archdiocese can confirm which specific registers survived.

6. **Search Chicago Tribune issues from October–November 1871** on Newspapers.com. Survivor lists, relief fund rolls, and property-loss notices published in the weeks after the fire name thousands of affected residents and can place a family in a specific ward or neighborhood.

7. **For immigrant ancestors:** Anchor on the 1870 census and trace backward via passenger lists. The 1870 citizenship column partially replaces destroyed naturalization papers; passenger lists (New York or Baltimore arrival) document the transatlantic journey.

8. **The reconstructed pre-fire court records** (vol. 009982110, 38 images) are browse-only with no name index. Budget time to go through them page-by-page if you need pre-fire court documentation.

9. **Post-fire vital registration was inconsistent before 1877** (when statewide registration began). Always cross-check FamilySearch birth and death certificates from 1871–1877 against church records.

10. **Mortality schedules for 1870** capture deaths from June 1869–June 1870 — useful for documenting deaths in the year just before the disaster. The 1880 mortality schedule covers June 1879–June 1880 and helps establish post-fire family losses.

---

This guide is grounded in four data sources: the **FamilySearch collections index** (4 indexed collections totaling ~3.3M records for Cook County), **two digitized FamilySearch volumes** (reconstructed court records and property re-recordings), **Wikipedia** ("Great Chicago Fire"), and the **FamilySearch Research Wiki** ("Cook County, Illinois Genealogy — Record Loss and the Great Fire" and "Repositories"). The Illinois wiki place pages returned generic content for all four sections; Cook County–specific guidance came from the wiki search results and the Wikipedia article.

# Locality Guide: Chicago, Cook County, Illinois — Great Chicago Fire (1871)

## Jurisdiction overview
- **Formed:** Cook County organized 1831; Chicago incorporated as town 1833, city 1837
- **County seat:** Chicago
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Illinois (state from 1818)
- **Key historical event:** Great Chicago Fire, October 8–10, 1871 — destroyed ~3.3 sq mi, killed ~300, left 100,000+ homeless; destroyed Cook County Courthouse with virtually all pre-fire official records

## ⚠️ Critical record loss: The Great Chicago Fire
Destroyed at the Cook County Courthouse: all pre-fire deeds, civil vital records, court files (including naturalizations), probate records, and tax rolls. Many church registers in the burn zone also lost.

## Available record types

### Vital records
- Pre-fire: destroyed. Post-fire FamilySearch collections (indexed + images):
  - Birth Certificates 1871–1940: 1,450,000 records
  - Death Records 1871–1947: 980,000 records
  - Marriage Records 1871–1920: 620,000 records
- Illinois statewide registration not required until 1877; 1871–1877 compliance inconsistent

### Church records
- FamilySearch: Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925 — 285,000 records (indexed + images)
- Catholic originals: Archdiocese of Chicago, Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives
- Parish survival depends on burn-zone location

### Census records
- 1870 (survived — originals in Washington D.C.), 1860, 1850, 1880, 1900: indexed + images on FamilySearch/Ancestry
- 1890: largely destroyed nationwide (separate 1921 fire)
- Mortality schedules 1860, 1870, 1880 available

### Court and probate records
- Pre-fire: destroyed. FamilySearch vol. 009982110 (38 images, browse-only): reconstructed pre-fire court records 1865–1871
- Post-1871: IRAD at Northeastern Illinois University

### Land records
- Pre-fire deeds: destroyed. Burnt Record Act 1872 re-recordings: FamilySearch vol. 009982111 (112 images, 41% indexed)
- Chicago Title & Trust insurance records at Chicago History Museum (in-person only)

### Military records
- Civil War service/pension: NARA; Fold3 (subscription)
- 1863 draft registrations: Ancestry/FamilySearch

### Cemetery records
- FindAGrave covers major Chicago cemeteries
- Sexton records: individual cemetery offices / Chicago History Museum

### Newspaper records
- Newspapers.com; Newberry Library
- Chicago Tribune Oct–Nov 1871: survivor lists, relief rolls, property-loss notices
- City directories from 1840s at Newberry Library (survived the fire)

### Immigration and naturalization
- Passenger lists: NARA; Ancestry; FamilySearch
- Pre-fire naturalizations: destroyed; use 1870 census citizenship column + passenger lists
- Post-fire: IRAD and FamilySearch

## Key repositories
- **Chicago History Museum** — Chicago Title & Trust insurance records (in-person)
- **Newberry Library** — city directories 1840s+; maps; manuscripts
- **IRAD at Northeastern Illinois University** — post-1871 Cook County records
- **Archdiocese of Chicago, Bernardin Archives** — Catholic parish registers
- **Cook County Clerk** — vital records 1871+
- **Illinois State Archives** — statewide and some Cook County records
- **NARA Great Lakes Region (Chicago)** — federal records, naturalization

## Key substitute sources for pre-fire records
| Destroyed | Best substitute |
|---|---|
| Pre-fire deeds | 1872 re-recordings (FamilySearch vol. 009982111); Chicago Title & Trust at CHM |
| Pre-fire vital records | Church registers; 1870 census; city directories |
| Pre-fire probate | Reconstructed court records (vol. 009982110, browse-only); newspapers |
| Pre-fire naturalizations | 1870 census citizenship column; passenger lists |
| Pre-fire tax rolls | City directories; post-fire land records |

---

# 📜 Louisiana French Colonial Period (1700–1769)
## Locality Research Guide

---

### Jurisdictional Context

French Louisiana (*La Louisiane*) was an administrative district of **New France**, claimed for France in 1682 and continuously administered from roughly 1699. New Orleans was founded in 1718. The colony was governed by the **Superior Council** (*Conseil Supérieur*) and the Catholic Church was the sole legally recognized religious institution.

**Critical boundary event:** By the **Treaty of Fontainebleau (1762)**, France ceded Louisiana west of the Mississippi to Spain; Spain assumed effective control around **1769**. This means the 1700s straddles two colonial regimes:
- **French administration: ~1699–1769** — records in French
- **Spanish administration: 1769–1800** — records shift to Spanish

If your ancestor lived in Louisiana in the 1770s–1790s, you are already in Spanish colonial records, not French ones. Research both regimes together as one continuous trail.

---

### Record Types & Digitization

#### 🥇 1. Catholic Parish Registers — PRIMARY SOURCE
**The single most important record class for this period.** The Catholic Church was the only legally recognized church in French Louisiana, making parish registers the functional equivalent of civil vital records. The *Code Noir* (Black Code) required baptism of all enslaved persons, so registers document enslaved ancestors alongside free colonists — typically recording given name, enslaver's name, and sometimes origin.

**St. Louis Cathedral** (New Orleans) holds registers from **1720**.

- **FamilySearch collection:** *Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925* (ID: 1612905)
  ~987,654 records · ~493,827 images
  🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905
  **Digitization level: Indexed + images** (both record count and image count present)
- ⚠️ Records are in **French** — proficiency in colonial French and secretary-hand script is essential.

---

#### 2. Superior Council (*Conseil Supérieur*) Records
The colonial civil court. These records include:
- **Land grants (concessions)**
- **Court proceedings**
- **Contracts and commercial transactions**
- **Estate inventories**

**Repository:** Louisiana Historical Center, Old U.S. Mint building, New Orleans. Per the FamilySearch Wiki article *Louisiana Genealogy*, these records have been **partially digitized**. They are in French. No dedicated FamilySearch online collection for these records appeared in the collections search — treat as **physical or partially digitized access** at the repository.

---

#### 3. Notarial Archives — from the 1730s onward
The **New Orleans Notarial Archives** is the oldest continuously operating notarial office in North America. Holdings include:
- Contracts
- Wills
- Marriage settlements
- Property transactions

Starting date: **1730s onward**, so these directly cover the French colonial period.
**Repository:** New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (public access).
Digitization level: **Physical repository access** — full online availability could not be confirmed from tool results.

---

#### 4. Probate Records — late colonial overlap
- **FamilySearch collection:** *Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984* (ID: 2078341)
  ~456,789 records · ~228,394 images
  🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341
  **Digitization level: Indexed + images**
- Covers only the **last 13 years of French colonial administration** (1756–1769). If your ancestor appears in an estate record during this window, this is a searchable online source.

---

#### 5. Land Records & French Concessions
French land grants followed **French civil law, not English common law** — a critical distinction. Sources:
- **American State Papers: Public Lands** — documents French concessions, available in digitized form through several repositories
- **Louisiana State Land Office** — holds additional grant records
- Some later Spanish-era records confirmed French-era land claims, so searching the Spanish period can shed light on earlier grants

---

#### 6. French Colonial Administrative Records (France)
- **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France
- Holdings include: administrative correspondence and **colonial census rolls** (*recensements*)
- These colonial census rolls are distinct from the U.S. federal census (which begins in 1790, after the colonial period)
- Physical access at ANOM; partial digitization is available via their online portal. Specific digitized census years for Louisiana could not be confirmed from tool results.

---

### Records That Do NOT Apply to the French Colonial Period

| Collection | Why It Doesn't Apply |
|---|---|
| *Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957* | Entirely post-period — no colonial coverage |
| *Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964* | Entirely post-period |
| U.S. Federal Census | Begins 1790, after French colonial rule ended |
| Newspapers.com | Minimal Louisiana newspaper coverage in the 1700s |

---

### Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church records 1718–1925; probate records 1756–1984 | Online, indexed |
| **St. Louis Cathedral, New Orleans** | Parish registers from 1720 | Physical + partial online |
| **Louisiana Historical Center** (Old U.S. Mint, New Orleans) | Superior Council records | Physical; partial digitization |
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | Contracts, wills, notarial acts from 1730s | Physical; public access |
| **Louisiana State Land Office** | Land grants and concessions | Physical |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)**, Aix-en-Provence, France | Administrative records, census rolls | Physical + partial online portal |
| **NARA** (archives.gov) | Federal-era records; some post-1803 records trace colonial antecedents | Online + physical |

---

### Research Quirks & Warnings

1. **Language:** All French colonial records are in French, often in 18th-century cursive secretary hand (*écriture*). Spanish-period records (post-1769) are in Spanish. Budget for transcription/translation help.
2. **Dual colonial regimes:** The 1700s spans both French (to ~1769) and Spanish (1769–1800) administration. Don't stop at 1762 — search both regimes as one continuous research trail.
3. **No civil registration:** There was no secular vital registration in this period. The Catholic Church is your only systematic source for births (baptisms), marriages, and deaths (burials).
4. **Enslaved ancestors:** The *Code Noir* required Catholic baptism — parish registers are one of the few record types that document enslaved individuals by name, even if incompletely.
5. **Land law:** French concessions used civil law traditions. Searching under English-common-law assumptions (deeds in a courthouse) will miss French-era land records held in notarial and council records.
6. **No FamilySearch Wiki page found:** All four sections of the Louisiana wiki place page returned generic content, and wiki reads redirected to a generic article. For the most current guidance, consult the FamilySearch Wiki directly: **https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Louisiana_Genealogy**

---

### Coverage Gaps to Note

- **External links search** returned 6 results for Louisiana in this time window, all generic (Ancestry census, FindAGrave, NARA, etc.) — no FamilySearch-curated Louisiana colonial-specific links were returned.
- The Superior Council records and notarial archives are **not currently surfaced as searchable FamilySearch indexed collections** — they require in-person or repository-specific digital access.
- ANOM digitization depth for Louisiana census rolls could not be confirmed from tool results — contact ANOM directly or consult their online catalog (*Archim*).

---

**Starting point recommendation:** Begin with the FamilySearch *Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925* collection (indexed, online) to establish the family in parish registers, then extend into the Superior Council and Notarial Archives for property, legal, and estate context.

Now let me begin the survey. First I'll establish the jurisdictional context with a place search for Pennsylvania.Good — `standardPlace` is **"Pennsylvania, United States"**. The coal mining region spans multiple counties: the anthracite belt (Schuylkill, Luzerne, Carbon, Lackawanna, Northumberland) in the northeast, and the bituminous belt (Cambria, Fayette, Westmoreland, Washington) in the west. Now I'll fire all the survey calls at once.Good — the wiki search surfaced two key sections from the **Pennsylvania Occupational Records** article, and the collections/volume data is in. Now let me read that wiki page and the general Pennsylvania genealogy article.I now have enough from the tools to compile the complete guide. Here it is:

---

# Pennsylvania Coal Mining Towns — Genealogy Research Guide
**Region:** Anthracite belt (Schuylkill, Luzerne, Carbon, Lackawanna, Northumberland counties) · Bituminous belt (Cambria, Fayette, Westmoreland, Washington counties)
**Period:** Late 1800s (c. 1860–1910)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Pennsylvania's coalfields straddle two distinct geological and administrative regions. The two most important county-level quirks for this era:

- **Lackawanna County did not exist before 1878** — it was carved from Luzerne County that year. Any search for Scranton or the northeastern anthracite towns before 1878 must be run under **Luzerne County** records.
- **Pennsylvania had no reliable statewide vital registration until 1906.** Before that date, births, marriages, and deaths were not systematically recorded by the state. Church records and county-level filings are your primary sources for the late 1800s.

---

## Record Types, Access, and Where to Find Them

### 1. 🏭 Coal Mining–Specific Records *(the category most unique to this region)*

These records don't exist elsewhere and can be the richest sources for mining families.

| Record | Coverage | Access Level | Where |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mine Worker Employment Cards** | Schuylkill Co., 1870–1900 · 410 images | **Indexed + full-text searchable** (95% name-indexed) | FamilySearch, image group 002240015 |
| **Mine Accident Reports** | Luzerne Co., 1870–1895 · 287 images | **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text searchable — must be browsed image by image) | FamilySearch, image group 002240016 |
| **Colliery Payroll Ledgers** | Lackawanna Co., 1878–1900 · 199 images | **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text searchable) | FamilySearch, image group 002240017 |
| **State Mine Inspector Reports** | Statewide, annual from 1870 | Physical/microfilm; some digitized | Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg) |
| **Company Employment Records & Payrolls** | Fragmentary, Schuylkill, Luzerne, Lackawanna | Physical | PA State Archives; local historical societies |
| **UMWA Union Records** | 1890+ membership rolls & grievance files | Physical | Various archives; check Anthracite Heritage Museum |

> **FamilySearch returned 23 digitized volumes total** for Pennsylvania in this period (3 shown above); additional volumes covering other coal counties are in the set — use `volume_search` for Cambria, Fayette, Carbon, and Northumberland individually.

> **Pennsylvania Occupational Records wiki article** (FamilySearch Wiki) notes that the annual Mine Inspector reports list fatal accidents by name, age, nationality, and cause of death — an invaluable source when a miner died on the job.

---

### 2. ⛪ Church Records *(essential substitute for civil vital records before 1906)*

Because Pennsylvania had no statewide registration before 1906, **ethnic parish registers are often the only surviving vital record** for mining families.

Mining communities were ethnically segregated by neighborhood, parish, and even which mine shaft a man worked. Key denominations to target by ethnicity:
- **Irish families** → Roman Catholic parishes
- **Welsh families** → Welsh Congregational / Welsh Methodist / Welsh Presbyterian churches
- **Polish, Lithuanian, Slovak, Hungarian families** → ethnic Roman Catholic parishes (often with records in the immigrant language)
- **English families** → Anglican / Episcopal, Methodist
- **German families** → German Lutheran or German Reformed

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* — 1,245,830 records with 607,220 images (collection ID 1401638). Covers baptisms, marriages, and burials from many denominations across the state.

> ⚠️ Many ethnic Catholic and non-English Protestant registers are in Polish, Lithuanian, Welsh, or German. You will need to read the original language or work with a translator.

---

### 3. 📋 Vital Records

| Record | Date Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Statewide death certificates | **1906 onward only** | FamilySearch collection 2255317 (4.2M records, fully indexed). For deaths before 1906, use church burial registers. |
| County marriages (civil) | 1885–1950 | FamilySearch collection 1921317, 524,189 records, 312,045 images |
| Civil marriages | 1677–1950 | FamilySearch collection 1571957, 312,045 records, 156,023 images |
| Compiled marriages | 1700–1900 | FamilySearch collection 1502909, 198,765 records; **no images** |

> **Critical gap:** Births before 1906 are essentially unregistered at the state level. Look to church baptismal records (above) and, after 1890, to the federal census.

---

### 4. 📊 Federal Census Records

Federal censuses for this period (1870, 1880, 1900 — **1890 is almost entirely destroyed**) specifically list occupation as "miner," "coal miner," or similar. The 1880 census also records employer, which can connect a miner to a specific colliery. These are accessible via Ancestry.com (curated link returned by FamilySearch's external resources) and FamilySearch.

---

### 5. 🛂 Immigration & Naturalization Records *(high priority for this region)*

Pennsylvania's coal mines drew massive waves of immigrants. The wiki article *Pennsylvania Occupational Records* notes: "Many miners immigrated through the ports of Philadelphia and New York and naturalized in county courts near the mines."

- **Passenger lists:** Philadelphia and New York arrival records — available through NARA and Ancestry
- **Pre-1906 naturalizations:** Filed in any court of record (Common Pleas, Quarter Sessions). Each county courthouse holds its own naturalization dockets. **Schuylkill County naturalization records (1840s–1906) are specifically held at the Pennsylvania State Archives.**
- **After September 1906:** Standardized federal naturalization papers through federal district courts; NARA holds these.

---

### 6. ⚖️ Probate & Court Records

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* — 893,214 records with 446,607 images (collection ID 1999196). Covers wills, estate inventories, and administration records.

Court records at the county courthouse may also contain guardian filings (important when a miner died leaving minor children), name changes (common among immigrants anglicizing names), and equity cases.

---

### 7. ✉️ Newspapers

Mining-town newspapers (e.g., *Miners' Journal* of Pottsville for Schuylkill County, *Scranton Times* for Lackawanna) published accident notices, obituaries, union news, and immigration arrivals. **Newspapers.com** is curated by FamilySearch for this region and period.

---

### 8. 🪦 Cemetery Records

Coal-patch cemeteries were often maintained by the colliery company or the ethnic church. **FindAGrave** is the FamilySearch-curated external resource for cemetery searches.

---

### 9. 🎖️ Military Records

Many miners of the 1880s–1890s were Civil War veterans. Pension files and service records are at **NARA** (curated by FamilySearch); the **Fold3** platform (also listed) hosts digitized Civil War records. Pennsylvania adjutant general records are at the Pennsylvania State Archives.

---

## Key Repositories

| Repository | What's Here |
|---|---|
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg, PA) | Statewide collections: Schuylkill Co. naturalizations (1840s–1906), company employment records, Mine Inspector reports, state census fragments |
| **Historical Society of Schuylkill County** (Pottsville, PA) | Local anthracite mining collections, newspapers, local genealogies |
| **Anthracite Heritage Museum** (Scranton, PA) | Mining community collections, UMWA records, ethnic community records |
| **County historical societies** | Each coal county (Luzerne, Carbon, Cambria, etc.) has its own society with deed books, local church registers, and county histories |
| **FamilySearch.org** | 6 indexed Pennsylvania collections; 23+ digitized volumes for the coal region (see above); some browse-only |
| **NARA Philadelphia Region** | Pre-1906 federal court naturalizations; military pension files |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census, city directories, passenger lists |

---

## Research Quirks & Gotchas

1. **Lackawanna County before 1878 = Luzerne County.** All Scranton-area records before 1878 were filed under Luzerne County. Search Luzerne first, then Lackawanna from 1878 onward — treat them as one continuous trail.
2. **No statewide birth or death registration before 1906.** Church baptismal and burial registers are the primary substitute.
3. **Ethnic parish records may be in Polish, Lithuanian, Welsh, or German** — plan for translation needs.
4. **Mine accident reports (Luzerne, 287 images) and colliery payroll ledgers (Lackawanna, 199 images) are browse-only** — no name index, no full-text search. You must browse image by image.
5. **UMWA records begin only in 1890**; for earlier labor records, check company payroll ledgers and state mine inspector reports.
6. **The 1890 federal census is destroyed** — use 1880 and 1900, and fill the gap with church records, city directories, and county tax records.
7. **Pre-1906 naturalizations can appear in any county court of record**, not just federal courts — check Common Pleas and Quarter Sessions dockets at the county courthouse.

---

## Where to Start

For a miner's family in the late 1800s, a practical sequence is:
1. **Federal censuses** (1870, 1880, 1900) to establish the household and birthplaces
2. **Church records** (FamilySearch Church and Town Records collection, or the ethnic parish directly) for births/baptisms, marriages, burials
3. **Mine employment cards / payroll ledgers** (FamilySearch volumes above) to confirm the colliery and employment dates
4. **Mine Inspector reports / accident records** if the miner died in an occupational accident
5. **Naturalization records** (county court dockets or PA State Archives) to establish country of origin and immigration date
6. **Passenger lists** (NARA / Ancestry) to trace the immigration journey

# 📜 Massachusetts Early Puritan Settlements — Locality Research Guide
**Place:** Massachusetts, United States | **Period:** ca. 1620–1700

---

## ⚠️ Jurisdictional Context — Two Colonies, One Modern State

The single most important thing to establish before searching any record: **which colony did your ancestor live in?** The early 1600s in Massachusetts involved two entirely separate governments with separate record systems:

| Colony | Years | Geography | Key character |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plymouth Colony** | 1620–1691 | Modern Plymouth, Bristol, Barnstable counties | Separatist ("Pilgrim") founders |
| **Massachusetts Bay Colony** | 1628–1691 | Boston and most of eastern MA | Puritan Great Migration (1630–1640, ~20,000 settlers) |

In **1691** both merged into the **Province of Massachusetts Bay**. Records created before 1691 must be sought under the correct colonial government. Plymouth Colony records are **published and indexed separately** from Massachusetts Bay records — treat them as a distinct research trail, not a subset of the same collections.

---

## 📋 Record Types, Access & Repositories

### 1. Town Vital Records — 🟢 Indexed + Images

**The single most important source for early Massachusetts families.**

- Massachusetts Bay Colony **mandated town recording of births, marriages, and deaths beginning in 1639** — one of the earliest civil registration systems in North America (per Wikipedia, *Massachusetts Bay Colony*).
- **FamilySearch collection:** [Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2061550) (ID: 2061550) — name-indexed, covering the full target period.
- **Published abstracts:** The **"Vital Records of [Town Name] to 1850"** series (Tanner–Corbin series) abstracts town clerk records for **over 200 Massachusetts towns** — the standard published go-to for colonial vital events (per FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Genealogy: Colonial Vital Records*).
- ⚠️ **Gap:** Pre-1639 events (1620–1638) have no mandated civil recording. For this window, rely exclusively on church records and published Great Migration genealogies.
- **Where held:** Town clerk offices (originals); Massachusetts Archives (Boston); FamilySearch (digitized/indexed); NEHGS.

---

### 2. Church Records (Congregational) — 🟢 Indexed + Images

- The Congregational church was the **established church** of the Massachusetts Bay Colony. It kept registers of **member admissions, baptisms, marriages, and disciplinary proceedings** (excommunications, admonishments) — often the only record of events before 1639 and a vital corroboration afterward.
- **FamilySearch collection:** [Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1921657) (ID: 1921657) — covers the target period from 1630.
- Many early church records have been **published** in the Colonial Society of Massachusetts series and local historical society publications (per FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Church Records: Congregational*).
- **Plymouth Colony:** Separatist (Pilgrim) church records are published separately; consult NEHGS and the Pilgrim Society (Plymouth, MA).
- **Where held:** FamilySearch (digitized); NEHGS; denominational archives; local historical societies; Massachusetts Archives.

---

### 3. Probate Records — 🟢 Indexed + Images

- County probate courts have records beginning in the **1630s** — wills, estate inventories, guardianship files — invaluable for establishing family relationships.
- **FamilySearch collection:** [Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678901) (ID: 1678901) — covers the full target period.
- **Where held:** County probate courts (originals); Massachusetts Archives; FamilySearch (digitized/indexed).

---

### 4. Land Records (Deeds) — 🟡 Physical + Some Digitized

- Deeds were recorded at the **county level from the 1630s** and survive in remarkably complete runs at county Registries of Deeds (per FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Church Records*).
- Early colonial land grants were also recorded in colony-level records at the **Massachusetts Archives**.
- **Plymouth Colony:** Plymouth land records are published separately; consult NEHGS and *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth*.
- **Where held:** County Registries of Deeds; Massachusetts Archives (Boston); NEHGS.

---

### 5. Colony & Court Records — 🟡 Physical + Published Transcriptions

- The **Massachusetts Archives (Boston)** holds original colony records, General Court (legislature) records, governor's correspondence, and court records from the **1620s onward** — rich sources for legal disputes, land grants, town incorporations, and identifying individuals.
- Key published transcription: *Records of the Governor and Company of the Massachusetts Bay in New England* (Shurtleff, ed., 5 vols.) — widely available in research libraries.
- **Where held:** Massachusetts Archives (Boston); NEHGS; published volumes.

---

### 6. Great Migration Study — 🟢 Published Index + Online Database

**Essential for any Great Migration ancestor (1620–1643).**

- The **NEHGS *Great Migration* study series** documents every known settler who arrived in New England 1620–1643 with detailed biographical, genealogical, and source entries — the most thoroughly sourced finding aid for early Puritan families (per FamilySearch Wiki, *Massachusetts Genealogy*).
- Searchable online at **AmericanAncestors.org** (subscription required).
- **Where held:** NEHGS (Boston); AmericanAncestors.org; published volumes widely held in genealogical libraries.

---

### 7. Plymouth Colony Records — 🟢 Published & Indexed (Separate Trail)

- Plymouth Colony (1620–1691) maintained its own court, vital, and land records — published as *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* (Shurtleff & Pulsifer, eds., 12 vols.).
- These are **indexed separately** from Massachusetts Bay collections; do not assume FamilySearch's Massachusetts collections cover Plymouth fully.
- **Where held:** Pilgrim Society / Mayflower Society (Plymouth, MA); NEHGS; published volumes.

---

### 8. Colonial Militia / Military — 🟡 Physical + Published

- Colonial militia records document participation in early conflicts: **Pequot War (1636–1638)** and **King Philip's War (1675–1676)**.
- **Where held:** Massachusetts Archives; published in *Records of the Governor and Company* and local town histories.
- ⚠️ Note: FamilySearch's Fold3 military holdings cover primarily post-Revolutionary records — not relevant for 1600s militia research.

---

### 9. Cemetery Records — 🟡 Partial (Browse + Physical)

- Early Puritan burial grounds contain some of the oldest surviving gravestones in North America (Boston, Salem, Plymouth, Cambridge). Wooden markers from the earliest decades rarely survive.
- Gravestone transcriptions available at **FindAGrave.com** and local historical societies.
- **Where held:** Local historical societies; FindAGrave; town records.

---

### 10. Passenger / Arrival Records — ⚠️ No Direct Lists; Use Substitutes

- No passenger lists in the modern sense exist for most 1620s–1640s arrivals. Arrival is reconstructed from:
  - NEHGS *Great Migration* volumes
  - Mayflower passenger lists (Pilgrim Society)
  - Ship manifests preserved in England (The National Archives, Kew — available via Findmypast)
- ⚠️ FamilySearch's **Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891** (ID: 1784312) post-dates the target period by ~180 years — not relevant for early 1600s research.

---

## 🏛️ Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What's There for the 1600s |
|---|---|
| **Massachusetts Archives** (Boston) | Colony/court records, General Court, governor's correspondence, 1620s+ |
| **NEHGS / AmericanAncestors.org** | Great Migration studies, Tanner–Corbin vital records series, Plymouth records, manuscripts |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Indexed/digitized town vitals, church records, probate records |
| **County Registries of Deeds** | Land/deed records from 1630s |
| **County Probate Courts** | Probate records from 1635 |
| **Pilgrim Society / Mayflower Society** (Plymouth, MA) | Plymouth Colony-specific records |
| **Colonial Society of Massachusetts** | Published Congregational church records |
| **The National Archives (Kew, England)** | Some ship manifests; via Findmypast |

---

## 🔑 Critical Research Tips

1. **Identify the colony first.** Plymouth or Massachusetts Bay? They had separate governments, separate courts, and separate record series. After 1691, both became Province of Massachusetts Bay.

2. **Town vital records begin 1639, not 1620.** For the decade 1620–1638, church records and Great Migration studies are your primary substitutes.

3. **Start with the Great Migration series.** If your ancestor arrived before 1643, the NEHGS volumes give you the most thoroughly sourced biography available — use them before diving into original records.

4. **Plymouth is indexed separately.** Published volumes of *Records of the Colony of New Plymouth* are the essential finding aid; do not assume FamilySearch's Massachusetts collections cover Plymouth Colony comprehensively.

5. **No census for this period.** The first federal census is 1790. Substitutes: town freeman lists, militia/tax lists, church membership rolls, and court records.

6. **Congregational church records can predate civil registration.** For events 1620–1638 and as a corroborating source for all events, church registers are essential — especially baptisms, which often substitute for birth records.

7. **FamilySearch wiki coverage gap noted.** The wiki place-page tool returned only generic content for Massachusetts — consult the [Massachusetts Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Genealogy) and [Massachusetts Church Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Massachusetts_Church_Records) articles directly on FamilySearch Wiki for additional research-strategy guidance.

---

This is one of the richest research environments in American genealogy for the 1600s — between the mandatory civil registration (from 1639), the Congregational church records, the surviving probate and land records from the 1630s, and the NEHGS Great Migration studies, the documentary infrastructure for early Puritan families is exceptional. Would you like me to build a specific research plan for a particular ancestor or town?

# Locality Guide: Oklahoma / Oklahoma Territory & Indian Territory (1880–1910)

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Formed:** Oklahoma Territory created May 2, 1890 by the Organic Act following the Land Run of 1889. Indian Territory coexisted separately until both merged November 16, 1907 to form the **state of Oklahoma**.
- **Capital (territorial era):** Guthrie (Oklahoma Territory); tribal capitals for Indian Territory
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Federal territory — administered by Congress through the Department of the Interior
- **Economy:** Subsistence farming, ranching, early oil discovery; homestead agriculture dominated the Oklahoma Territory period
- **Ethnic/population composition:** A uniquely diverse mix — Anglo-American and African-American Land Run settlers (primarily from Kansas, Texas, Missouri, Arkansas, and the Midwest); the Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek/Muscogee, Seminole) in Indian Territory; freedmen (former tribal slaves granted citizenship); and smaller numbers of other Native nations
- **Key events:** Land Run of 1889 (April 22 — ~50,000 settlers, ~2 million acres); subsequent land runs in 1891, 1892, 1893, and 1895; Dawes Commission 1893–1914; statehood November 16, 1907

---

## ⚠️ Critical Boundary Note — Two Territories, Two Research Trails

Before November 1907, Oklahoma was **two entirely separate jurisdictions** with distinct record systems:

| | **Oklahoma Territory** (western OK) | **Indian Territory** (eastern OK) |
|---|---|---|
| Existed | 1890–1907 | Pre-statehood, governed by tribal nations |
| Records | Territorial courts, GLO land offices, county records | Dawes Commission rolls, tribal courts, missionary records |
| Held at | NARA Fort Worth, Oklahoma State Archives, OHS | NARA Fort Worth, Oklahoma Historical Society, tribal archives |

**Identify which territory your ancestor lived in before searching** — the record sets are entirely different. County-level records from Oklahoma Territory begin as counties were organized after 1890. Indian Territory had no county structure until statehood.

Post-1907 records for both areas transition to the unified state system and county courthouse administration.

---

## Available Record Types

### 🏆 Land Records — The #1 Source for Land Run Research
*(Indexed + images on FamilySearch; also on BLM GLO site)*

The Land Run of 1889 and subsequent openings generated the richest genealogical source class for this period. **Homestead files** at the National Archives (Record Group 49, General Land Office) typically contain:
- Original application (name, age, state of origin, family details)
- Proof of settlement with **neighbors listed as witnesses** (a chain-migration goldmine)
- Final certificate and land patent
- **Rejected applications also survive** and contain equally valuable family details

**Access:** Partially indexed on **FamilySearch** (collection *"Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907,"* 234,567 records, 117,283 images) and on the **BLM General Land Office Records** site at [glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov) (free, searchable by name). Physical originals at **NARA Washington, D.C.** (RG 49).

Land offices that processed 1889 claims: **Guthrie** and **Kingfisher**. Later runs used additional offices.

---

### Census Records

| Census | Coverage | Access | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1890 Oklahoma Territory Special Census** | Oklahoma Territory only | Physical — Oklahoma Historical Society | ⭐ One of the few surviving 1890-era census records in the nation; the nationwide federal 1890 census was largely destroyed by fire in 1921. Invaluable for early Land Run settlers. |
| **1900 Federal Census** | Both Oklahoma Territory & Indian Territory (separate schedules) | Indexed + images — FamilySearch, Ancestry | Indian Territory enumerated with tribal affiliation |
| **1910 Federal Census** | State of Oklahoma | Indexed + images — FamilySearch, Ancestry | First census after statehood |
| **Indian Territory censuses** | Various years, Five Civilized Tribes | Indexed + images — FamilySearch | FamilySearch collection *"Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959,"* 567,890 records, 283,945 images |

**Do not overlook** the 1900 Indian Territory census schedules — they record tribal affiliation and are held separately from the Oklahoma Territory schedules.

---

### Dawes Commission Rolls & Indian Records
*(Indexed + images — FamilySearch)*

For any ancestor connected to the Five Civilized Tribes, the **Dawes Commission rolls (1898–1914)** are essential. The Dawes Commission enrolled tribal citizens, freedmen, and intermarried whites, producing detailed citizenship applications that often contain birth dates, family relationships, and prior residence. The **FamilySearch collection** *"Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959"* (567,890 records, 283,945 images) covers these along with earlier tribal censuses. Originals at NARA Fort Worth.

---

### Vital Records — Civil Registration

**Oklahoma statewide vital registration began October 1908**, but compliance was poor through the 1930s. For the territorial period (1889–1907) and the early state period:

- **No statewide birth or death registration exists** before October 1908
- **Oklahoma State Archives** holds birth and death records from 1908 onward
- **Substitute sources for the territorial period:** church registers, the 1890 Territory census, federal censuses (1900, 1910), cemetery records, and newspaper vital notices

---

### Marriage Records
*(Indexed + images — FamilySearch)*

County marriage records begin as counties were organized after 1890. **FamilySearch collection** *"Oklahoma, County Marriages, 1890–1995"* holds 456,789 records across 913,578 persons, with 228,394 images. Marriage registration predates birth/death registration and is the strongest early vital record series. Marriages in Indian Territory may appear in tribal court records rather than county records.

---

### Church Records
*(Indexed + images — FamilySearch; physical at denominational archives)*

Baptist, Methodist, and Catholic congregations established themselves in the new towns beginning in 1889. **FamilySearch collection** *"Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965"* (123,456 records, 61,728 images) includes mission records, many relating to Indian Territory. For Indian Territory, **missionary records from the Five Civilized Tribes** are particularly valuable.

**Physical repositories:**
- Oklahoma Conference of the **United Methodist Church Archives**
- **Diocese of Oklahoma City** (Catholic registers)
- Denominational archives for Baptist and Presbyterian mission work among the tribes

Church registers are the primary substitute for missing civil vital records in the 1889–1908 period.

---

### Territorial & State Court Records
*(Physical — NARA Fort Worth and Oklahoma State Archives)*

Territorial court records for Oklahoma Territory (1890–1907) are at the **National Archives at Fort Worth (Region 6)** and the **Oklahoma State Archives**. They include:
- District court civil and criminal cases
- **Probate records** (wills, administrations, guardianships, inventories)
- **Naturalization records** — pre-1906 naturalizations were filed in any court of record; Oklahoma Territory district courts handled many. After September 1906, the Bureau of Immigration and Naturalization centralized filings.

Indian Territory had its own court system (U.S. Court for Indian Territory) — records also at NARA Fort Worth.

---

### Military Records
*(Indexed + images — Fold3; physical at NARA)*

Key military record sets for the Land Run era:
- **Civil War pension files** — many Land Run settlers were Civil War veterans who relocated to claim free land; pension files can contain rich biographical data including family details, state of origin, and physical descriptions
- **Indian Wars service records** — for those with military service on the frontier
- **Spanish-American War (1898)** — Oklahoma Territory mustered volunteers; records at Oklahoma State Archives and NARA

Access military service records and pension files at **Fold3** and the **National Archives**.

---

### Newspapers
*(Partially digitized — Newspapers.com, Chronicling America)*

Early territorial towns established newspapers almost immediately after the 1889 Land Run — *The Oklahoma State Capital* (Guthrie), *The Daily Oklahoma State Capital*, and dozens of county papers. Newspapers are critical for:
- Obituaries (often the only death notice before civil registration)
- Marriage announcements
- Land claim disputes and legal notices
- County histories and biographical sketches

**Newspapers.com** (subscription) and the **Library of Congress Chronicling America** project (free) hold digitized runs. The **Oklahoma Historical Society** also holds microfilm of many territorial-era papers.

---

### Tax Records
*(Physical — Oklahoma Historical Society and county courthouses)*

County-level tax rolls from the 1890s are among the earliest records documenting individual settlers in Oklahoma Territory and can substitute for missing census years. They confirm presence, real property owned, and personal property value. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds many early territorial tax records.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Key Collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Early Land Records 1834–1907; Indian Censuses & Rolls 1851–1959; County Marriages 1890–1995; Mission & Church Records 1820–1965 | Free |
| **BLM GLO Records** ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) | Homestead patents and land entries, searchable by name | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census (1900, 1910); Civil War pension index | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Military service records, pension files, Indian Territory records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Territorial and early state newspapers | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov) | Selected Oklahoma territorial newspapers | Free |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (Oklahoma City) | 1890 Oklahoma Territory census; land run claim files; town lot records; early county records; newspaper microfilm; tax rolls | In-person; some materials online |
| **Oklahoma State Archives** (Oklahoma City) | Birth/death records from 1908; territorial legislative records; state military records | In-person / online request |
| **National Archives at Fort Worth (Region 6)** | Oklahoma Territory & Indian Territory district court records; naturalization records; Dawes Commission records | In-person / NARA online order |
| **NARA (Washington, D.C.)** | Record Group 49 (GLO homestead files); federal census originals; military service records | In-person / NARA online order |
| **Oklahoma Conference United Methodist Church Archives** | Methodist church registers, mission records | Contact for access |
| **Diocese of Oklahoma City** | Catholic parish registers | Contact for access |
| **Tribal archives** (Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek, Seminole Nations) | Tribal citizenship records, allotment records, tribal court records | Contact individual nations |

---

## Records NOT Online

- **1890 Oklahoma Territory special census** — held physically at the Oklahoma Historical Society; not fully digitized online
- **Early territorial tax rolls** — largely physical at OHS and county courthouses
- **Indian Territory tribal court records** — originals at NARA Fort Worth; partial digitization only
- **Local church registers** (congregational-level records not yet microfilmed or digitized)
- **Rejected homestead application files** — physical at NARA; not indexed online though sometimes retrievable by legal description

---

## Known Record Losses & Gaps

- **1890 federal census:** Nationally, most was destroyed by fire (1921). The **Oklahoma Territory special 1890 census** is a remarkable exception — it survives and covers the earliest Land Run settlers.
- **Vital registration gap (1889–1908):** No statewide civil registration existed for the first two decades of settlement. Church records, newspapers, and the federal censuses are the only contemporaneous sources.
- **Early county courthouse losses:** Some Oklahoma county courthouses suffered fires or flood damage in the early territorial period; check county-level histories for specific losses before assuming records survive.
- **Indian Territory record fragmentation:** Records are split across NARA Fort Worth, tribal archives, and the OHS — no single repository holds a complete run.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the 1890 Oklahoma Territory special census** — it is unique in American genealogy; most 1890 census records for the entire country are gone. If your ancestor was an early Land Run settler, this may be the only enumeration placing them on their claim.

2. **Homestead files are biographical goldmines.** The proof-of-settlement documents require neighbors to testify — those witnesses are often relatives, fellow migrants from the same community, or chain-migration partners. Always read the full file, not just the patent.

3. **Determine Oklahoma Territory vs. Indian Territory first.** Western Oklahoma (Unassigned Lands, Cherokee Outlet, etc.) → Oklahoma Territory records. Eastern Oklahoma (Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek, Seminole lands) → Indian Territory / Dawes Commission records. The research paths diverge completely.

4. **Trace the origin state.** Many Land Run settlers came from Kansas, Texas, Missouri, or Arkansas. FamilySearch Wiki notes this migration pattern explicitly — searching departure-state records often reveals parents, siblings, earlier marriages, and pre-migration vital events that Oklahoma records will not contain.

5. **Dawes Rolls are not just for Native American ancestors.** "Intermarried whites" and "freedmen" (formerly enslaved people held by tribal members) were also enrolled. If an ancestor lived in Indian Territory, check the Dawes rolls regardless of stated ethnicity.

6. **Use county marriage records as your earliest vital record anchor.** County marriages begin 1890, predating any civil vital registration — they are the single strongest early vital record series for territorial-era families.

7. **Check the BLM GLO site ([glorecords.blm.gov](https://glorecords.blm.gov)) independently of FamilySearch** — the two databases index different portions of the land record corpus.

8. **Naturalization records through 1906:** Filed in whatever territorial district court had jurisdiction — not centralized. Search NARA Fort Worth court records by county and term of court.

9. **Newspapers:** Early territorial papers ran almost immediately after the 1889 run. The *Guthrie Daily Leader* and county-seat papers published land-claim news, arrival notices, and vital announcements before any government registration system existed.

10. **The Oklahoma Historical Society is indispensable.** No online database replicates its holdings of territorial-era county records, town lot records, and the 1890 special census.

---

This guide covers the full record landscape for Oklahoma's Land Run era. The most productive research sequence for Land Run families is typically: **(1) BLM/FamilySearch land records → (2) 1890 Oklahoma Territory census (OHS) → (3) 1900/1910 federal census → (4) county marriage records → (5) church records/newspapers → (6) territorial court records at NARA Fort Worth.** For Indian Territory families, substitute the Dawes Commission rolls for steps 1–2.
