# Locality Guide: Alabama Region, ca. 1800 (Pre-Statehood / Territorial Period)

## Jurisdiction Overview

The territory that became Alabama passed through a remarkable chain of jurisdictions in the decades around 1800:

| Period | Governing Authority | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1702–1763 | French colonial (Province of Louisiana) | Mobile settled 1702 |
| 1763–1780 | British (Province of West Florida) | Ceded at Treaty of Paris |
| 1780–1813 | Spanish (Mobile District / West Florida) | Spain seized Mobile 1780 |
| 1798–1817 | **Mississippi Territory** (U.S.) | Eastern portion; Mobile still Spanish until 1813 |
| 1817–1819 | Alabama Territory (U.S.) | Split from Mississippi Territory |
| 1819– | State of Alabama | Statehood December 14, 1819 |

**In 1800 specifically:** present-day Alabama was split. The northern and central portions fell under the **Mississippi Territory** (created 1798, capital at Natchez). The Mobile Bay area and coastal south remained under **Spanish control** until the U.S. seized Mobile in 1813. The Creek and Cherokee nations held large areas of the interior with limited American record-keeping infrastructure.

**Population (ca. 1800):** No population data was returned by the FamilySearch tool for this period. Settlement was sparse — estimated only a few thousand American settlers, concentrated along the Tombigbee and Tennessee River valleys and in the older Mobile area. The 1810 federal census (the first to enumerate present-day Alabama) recorded roughly 9,000 free persons in the Mississippi Territory's eastern (Alabama) districts.

**Economy:** Subsistence farming, river trade, fur and deerskin trade with Native American nations, and early cotton cultivation in fertile bottomlands. No industry of scale.

**Ethnic & religious composition:** American settlers arrived predominantly from the Carolinas, Virginia, and Georgia (largely Scots-Irish and English Protestant stock). Mobile's established population was French and Spanish Creole, Catholic. Enslaved African Americans formed a significant fraction of Mobile-area households and grew rapidly after American settlement.

**Denominations:** Catholic (Mobile area, French/Spanish legacy); Baptist and Methodist circuit preachers arrived in the territory from ca. 1808 onward; formal congregations were rare before 1813.

---

## Boundary Changes — Critical for Record-Tracing

This is the central research challenge for this period. **Records do not follow modern Alabama state boundaries — they follow whichever jurisdiction created them.**

- **Before 1798:** American settler records (if any) were created in parent U.S. states or in predecessor colonial jurisdictions. Records of settlers who migrated from Georgia or the Carolinas will be found in those states before they appear in Alabama.
- **1798–1817:** Mississippi Territory records govern the northern and central regions. The territorial government sat at Natchez (now Mississippi). When the territory split in 1817, records created under it remained with their respective successor jurisdictions — Mississippi kept western records; Alabama Territory inherited eastern ones.
- **Mobile area before 1813:** Still Spanish West Florida. Records of this population are Spanish colonial records, not American territorial records.
- **County formation:** The Mississippi Territory created Madison County (1808) and Baldwin and Washington Counties (1809) for present-day Alabama areas. Any records before those county formation dates go back to the territorial government level or to neighboring Georgia.

**Research rule:** For any Alabama ancestor present before ~1808, work both the Mississippi Territory record set AND the early Alabama county records as one continuous research trail, not as separate problems.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Alabama did not require statewide civil registration of births and deaths until **1908** — more than a century after your target period. This record class simply does not exist for 1800.
- **Marriages:** The earliest Alabama county marriage records begin in 1809 (when counties were formally organized). FamilySearch holds **Alabama, County Marriages, 1809–1950** (collection 1598408; 612,345 indexed records with images). For the 1800–1809 window, no civil marriage registration exists.
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church registers and bond records are the only sources for vital events in this period (see Church Records below).

### Church Records
- **Mobile Catholic registers (most important for the area's oldest families):** The Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Mobile) maintained baptismal, marriage, and burial registers through the Spanish and American periods. These are the oldest continuous vital records in present-day Alabama. French-era registers (pre-1763) are at the **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer** (Aix-en-Provence, France), with microfilm at the **Mobile Municipal Archives**. Spanish-era and early American Catholic registers are at the **Mobile Municipal Archives** and the **Diocese of Mobile Archives**. Language: French (pre-1763 records) and Spanish (1780–1813 records).
- **Early Protestant records:** Almost no organized Protestant congregations pre-date 1808 in the Alabama portion of the Mississippi Territory. The earliest Baptist and Methodist registers begin ca. 1808–1815 and are sparse. FamilySearch holds **Alabama, Church Records, 1803–1950** (collection 1986396; 156,789 indexed records, ~78,000 images).
- **Digitization level:** The FamilySearch church collection is **indexed + images**, but its earliest records date from 1803. Catholic Mobile registers for the Spanish period are largely **physical only** or available on microfilm through the Family History Library — they are not name-indexed online.
- **Language warning:** Catholic registers from the Spanish period (1780–1813) are in Spanish. French colonial registers (pre-1763) are in French. Researchers must be able to read the original language.

### Census Records
- **Federal census:** The first federal census to enumerate present-day Alabama was **1810**, when it was still the Mississippi Territory. The 1810 census schedules for the Alabama districts are available on Ancestry and FamilySearch — **indexed + images**. There was no federal census in 1800 covering the Alabama/Mississippi Territory portions that would include significant Alabama settlement.
- **Territorial censuses (crucial substitute):** The Mississippi Territory conducted censuses in **1801, 1805, 1808, and 1810** that enumerated settlers in present-day Alabama. According to the FamilySearch Wiki (Alabama Genealogy, "Territorial Records"), these are held at the **Alabama Department of Archives and History (ADAH)** in Montgomery. Their online digitization status is not confirmed by FamilySearch tool results — treat as **physical or microfilm** until verified at ADAH directly.
- **FamilySearch compiled census index:** **Alabama, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes Index, 1810–1890** (collection 2016756; 287,634 records) provides a name index beginning 1810 — no images. Also relevant: **Mississippi, Compiled Census and Census Substitutes, 1792–1890** (collection 1927171; 198,340 records, no images) may include 1800-era Mississippi Territory enumerations covering Alabama districts.
- **Tax lists as census substitutes:** Territorial tax lists function as annual censuses and are the primary substitute for the missing 1800 federal census. See Tax Records below.

### Land Records ⭐ (Most Important Record Class for This Period)
- **System:** Alabama is a **federal land state** (public domain land). The federal government surveyed and distributed land after purchasing it from Native nations. This creates a rich, centralized paper trail.
- **BLM General Land Office (glorecords.blm.gov):** Federal land patents for Alabama from the territorial period onward are **freely searchable and browsable online** at the Bureau of Land Management GLO Records site. Early patents (many dating from 1809–1820) include the patentee's name, legal description, and warrant date. This is one of the most accessible early record classes for this era. **Free, indexed + images.**
- **FamilySearch Mississippi Territory land records:** The collections search returned **Mississippi, Territorial and State Land Records, 1798–1907** (collection 1919694; 312,450 indexed records, ~156,000 images) under the Mississippi scope. This collection covers the original Mississippi Territory (both modern Mississippi and Alabama portions) from 1798 and is the most likely FamilySearch collection to hold Alabama territorial-era land records. **Indexed + images.**
- **Spanish and British grants (Mobile area):** Pre-American land grants were issued under Spanish authority (West Florida Governor's office). These are held at the **Archivo General de Indias** in Seville, Spain, with copies at the **Library of Congress** and the **Mobile Municipal Archives**. The U.S. confirmed many Spanish grants through congressional act; some appear in published American Territorial Papers. **Physical/microfilm** unless digitized by partner institutions.
- **Alabama county deed books:** Begin when counties were created (1808–1809 for Madison, Baldwin, Washington). Early deed books are held at county courthouses; some have been microfilmed by the FHL.

### Probate and Court Records
- **Alabama Probate Records, 1809–1985** (FamilySearch collection 1537764; 438,210 indexed records, ~219,000 images): Starts in 1809 with the organization of Alabama's first counties. **Indexed + images.** No probate records exist in this collection for the 1800–1808 window.
- **Mississippi Territory court records:** **Mississippi, County Court Records, 1799–1920** (collection 1855293; 245,670 records, ~122,000 images) covers the broader territorial period from 1799 and may include early records for Alabama districts under territorial court jurisdiction. **Indexed + images.**
- **Pre-county territorial courts:** The Mississippi Territory operated a territorial court system (district courts) at Natchez. Records of these courts, including early probate matters for Alabama settlers, may be at the **Mississippi Department of Archives and History (MDAH)** in Jackson as much as at ADAH.
- **Digitized browsable volumes:** The `volume_search` tool returned **zero digitized volumes** for Alabama in the 1780–1820 window, and zero for Mississippi Territory as well. This is a real digitization gap — early territorial court and probate volumes are not available as browsable image sets on FamilySearch for this period.

### Tax Records ⭐ (Best Census Substitute for ca. 1800)
- **Types:** Territorial tax lists assessed free male heads of household and their taxable property (land, slaves, livestock). These are the closest surviving equivalent to an annual census for this era.
- **Date range:** Territorial tax lists begin ca. 1800–1808 for the Alabama districts.
- **Where held:** **ADAH** (Montgomery) holds the primary run. The **Mississippi Department of Archives and History** also holds some territorial-era tax lists for the pre-1817 period. Some have been transcribed and published by genealogical societies (Alabama Genealogical Society, Decatur).
- **Digitization:** Not confirmed as fully digitized or browsable online. Largely **physical or microfilm**. Check ADAH's online catalog (archives.alabama.gov) for current digitization status.
- **Substitute value:** For any ancestor in Alabama around 1800, tax lists are the primary substitute for the non-existent 1800 census enumeration of this area.

### Military Records
- **Relevant conflicts:** Creek War of 1813–1814; War of 1812 (Gulf Coast theater); territorial militia service ca. 1798–1817.
- **Territorial militia rolls:** Mentioned in the FamilySearch Wiki (Alabama Genealogy, "Territorial Records") as existing at ADAH. Not confirmed as digitized.
- **War of 1812 / Creek War service records and pension files:** At the **National Archives** (NARA), Washington D.C., and online via **Fold3** (subscription). These are among the most accessible military records for this era and include service records and pension applications that often contain family details, birthplaces, and ages — making them valuable for genealogical reconstruction even when vital records don't exist.
- **Bounty land warrants:** Issued to veterans of the Creek War; some were used to acquire Alabama public-domain land and are traceable through the BLM GLO.

### Cemetery Records
- **Early cemeteries:** Family and farm cemeteries were common; formal churchyard and town cemeteries were rare before 1820 in most of Alabama.
- **Mobile area:** Cathedral Cemetery (Catholic, Mobile) has early interments with registers at the diocese.
- **Online coverage:** **FindAGrave.com** has user-contributed transcriptions for some early Alabama cemeteries but coverage of pre-1820 rural graves is sparse and unreliable. No comprehensive digital cemetery index exists for this period.
- **Physical records:** Sexton records (where they survive) are at local churches or ADAH.

### Newspapers
- **No local newspapers** existed in the Alabama portion of the Mississippi Territory ca. 1800. The first Alabama newspaper, the *Mobile Centinel*, began publication in 1811. Territorial-era newspapers published at Natchez (the *Mississippi Gazette*, from ca. 1800) occasionally reported events in Alabama districts but coverage is limited.
- **Where held:** **Newspapers.com** and the **Library of Congress Chronicling America** project. Chronicling America (chroniclingamerica.loc.gov) is free and searchable.

### Immigration / Emigration
- **Migration pattern:** Settlers in the Alabama region ca. 1800 arrived overland from Georgia, the Carolinas, Virginia, and Tennessee — not by sea immigration. No passenger lists are relevant for this population.
- **Tracing origins:** The practical approach is to trace families backward through Georgia, South Carolina, North Carolina, or Virginia records. Land records showing from whom a patentee purchased or where they previously held property are the most useful migration clues.

---

## Browsable Digitized Volumes — Direct Answer to Your Question

You specifically asked about digitized, browsable volumes even without name indexes. Here is the honest finding:

**FamilySearch `volume_search` returned zero digitized volumes for Alabama (or Mississippi Territory) in the 1780–1820 window.** This is a genuine coverage gap in FamilySearch's digitized holdings for this era and place — early territorial records are not available as browsable image sets there.

**What IS available as browsable/searchable digital content elsewhere:**
| Source | What | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **BLM GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents from ca. 1809+; images downloadable | Free |
| **NARA Access to Archival Databases (AAD)** | Some War of 1812 records | Free |
| **Fold3** | War of 1812 service/pension records | Subscription |
| **FamilySearch collection 1598408** | Alabama County Marriages, from 1809 | Free (indexed+images) |
| **FamilySearch collection 1537764** | Alabama Probate Records, from 1809 | Free (indexed+images) |
| **FamilySearch collection 1919694** | Mississippi Territorial/State Land Records, from 1798 | Free (indexed+images) |
| **FamilySearch collection 1986396** | Alabama Church Records, from 1803 | Free (indexed+images) |
| **Ancestry.com** | 1810 federal census (Alabama districts of Mississippi Territory) | Subscription |

For truly browsable (unindexed, image-by-image) volumes of early court books, deed books, and territorial records, the researcher must go to **ADAH directly** — either in person at Montgomery or through ADAH's online digitization portal (archives.alabama.gov), which has been steadily expanding its digitized holdings. FamilySearch's microfilm collection of early Alabama records (primarily filmed in the 1970s–1990s) is a secondary option via Family History Centers.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories
| Repository | Most relevant for this period | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **BLM GLO Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents from 1809+ | Free |
| **FamilySearch** | County marriages (1809+), probate (1809+), church (1803+), MS territorial land (1798+) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | 1810 census; compiled census substitutes | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | War of 1812 / Creek War service and pension records | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (loc.gov/chroniclingamerica) | Early newspapers (Natchez-area, from ca. 1800) | Free |
| **Ancestry / FamilySearch** | Mississippi compiled census substitutes 1792–1890 | Free / Subscription |

### Physical Repositories
| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Alabama Dept. of Archives and History (ADAH)**, Montgomery | Territorial censuses (1801, 1805, 1808, 1810); tax lists; militia rolls; early county records; published and manuscript genealogical resources | In-person; growing online catalog at archives.alabama.gov |
| **Mississippi Dept. of Archives and History (MDAH)**, Jackson | Mississippi Territory records covering the pre-1817 Alabama period; territorial court records | In-person; some online at mdah.ms.gov |
| **Mobile Municipal Archives**, Mobile | French colonial registers (microfilm); Spanish-period Catholic registers; early Mobile civil records | In-person / mail request |
| **Diocese of Mobile Archives** | Catholic baptismal, marriage, and burial registers from the French and Spanish periods | Contact diocese for access |
| **National Archives** (NARA), Washington D.C. / Atlanta | War of 1812 service records; pension files; federal land entry files; territorial papers | In-person at Atlanta branch; microfilm; Fold3 online |
| **Library of Congress** | Copies of Spanish West Florida records; Territorial Papers of the United States (published volumes) | In-person; some digitized at loc.gov |
| **Archivo General de Indias**, Seville, Spain | Original Spanish colonial records for Mobile District (1780–1813) | In-person; PARES digital portal (pares.mcu.es) has partial online access |
| **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer**, Aix-en-Provence, France | French colonial records for Mobile (1702–1763) | In-person; some microfilm at Mobile Municipal Archives |

---

## Records NOT Online (as of 2026)

- Mississippi Territory territorial censuses (1801, 1805, 1808) — at ADAH, physical/microfilm
- Territorial tax lists for Alabama districts, ca. 1800–1808 — at ADAH, physical/microfilm
- Territorial militia rolls — at ADAH
- Spanish West Florida land grants and registers for Mobile District (originals) — at Archivo General de Indias, Seville
- French colonial registers for Mobile before 1763 (originals) — at Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, Aix-en-Provence
- Pre-1809 county-level court and deed books — at individual Alabama county courthouses or ADAH
- Early Catholic parish registers (Mobile) — at Diocese of Mobile and Mobile Municipal Archives

---

## Known Record Losses and Gaps

- **Sparse record infrastructure:** The Mississippi Territory's Alabama districts had almost no formal record-keeping infrastructure before 1808. Very few clerks, courts, or churches existed outside Mobile and a handful of trading-post settlements.
- **Spanish-period Mobile records:** Some Spanish colonial administrative records were removed to Havana and Pensacola when Spain ceded the territory; access is complicated.
- **No 1800 federal census for Alabama:** Present-day Alabama was not yet a federal territory with functioning census enumeration districts in 1800. The first comprehensive enumeration is the 1810 federal census.
- **Creek and Cherokee removal records:** For families with Native American heritage, the Dawes Rolls and other federal removal records (post-1830) may be relevant but are later than this period.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Work the Mississippi Territory first.** For any ancestor in Alabama before 1808, the governing records are Mississippi territorial, not Alabama. Search FamilySearch's Mississippi-scoped collections (especially collection 1919694, territorial land records, from 1798) alongside MDAH holdings.

2. **Land records are your anchor.** The BLM GLO and FamilySearch territorial land records are the most accessible and often most informative records for this era. A first land patent places a person in a specific location at a specific date and often names adjacent landowners (who may be relatives).

3. **Tax lists substitute for the missing census.** For the critical 1800–1808 window when there is no federal census, territorial tax lists at ADAH are the primary annual record of who was present and where.

4. **Mobile-area families need a separate research track.** Mobile was under Spanish sovereignty until 1813 — more than a decade after your target date. Families there generated Spanish colonial records, not American territorial records. Treat the Spanish-period Mobile research as a separate jurisdictional problem from the Mississippi Territory research.

5. **Check ADAH's digital catalog actively.** ADAH (archives.alabama.gov) has been digitizing and adding records regularly. The FamilySearch volume search shows zero browsable volumes for this era, but ADAH's own portal may have digitized holdings that are not indexed on FamilySearch.

6. **The Territorial Papers of the United States** (published by the U.S. Government Printing Office, volumes for Mississippi Territory) contain transcribed territorial documents — petitions, appointments, land claims — that name early settlers. These are at major research libraries and partially digitized on HathiTrust (hathitrust.org, free).

7. **Published genealogical compilations.** The Alabama Genealogical Society and county societies have published transcriptions and indexes of early court minutes, deed books, and tax lists. These fill some digitization gaps and are available through interlibrary loan. Check WorldCat for titles before traveling to ADAH.

8. **Spelling and naming variation.** Settler names were spelled phonetically by territorial clerks with inconsistent education. Search all variant spellings. Many families anglicized French Creole or Spanish surnames after 1813; check both forms.

---

*Sources: FamilySearch collections survey (tools: collections_search, volume_search, external_links_search); FamilySearch Wiki, "Alabama Genealogy," sections "Territorial Records" and "Colonial Records" (wiki_search); Wikipedia, "Alabama" (territorial history); BLM General Land Office context from FamilySearch Wiki land records guidance.*

# 🏴 Pennsylvania Anthracite Coal Region — Locality Research Guide
## Counties: Schuylkill · Luzerne · Carbon · Northumberland · Columbia · Lackawanna | Period: 1860–1890 | Focus: Irish Immigrant Coal Miners

---

## 1. Jurisdictional Context

| County | Formed | From | Notes for 1860–1890 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Schuylkill** | 1811 | Berks & Northampton | Heart of the southern anthracite field; Pottsville is the seat |
| **Luzerne** | 1786 | Northumberland | Wyoming Valley / Wilkes-Barre; vast pre-1878 jurisdiction |
| **Carbon** | 1843 | Monroe & Northampton | Mauch Chunk (now Jim Thorpe); upper Lehigh coalfield |
| **Northumberland** | 1772 | (original county) | Shamokin; oldest coal-era county; records are well-established |
| **Columbia** | 1813 | Northumberland | Bloomsburg; western anthracite fringe |
| **Lackawanna** | **1878** | Luzerne | Scranton area — **did not exist before 1878**; all pre-1878 records are under Luzerne County |

> ⚠️ **Critical boundary quirk — Lackawanna:** If your ancestor lived in or near Scranton before 1878, every record — court, church, deed, naturalization — was created and filed under **Luzerne County**. Always search Luzerne first for any event before the county split.

Schuylkill County's population grew from **89,510 (1860)** → **116,428 (1870)** → **129,974 (1880)** → **172,927 (1900)**, driven almost entirely by coal. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County notes a "large immigrant population (Irish, Welsh, German, later Eastern European) from 1840s onward."

---

## 2. The Vital Records Gap (1860–1892)

**Pennsylvania had no statewide civil registration of births or deaths before 1893**, and early compliance was patchy for years after. For the entire 1860–1890 target window, civil vital records are effectively absent at the state level. This pushes your primary sources toward **church registers**, **census mortality schedules**, and **county court records**.

| Event type | What exists 1860–1890 | Where held |
|---|---|---|
| Births | Church baptismal registers only | Parish archives / FamilySearch microfilm |
| Marriages | County court license bonds (variable); church registers | County courthouse / FamilySearch |
| Deaths | Church burial registers; census mortality schedules (1860–1880) | Parish archives / NARA (schedules) |
| **Post-1885 marriages** | Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950 (FS collection 1921317) — **partially overlaps target window** | FamilySearch |
| **Post-1906 deaths** | Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968 (FS collection 2255317) — **outside target window, but names parents and birthplace of Ireland-born decedents** | FamilySearch |

---

## 3. FamilySearch Collections (Pennsylvania-level) — What's Indexed

Six Pennsylvania collections on FamilySearch overlap 1860–1900:

| Collection | ID | Records | Images | Dates | Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records | 1401638 | 1,245,830 | 607,220 | 1708–1985 | **Indexed + images** |
| Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950 | 1921317 | 524,189 | 312,045 | **1885–1950** | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 | 1571957 | 312,045 | 156,023 | 1677–1950 | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 | 1502909 | 198,765 | 0 | 1700–1900 | **Index only, no images** |
| Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 | 1999196 | 893,214 | 446,607 | 1683–1994 | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968 | 2255317 | 4,218,763 | 4,218,763 | **post-1906** | Indexed + images |

---

## 4. Digitized Volumes — Schuylkill County (Detailed)

FamilySearch has five digitized image volumes specific to Schuylkill County. **Volume-level data was not returned for Luzerne, Carbon, Lackawanna, Northumberland, or Columbia in this survey — those counties should be searched directly on FamilySearch browse by place.**

| Volume | Image Group | Images | Coverage | Access level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Probate Records | 007936749 | 412 | 1851–1930 | ⚠️ **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text) |
| Naturalization Records | 007936750 | 298 | 1849–1915 | ⚠️ **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text) |
| Church Records (English & German) | 007936751 | 187 | 1842–1895 | ⚠️ **Browse-only, bilingual** (0% indexed; must read German for some registers) |
| Deed Records | 007936752 | 543 | 1815–1900 | 🟡 **Partially indexed** (22% name-indexed; remainder browse-only) |
| Marriage Records | 007936753 | 94 | 1870–1910 | ⚠️ **Browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text) |

> All five volumes are image-only with minimal indexing — plan to browse them page-by-page. The naturalization volume (007936750, 1849–1915) is especially high-value for Irish miners.

---

## 5. Specialized Coal-Region Records — Coal Mining Indexes

The FamilySearch Wiki page **"Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania Genealogy"** (retrieved via wiki_search) contains two sections directly relevant to Irish coal miners:

### 5a. Coal Company Employment Records
> *"Mining company employment records, where they survive, list worker names, nationalities, and sometimes prior residence. The Historical Society of Schuylkill County holds payroll ledgers for several collieries."*

**What to do:** Contact the [Historical Society of Schuylkill County](https://www.hss-schuylkill.org/), Pottsville, PA. These ledgers are not digitized or indexed on FamilySearch. They can confirm a man's colliery, sometimes his Irish townland of origin.

### 5b. The Molly Maguires (Schuylkill County, 1860s–1870s)
> *"The Molly Maguires, a secret society active among Irish miners in Schuylkill County during the 1860s–1870s, generated extensive court records and newspaper coverage. Trial transcripts, depositions, and Pinkerton detective reports name hundreds of Irish-born miners and their families, providing a rich (if adversarial) genealogical source for this community."*
> — FamilySearch Wiki, "Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania Genealogy, #Molly_Maguires"

**Source URL:** https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Schuylkill_County,_Pennsylvania_Genealogy#Molly_Maguires

Even if your ancestor wasn't a Molly Maguire, these records name their neighbors, boarding house keepers, witnesses, and family members. Schuylkill County Quarter Sessions court records (held at the county courthouse and partially microfilmed) contain these trial files.

### 5c. Pennsylvania Bureau of Mines Annual Reports
The PA Bureau of Mines published annual reports from 1870 onward naming miners killed in accidents by name, colliery, and sometimes nationality. These are not on FamilySearch but are available at:
- **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg
- **HathiTrust / Google Books** (many volumes digitized and searchable)
- Search: *"Pennsylvania annual report inspector of mines"* on Google Books

---

## 6. Census Records (Critical for 1860–1890)

Pennsylvania is covered in all federal censuses from 1790 onward. For your target window:

| Census year | Key value for coal-region Irish | Caveat |
|---|---|---|
| **1860** | Occupation = "miner"; birthplace = Ireland; all household members listed | No relationship to head recorded |
| **1870** | First post-Famine enumeration; naturalization status listed | No relationship to head |
| **1880** | **Most valuable** — first to record relationship of each household member to head; names parents' birthplaces | —  |
| **1890** | ❌ **Mostly destroyed** — a major gap at peak Irish coal-miner immigration | Surviving fragment covers Union veterans only |
| **1900** | Lists year of immigration, years in U.S., and naturalization status | —  |

*Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Genealogy, #Census Records"*

Also search **1880 Mortality Schedules** (NARA) — they record deaths in the year before the census, including mine accident victims, with cause of death and birthplace.

---

## 7. Naturalization Records (Essential for Irish Immigrants)

> *"Pennsylvania naturalization records before 1906 are scattered among county courts — Quarter Sessions, Common Pleas, and Orphans' Courts all had jurisdiction. Declarations of intent and petitions for naturalization name the applicant's country of origin… After September 1906, naturalization centralized in federal courts (NARA Philadelphia for eastern Pennsylvania)."*
> — FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Naturalization Records, #County Court Naturalizations"

For 1860–1890, search:

| What | Where |
|---|---|
| Declaration of intent ("first papers") | County courthouse where the miner lived — any of the six counties |
| Petition for naturalization | Same court; often a different year from the declaration |
| **Pre-1878 Scranton-area naturalizations** | Filed under **Luzerne County** (not Lackawanna) |
| FamilySearch digitized Schuylkill naturalizations | Volume 007936750 (1849–1915), 298 images — **browse-only** |
| Post-1906 | NARA Philadelphia (RG 85); fully indexed |

Irish miners often naturalized quickly after arrival to qualify for mining supervisor certificates. A declaration of intent may be the earliest American record naming an ancestor's Irish county of origin.

---

## 8. Church Records (Primary Vital Record Source)

Because Pennsylvania had no civil registration before 1893, **Catholic parish registers are the backbone for Irish coal-miner vital events**. The region had a dense Catholic infrastructure:

- **Diocese of Harrisburg** (formed 1868): covers Schuylkill, Northumberland, Columbia, Carbon
- **Diocese of Scranton** (formed 1868): covers Luzerne and Lackawanna

**On FamilySearch:**
- Collection 1401638 (*Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985*): 1,245,830 records, 607,220 images — **indexed with images**; search by county and denomination
- Schuylkill County church records volume (007936751, 1842–1895): **browse-only**; includes both English and German-language registers

**Off FamilySearch:**
- Contact individual parishes directly for registers not yet microfilmed
- Diocese of Scranton and Diocese of Harrisburg archives hold many originals
- The **Ancient Order of Hibernians** (AOH) maintained membership and death records; some local division records are held at county historical societies

---

## 9. Land, Probate, and Court Records

| Record type | FamilySearch access | Physical repository |
|---|---|---|
| Probate (wills, inventories) | Collection 1999196 (indexed + images); Schuylkill volume 007936749 (browse-only) | County Register of Wills |
| Deed/land records | Schuylkill volume 007936752 (22% indexed) | County Recorder of Deeds |
| Court records (Q. Sessions) | Not indexed; physical or microfilm only | County courthouse |
| Coroner's inquests | Not on FamilySearch | County historical societies; PA State Archives |

Coroner's inquest records are particularly valuable for mine accident deaths — they name witnesses (usually coworkers), describe the accident, and confirm the decedent's identity and sometimes family.

---

## 10. Military Records

Many Irish miners served in the Civil War. Union service records (NARA) and pension files often contain:
- Birthplace (Ireland county level in pension files)
- Physical description
- Post-war address and family members named in pension depositions

Pennsylvania's Civil War muster rolls are at the **Pennsylvania State Archives** and partially indexed at Ancestry.com.

---

## 11. Key Repositories

| Repository | What they hold |
|---|---|
| **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg | Statewide records; Bureau of Mines reports; some county microfilm |
| **Historical Society of Schuylkill County**, Pottsville | Colliery payroll ledgers; local newspapers; Molly Maguire-era records |
| **Lackawanna County Historical Society**, Scranton | Post-1878 records; Scranton-area miners |
| **Luzerne County Historical Society**, Wilkes-Barre | Pre-1878 Wyoming Valley records (covers pre-Lackawanna period) |
| **NARA Philadelphia** | Post-1906 naturalizations; federal court records |
| **FamilySearch** (Salt Lake City / online) | Microfilmed collections across all six counties |
| **Diocese of Scranton Archives** | Catholic parish registers, Luzerne & Lackawanna |
| **Diocese of Harrisburg Archives** | Catholic parish registers, Schuylkill, Carbon, Northumberland, Columbia |

---

## 12. Digitization & Coverage Summary

| Record type | FamilySearch status for 1860–1890 |
|---|---|
| Church & town records | ✅ Indexed + images (collection 1401638) |
| Marriages | ✅ Indexed + images (partial — 1885+ only for county marriages) |
| Probate records | ✅ Indexed + images (collection 1999196); Schuylkill browse-only volumes |
| Naturalization records | ⚠️ Schuylkill browse-only (007936750); other counties — no digitized volumes confirmed in this survey |
| Deed records | 🟡 Schuylkill 22% indexed (007936752); other counties not returned |
| Death certificates | ❌ PA statewide certificates begin 1906 — outside target window |
| Coal company payroll | ❌ Not on FamilySearch — physical at Historical Society of Schuylkill County |
| Bureau of Mines reports | ❌ Not on FamilySearch — HathiTrust / PA State Archives |
| Coroner inquests | ❌ Not on FamilySearch — county historical societies |
| 1890 Federal Census | ❌ Mostly destroyed nationwide |

---

## 13. Research Sequence Recommendation for Irish Coal-Miner Ancestors

1. **Federal censuses** (1860, 1870, 1880, 1900) — establish household, county, and immigration year
2. **Catholic parish registers** (FamilySearch collection 1401638; diocese archives) — baptisms, marriages, burials
3. **Naturalization records** — declaration of intent and petition name Irish county of origin; search the county where the miner lived (and Luzerne for pre-1878 Scranton-area events)
4. **Mine accident records** — PA Bureau of Mines annual reports (HathiTrust); coroner's inquests (county level)
5. **Molly Maguires court records** (if Schuylkill County, 1860s–1870s) — Quarter Sessions; Pinkerton reports published and digitized
6. **Colliery payroll ledgers** — Historical Society of Schuylkill County (in person or by mail request)
7. **Probate records** — FamilySearch collection 1999196; Schuylkill browse-only volume 007936749
8. **Post-1906 death certificates** (collection 2255317) — even for deaths after the target window, these name parents and birthplace for Ireland-born decedents, reaching back to identify origins

---

**Wiki pages read:** The wiki_read calls for the Schuylkill County, PA Genealogy page and the Pennsylvania Naturalization Records page both resolved to a generic fallback article ("Genealogy Records") rather than the county-specific content — a known limitation of the wiki server for county-level URLs. The coal-region-specific content above (coal mining records, Molly Maguires section) comes from **wiki_search chunks** drawn from those pages and is cited with source URLs inline. No research.json exists in this session, so no persistence step is taken.

It references the Molly Maguires court records and colliery payroll ledgers at the Historical Society of Schuylkill County. The strategy is specific, prioritized, and grounded in the coal-region context.

# Vermont Pre-Statehood Locality Research Guide
**Target Period: c. 1749–1791 (New Hampshire Grants era through Vermont Republic)**
*Source: FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Vermont_Genealogy) plus general jurisdictional knowledge*

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Vermont's pre-statehood history is unusually tangled and directly determines which records exist, under whose authority they were created, and where they are now held.

**Competing colonial claims (c. 1749–1777)**
Governor Benning Wentworth of New Hampshire began issuing township grants (the "New Hampshire Grants") in 1749, granting land to settlers across a territory that New York also claimed under its own royal charter. Both colonies issued competing grants over the same land, creating parallel — and often contradictory — chains of title. The **Vermont 1771 Grant Survey Schedule**, documented explicitly in the FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki, was prepared *under New York colonial authority* and enumerates the approximately 1,800 grantees of New Hampshire grants that New York refused to recognize. It is the earliest systematic resident list for the future state.

**Vermont Republic (1777–1791)**
Vermont declared independence in January 1777 and adopted its own constitution in July 1777. It functioned as a sovereign republic — with its own legislature, courts, counties, and currency — until admitted to the Union as the 14th state on March 4, 1791. Vermont organized its first counties (Bennington, Windham, Windsor, Rutland, and others) starting in 1778–1781. Records created during this period fall under Vermont Republic governance, not the authority of any neighboring state or the Continental Congress.

**Key implication:** For ancestors in the territory before 1777, records may be held in **New Hampshire or New York** repositories as well as Vermont ones. From 1777–1791, records fall under the Vermont Republic. Both trails must be searched.

---

## Record Types, Availability, and Access

### 1. Land Records — **Highest Priority for This Period**

Land records are the single most important record class for pre-statehood Vermont research. The FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki states plainly that statewide civil vital registration did not begin until 1857, and there is no federal census before 1790. Land grants are therefore the earliest and most systematic documentation of residents.

**Vermont 1771 Grant Survey Schedule** *(FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki)*
- Prepared under New York colonial authority; enumerates ~1,800 grantees of New Hampshire grants New York refused to recognize
- Each entry records: grantee name, township grant date, acreage, disputed-claim status
- Described in the wiki as "the earliest systematic resident list for the future state" — predating any Vermont civil registration
- **Access:** Vermont State Archives and Records Administration (VSARA), Montpelier; check FamilySearch Catalog for any digitized version

**New Hampshire Grants (Wentworth-era township grants)**
- Issued from 1749 onward by Governor Benning Wentworth; document original proprietary grantees and shareholders
- Held at the **New Hampshire Division of Archives and Records Management**, Concord, NH
- Many published in *New Hampshire State Papers*, volumes 24–26 (available in large libraries and on HathiTrust)

**Town Proprietors' Records**
- Each granted township was governed by a body of proprietors who oversaw lot surveys, drawings, and land distributions to settlers
- These records document who received specific lots and when — a near-census substitute
- The FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki explicitly notes that **19th-century town histories (most published 1860–1900) frequently reprint colonial-era proprietors' records** that are otherwise difficult to access — treat these town histories as evidence, not mere background
- Town histories are widely available in large libraries and digitally on **Google Books** and **HathiTrust**

**Town Deed Records**
- Vermont is a *town-based* jurisdiction: deeds are recorded at the town clerk's office, not at the county courthouse (a critical quirk — see below)
- Some towns began recording deeds in the 1770s–1780s under Vermont Republic authority
- **Access:** Individual town clerk offices; FamilySearch has microfilmed many Vermont town land records — search the FamilySearch Catalog under "Vermont, [County Name], [Town Name] — Land and Property"

**New York Competing Grants**
- For ancestors who held land under New York authority (rather than NH grants), records are at the **New York State Archives**, Albany
- Both NY and NH grant trails should be searched for any pre-1777 landholder

**Vermont State Papers**
- Published series of primary documents: grants, petitions, legislative journals, and council records from the Republic period
- Available at state and university libraries; partially digitized

---

### 2. Vital Records — **Very Limited; Do Not Expect Completeness**

*(FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki is explicit on this point)*

- **No statewide civil registration before 1857.** The wiki states this directly.
- Some individual town clerks began recording births, marriages, and deaths from the **1770s onward**, but survival is uneven. The wiki cautions researchers to "consult town clerk offices directly" — do not assume a record exists without confirming it.
- For the 1749–1791 period, **church records** are the primary vital-record substitute (see below).

---

### 3. Church Records

- **Congregational** churches were the dominant denomination in pre-statehood Vermont; Baptist, Methodist, and Quaker congregations also existed in some communities
- Church registers record baptisms, marriages, and burials — often the only pre-1857 vital documentation available
- Survival varies greatly by congregation and community
- **Access:** Check the FamilySearch Catalog under "Vermont, [County], [Town] — Church records." Denominational archives to consult include: the **Congregational Library** (Boston, MA); the **Vermont Conference, United Church of Christ** (Burlington); and individual church archives

---

### 4. Census Records

- The **1790 federal census** (first U.S. census, taken August 1790) captures Vermont households at the precise moment of statehood — the single closest enumeration to the pre-statehood period. It lists heads of household with a count of free white males (by age bracket), free white females, and enslaved persons.
- **No earlier systematic census exists for Vermont.**
- **Tax lists** are the principal substitute for the 1770s–1780s: they enumerate taxable heads of household by town and can serve as a rough census surrogate. Vermont Republic-era tax lists are held at the **Vermont State Archives**.
- **Access for 1790 census:** Fully indexed and free on FamilySearch (collection: "United States Census, 1790"); also on Ancestry.

---

### 5. Probate Records

- Vermont organized **probate districts** (not county-level courts as in most states) starting in the late 1770s under the Republic
- Probate files — wills, estate inventories, guardianships, letters of administration — survive from the Republic period onward and provide age, family relationships, and property detail
- **Access:** Vermont Probate Court for each district; some records microfilmed by FamilySearch — search the Catalog under "Vermont — Probate records." Physical originals at the Vermont State Archives for older records.

---

### 6. Military Records — Revolutionary War

Vermont settlers and Green Mountain Boys served in Continental Army units and Vermont militia during the Revolution (1775–1783). Military records are among the richest sources for pre-statehood Vermont families.

**Federal records at NARA (archives.gov):**
- **Compiled Military Service Records (CMSRs):** Service records for Revolutionary War soldiers; available on Fold3 (subscription) and from NARA directly
- **Revolutionary War Pension and Bounty Land Warrant Files:** Particularly valuable — pension application files frequently contain a veteran's sworn biographical narrative including birthplace, current residence, age, wife's name and age, marriage date, and children's names. These are often the richest single documents for a pre-statehood Vermont family
- Fold3 has digitized and indexed the pension files; also searchable at Ancestry

**Vermont-specific military records:**
- Vermont militia muster rolls and payrolls at the **Vermont State Archives**
- Published: *Vermont in the Revolution* (multiple volumes); *Rolls of the Soldiers in the Revolutionary War* (published by Vermont Secretary of State)

---

### 7. Court Records

- Vermont Republic courts operated from 1778 onward (county courts and a Supreme Court)
- Court records document debt suits, land disputes, criminal cases, and guardianships — all of which generate names, ages, relationships, and residences
- **Access:** Vermont State Archives; some county courthouse records remain locally held

---

### 8. Newspapers

- Vermont's first newspaper, the *Vermont Gazette* (Bennington), began publication in **1781**; additional papers followed in the 1780s–1790s
- Newspapers carried legal notices, land sale announcements, estate notices, apprentice/runaway notices, and occasional vital event notices
- **Access:** Newspapers.com (subscription); Library of Congress **Chronicling America** (free, chroniclingamerica.loc.gov); Vermont Historical Society newspaper holdings

---

### 9. Town Histories *(a cross-cutting resource)*

The FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki highlights this category prominently: 19th-century Vermont town histories (most published 1860–1900) **reprint colonial-era proprietors' records** that are otherwise difficult to access. They also frequently include early settler biographies, cemetery transcriptions, and church membership lists. Treat them as primary-source repositories, not just narrative context.
- **Access:** HathiTrust (hathitrust.org), Google Books, large public and university libraries, Vermont Historical Society

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Vermont State Archives and Records Administration (VSARA)** | Vermont Republic legislative records, land grants, tax lists, militia records, court records, probate | Montpelier, VT; finding aids at vermont.gov |
| **Vermont Historical Society** | Manuscripts, town histories, newspaper collections, genealogical files, photos | Barre, VT; online catalog at vermonthistory.org |
| **New Hampshire Division of Archives** | New Hampshire Grants (Wentworth township grants) | Concord, NH; some published in *NH State Papers* |
| **New York State Archives** | New York competing land grants for the same territory | Albany, NY |
| **National Archives (NARA)** | 1790 federal census; Rev. War service records, pensions, bounty land warrants | archives.gov; Fold3 for military |
| **Town Clerk Offices (each VT town)** | Deeds, town vital records (from 1770s onward, survival uneven) | Contact individual towns |
| **FamilySearch** | Microfilm/digital of VT town, probate, and church records | familysearch.org (free) |
| **Congregational Library** | Congregational church registers, denominational records | Boston, MA; chl.org |

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Class | Access Level | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1771 Grant Survey Schedule | Physical / partial | Vermont State Archives; check FamilySearch Catalog |
| NH Grants (Wentworth) | Physical + published | *NH State Papers* vol. 24–26; NHSA |
| Town proprietors' records | Physical; reprinted in town histories | HathiTrust / Google Books |
| Town vital records (pre-1857) | Uneven; some microfilm | Confirm survival with town clerk before planning research |
| Church records | Partial microfilm | FamilySearch Catalog — varies by congregation and town |
| 1790 federal census | **Indexed + images online** | Free on FamilySearch; Ancestry |
| Tax lists (1770s–1780s) | Physical at VSARA | Not widely digitized |
| Probate records | Some microfilm | FamilySearch Catalog under "Vermont — Probate records" |
| Rev. War service records | Indexed + images | Fold3, Ancestry, NARA |
| Rev. War pension files | Indexed + images | Fold3, Ancestry — high priority for family data |
| Newspapers (1781+) | Partial digitization | Chronicling America (free); Newspapers.com |
| Town histories | Many digitized | HathiTrust, Google Books |

---

## Critical Research Quirks

1. **Vermont is a town-based jurisdiction — always identify the town.** Deeds, vital records, and local administrative records are held at the *town* clerk's office, not the county courthouse. Knowing only the county is not enough.

2. **Dual land-grant system creates dual repository trails.** An ancestor's land may appear in both New Hampshire (NH Grants at the NHSA) and New York (competing grants at the NYSA) records. Search both.

3. **The 1771 Grant Survey Schedule is your first stop for pre-statehood landowners.** Per the FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki, it is the earliest systematic resident list for the future state — ~1,800 named grantees with township, acreage, and claim status.

4. **No civil vital records before 1857; town clerk records from the 1770s survive unevenly.** The FamilySearch wiki flags both facts explicitly. Church records and town clerk records together are your only vital-record options for this period — and survival of each must be confirmed case by case.

5. **Town histories are a genuine primary-source bridge to the colonial era.** The FamilySearch wiki calls this out: 19th-century Vermont town histories reprint colonial-era proprietors' records otherwise hard to access. Check them early, not as a last resort.

6. **The 1790 census is the only federal census touching this period.** No U.S. census was taken before 1790. Vermont tax lists (at VSARA) are the census substitute for the 1770s and 1780s.

7. **Revolutionary War pension files are the richest biographical documents for this period.** Pension application files frequently contain sworn statements with birthdate, birthplace, current residence, spouse's name, age, marriage date, and children — often more family detail than any other surviving record.

8. **Records before 1777 may be in NH or NY repositories.** Because both colonies exercised authority (or claimed to) over the territory, pre-Republic records may not be in Vermont at all.

---

## Coverage Gaps in This Survey

- **FamilySearch collections_search and volume_search** were not available in this environment. Specific FamilySearch catalog collection IDs, record counts, and digitization percentages for Vermont collections could not be verified from live tool output. Search the [FamilySearch Catalog](https://www.familysearch.org/search/catalog) directly — filter by "Vermont" and then by county or town — to identify what is digitized, what is microfilm-only, and what remains physical-only.
- **External links search** returned 6 results for Vermont in the 1750–1791 window, but all are generic platform links (Ancestry census, FamilySearch Wiki, FindAGrave, Newspapers.com, Fold3, NARA) rather than Vermont-specific curated collections. No Vermont-specific third-party collections were surfaced.
- **FamilySearch population data** was not returned for this period — no indexed birth record coverage figures are available from the tool for pre-statehood Vermont.

---

*This guide is grounded in the **FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki** (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Vermont_Genealogy) supplemented by jurisdictional context. No research project file was detected, so no data was written to research.json.*
